components.name: AD
components.description:
Compute Accumulation/Distribution line to track cumulative money flow using close location within the high-low range.

    Use to measure whether volume is flowing into (accumulating) or out of (distributing) an
    asset based on where the close falls within each bar's range. Unlike OBV which only
    considers close direction, AD weights volume by the close location value, giving more
    nuance. Feed into ADOSC for a momentum oscillator, or into RollingZScoreTransform for
    direct signal use. Requires volume data.

    Output:
        Unbounded cumulative value. Rising = accumulation (buying pressure), falling =
        distribution (selling pressure). Absolute scale depends on volume. No NaN warmup.

    Formula:
        CLV = ((close - low) - (high - close)) / (high - low)
        AD_t = AD_{t-1} + CLV * volume

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> AD() -> RollingZScoreTransform(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use to measure whether volume is flowing into (accumulating) or out of (distributing) an asset based on where the close falls within each bar's range. Unlike OBV which only considers close direction, AD weights volume by the close location value, giving more nuance. Feed into ADOSC for a momentum oscillator, or into RollingZScoreTransform for direct signal use. Requires volume data.

components.name: ADOSC
components.description:
Compute Chaikin Accumulation/Distribution Oscillator to detect momentum shifts in money flow.

    Use to identify changes in buying/selling pressure by measuring the difference between
    fast and slow EMAs of the AD line. Positive ADOSC indicates accelerating accumulation,
    negative indicates accelerating distribution. Unlike the raw AD line which is cumulative,
    ADOSC oscillates around zero, making it easier to interpret directly. Feed into
    CrossSectionalZScore for cross-asset ranking. Requires volume data.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around zero. Positive = accumulation momentum increasing,
        negative = distribution momentum increasing. NaN during warmup (slow_period bars).

    Formula:
        ADOSC = EMA(AD, fast_period) - EMA(AD, slow_period)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> ADOSC(fast_period=3, slow_period=10) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60)
components.usage_hint: Use to identify changes in buying/selling pressure by measuring the difference between fast and slow EMAs of the AD line. Positive ADOSC indicates accelerating accumulation, negative indicates accelerating distribution. Unlike the raw AD line which is cumulative, ADOSC oscillates around zero, making it easier to interpret directly. Feed into CrossSectionalZScore for cross-asset ranking. Requires volume data.

parameters.name: fast_period
parameters.description: Short EMA period for the AD line. Shorter (2-3) captures rapid money flow shifts, longer (5-7) smooths out noise. Must be less than slow_period.

parameters.name: slow_period
parameters.description: Long EMA period for the AD line. Acts as the baseline. Standard range 8-15. Default 10 is the classic Chaikin setting. The spread between fast and slow controls oscillator sensitivity.

components.name: ADX
components.description:
Compute Average Directional Index (ADX), a trend strength indicator that measures how strongly price is trending regardless of direction.

    Use as a regime filter to distinguish trending from ranging markets. ADX measures
    trend strength only (not direction), so it is typically used to gate other directional
    signals: enable trend-following when ADX > 25, enable mean reversion when ADX < 20.
    Requires high/low/close data. Prefer ADX over ADXR when you want unsmoothed trend
    strength; use ADXR for a smoother version with more lag.

    Output:
        Range [0, 100]. Values > 25 = strong trend, < 20 = weak/ranging market,
        > 50 = very strong trend. Direction-agnostic (high ADX in both up and
        down trends). NaN during warmup (~2x `period` bars due to internal smoothing).

    Formula:
        +DI = 100 * EMA(+DM, period) / ATR(period)
        -DI = 100 * EMA(-DM, period) / ATR(period)
        DX = 100 * |+DI - -DI| / (+DI + -DI)
        ADX = EMA(DX, period)

    Example:
        ADX(period=14) -> AboveThresholdFilter(threshold=25)  # gate for trend signals
components.usage_hint: Use as a regime filter to distinguish trending from ranging markets. ADX measures trend strength only (not direction), so it is typically used to gate other directional signals: enable trend-following when ADX > 25, enable mean reversion when ADX < 20. Requires high/low/close data. Prefer ADX over ADXR when you want unsmoothed trend strength; use ADXR for a smoother version with more lag.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback for directional movement and ATR calculations. Shorter (7-10) detects trend changes earlier but is noisier, longer (14-28) gives more reliable trend strength readings. Default 14 is standard.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback for directional movement and ATR calculations. Shorter (7-10) detects trend changes earlier but is noisier, longer (14-28) gives more reliable trend strength readings. Default 14 is standard.

components.name: ADXR
components.description:
Compute Average Directional Movement Index Rating (ADXR), a smoothed version of ADX for trend strength measurement.

    Use as a smoother, laggier alternative to ADX for regime filtering. ADXR averages
    the current ADX with the ADX from `period` bars ago, reducing noise in trend strength
    readings. Requires high/low/close data. Prefer ADXR over ADX when you want fewer
    false transitions between trending and ranging regimes at the cost of slower detection.

    Output:
        Range [0, 100]. Interpretation same as ADX: > 25 = trending, < 20 = ranging.
        Smoother than ADX with more lag. NaN during warmup (~3x `period` bars
        due to ADX double-smoothing plus ADXR averaging).

    Formula:
        ADXR = (ADX_today + ADX_{period_bars_ago}) / 2

    Example:
        ADXR(period=14) -> AboveThresholdFilter(threshold=25)  # smooth regime gate
components.usage_hint: Use as a smoother, laggier alternative to ADX for regime filtering. ADXR averages the current ADX with the ADX from `period` bars ago, reducing noise in trend strength readings. Requires high/low/close data. Prefer ADXR over ADX when you want fewer false transitions between trending and ranging regimes at the cost of slower detection.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback for both the underlying ADX calculation and the averaging window. Shorter (7-10) still produces a smooth output, longer (14-20) gives very stable trend strength readings. Default 14. Effective lookback is ~2x period due to internal averaging.

components.name: ATR
components.description:
Compute Average True Range to measure absolute price volatility from high-low-close range.

    Use as a volatility filter or position sizing input. ATR measures absolute dollar
    volatility, so it is not comparable across assets with different price levels. For
    cross-asset volatility comparison, use NATR (percentage-normalized) instead. Often
    fed into universe filters to exclude low-volatility assets, or used in risk managers
    for stop-loss distance calculation.

    Output:
        Range [0, inf), in price units. Higher = more volatile. NaN during warmup
        (period bars). Scale depends on asset price level.

    Formula:
        TR = max(high - low, |high - prev_close|, |low - prev_close|)
        ATR = EMA(TR, period)  (Wilder's smoothing via TA-Lib)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> ATR(period=14) -> RollingZScoreTransform(window=60)
components.usage_hint: Use as a volatility filter or position sizing input. ATR measures absolute dollar volatility, so it is not comparable across assets with different price levels. For cross-asset volatility comparison, use NATR (percentage-normalized) instead. Often fed into universe filters to exclude low-volatility assets, or used in risk managers for stop-loss distance calculation.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback window in bars for ATR smoothing. Shorter (5-10) reacts faster to volatility spikes, longer (14-30) gives a smoother baseline. Default 14 is Wilder's standard period.

components.name: AboveThresholdFilter
components.description:
Filter for values above a threshold, converting continuous scores to a binary mask.

    Use to gate signals by minimum strength, e.g., only trade momentum when return
    exceeds a meaningful level. Works on raw (unnormalized) values. For range-based
    filtering use RangeSelector; for below-threshold filtering use BelowThresholdFilter.

    Output:
        Boolean DataFrame (True/False). True where value exceeds threshold,
        False otherwise. NaN values produce False.

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> AboveThresholdFilter(threshold=1.0) -> ApplyMask()
components.usage_hint: Use to gate signals by minimum strength, e.g., only trade momentum when return exceeds a meaningful level. Works on raw (unnormalized) values. For range-based filtering use RangeSelector; for below-threshold filtering use BelowThresholdFilter.

parameters.name: threshold
parameters.description: Minimum value to pass the filter. Any real number. For percentage returns: 1.0 = 1%. For z-scores: 1.0 = 1 sigma.

parameters.name: inclusive
parameters.description: If True, use >= (include exact threshold). If False (default), use > (strictly above).

components.name: Abs
components.description:
Compute absolute value to measure signal magnitude regardless of direction.

    Use when only the strength of a move matters, not its direction. Common for
    volatility proxies (absolute returns), regime detection (magnitude of momentum),
    or as input to filters that threshold on signal strength. Often placed after
    Difference or SimpleReturn.

    Output:
        Range [0, inf). NaN values preserved. Zero = no signal.

    Example:
        SimpleReturn(periods=4) -> Abs() -> EWMA(period=20)
components.usage_hint: Use when only the strength of a move matters, not its direction. Common for volatility proxies (absolute returns), regime detection (magnitude of momentum), or as input to filters that threshold on signal strength. Often placed after Difference or SimpleReturn.

components.name: AdverseVolCap
components.description:
Cap weights by worst-case adverse-direction portfolio vol (market-neutral).

    Computes the adverse-case scenario where every position's σ moves AGAINST
    the position direction simultaneously. For market-neutral books this is
    larger than Carver's classic "everything correlates to 1" worst case
    (which is small for market-neutral since longs cancel shorts).

    Formula:
        worst_adverse[t] = Σ |w_i[t] × σ_i[t]| × √bars_per_year
        M[t]             = clip(target_vol × ratio / worst_adverse[t], 0, 1)
        capped           = weights × M

    Adapted from Carver's M_corr cap (qoppac.blogspot.com/2020/05) for the
    market-neutral case where the worst case is "longs and shorts move
    OPPOSITE." Each asset's σ is annualized (already, by convention of the
    return_vol slot).

    Use as a final risk overlay alongside RealizedVolCap when you want
    protection against ADVERSE-DIRECTION scenarios as well as recent
    realized vol exceedance. The two caps are complementary:
      - RealizedVolCap responds to actual recent vol (slow, lagging).
      - AdverseVolCap responds to current σ × position structure
        (fast, forward-looking under adverse assumption).

    Causality:
        worst_adverse[t] uses bar-t weights and bar-t σ. Both observable at
        close[t] under right-labeled bars / decision-at-close[t].

    Tuning:
        ratio is the load-bearing parameter. ratio=2.0 fires when adverse-vol
        exceeds 2× target (e.g., 100% annualized at vt=0.50). ratio=6.5
        (Carver default) is very loose — fires only when adverse exceeds 325%.
        For tight risk control, try ratio in [1.5, 3.0].

    Example:
        ... → RealizedVolCap(target_vol=0.50, ratio=2.0)
            → AdverseVolCap(target_vol=0.50, ratio=6.5)
            → LeverageCap(5)
components.usage_hint: Computes the adverse-case scenario where every position's σ moves AGAINST the position direction simultaneously. For market-neutral books this is larger than Carver's classic "everything correlates to 1" worst case (which is small for market-neutral since longs cancel shorts).

parameters.name: return_vol_slot
parameters.description: Slot name for per-asset annualized return vol.

parameters.name: target_vol
parameters.description: Target portfolio realized vol (annualized).

parameters.name: ratio
parameters.description: Cap ratio. Threshold = target_vol × ratio. Higher = looser.

parameters.name: bars_per_year
parameters.description: Annualization factor. Default 365 (daily perp bars).

parameters.name: log_fires
parameters.description: Log fire count + avg M at INFO when True.

changelog.1: Initial release — market-neutral adverse-direction vol cap

components.name: AlignToFrequency
components.description:
Resample and align a signal to a target time frequency for execution grid matching.

    Use when upstream signals are computed at a lower frequency (e.g., 12h or 1d
    rebalance) and need to be upsampled to a higher execution frequency (e.g., 15min
    or 30min). Creates a regular time grid and fills values using the specified method.
    Typically placed late in the pipeline, after position sizing but before execution.

    Output:
        Same value range as input, reindexed to the target frequency. Gaps filled
        by method (forward-fill by default). Empty input returns empty output.

    Example:
        EqualWeightSizer() -> AlignToFrequency(frequency="30min", method="ffill")
components.usage_hint: Use when upstream signals are computed at a lower frequency (e.g., 12h or 1d rebalance) and need to be upsampled to a higher execution frequency (e.g., 15min or 30min). Creates a regular time grid and fills values using the specified method. Typically placed late in the pipeline, after position sizing but before execution.

parameters.name: frequency
parameters.description: Target pandas frequency string. Common values: "15min", "30min", "1h", "4h", "12h", "1d". Must match execution grid frequency.

parameters.name: method
parameters.description: How to fill gaps when upsampling. "ffill" (default) = carry forward last known value. "bfill" = fill from next value. "nearest" = use closest available value. **params: Extra keyword arguments (absorbed for pipeline deserialization compatibility, not used).

components.name: AnalyticalFDM
components.description:
Apply the Forecast Diversification Multiplier to restore combined forecast magnitude after diversification.

    .. deprecated::
        Use AnalyticalFDMCombiner instead. It combines forecasts and applies
        analytical FDM in one step with a single weights parameter, eliminating
        the duplication between ForecastCombiner and AnalyticalFDM.

    Place after ForecastCombiner to compensate for diversification drag (combined
    forecasts are smaller than individual ones due to cancellation). Receives the
    combined forecast and a correlation matrix from CorrelationEstimator via a
    Parallel step. Weights must match the upstream ForecastCombiner weights exactly.

    Output:
        Range [-20, 20] (hard-clipped). FDM scalar is in [fdm_min, fdm_max].
        With uncorrelated rules, FDM approaches 1/sqrt(1/N). With perfectly
        correlated rules, FDM = 1.0 (no diversification benefit).

    Formula:
        FDM = 1 / sqrt(w' R w)
        scaled_forecast = clip(forecast * FDM, -20, 20)
        where w = weight vector, R = correlation matrix

    Example:
        Use AnalyticalFDMCombiner instead:
        AnalyticalFDMCombiner(weights={"mr": 0.5, "mom": 0.5}, correlation_window="90d")
components.usage_hint: .. deprecated:: Use AnalyticalFDMCombiner instead. It combines forecasts and applies analytical FDM in one step with a single weights parameter, eliminating the duplication between ForecastCombiner and AnalyticalFDM.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights matching upstream ForecastCombiner. Required. Must have the same keys and values as the combiner weights.

parameters.name: forecast_key
parameters.description: Key in the Parallel output dict for the combined forecast. Default "combined".

parameters.name: correlation_key
parameters.description: Key for the NxN correlation matrix from CorrelationEstimator. Default "correlation".

parameters.name: fdm_min
parameters.description: Minimum FDM value. Default 1.0 (no amplification below this).

parameters.name: fdm_max
parameters.description: Maximum FDM value. Default 2.5. Caps the diversification benefit to prevent over-amplification with very low correlations.

components.name: AnalyticalFDMCombiner
components.description:
Combine forecast rules and apply analytical FDM correction in one step.

    Replaces the three-component pattern (ForecastCombiner + CorrelationEstimator +
    AnalyticalFDM) with a single Composer. Weights are specified once and used for
    both combination and FDM calculation — no duplication, no mismatch risk.

    Place directly after a Parallel step whose branches produce individual
    ForecastSeries DataFrames. Branch names must match the weights keys.

    Output:
        ForecastSeries (DataFrame). Range [-20, 20] (hard-clipped). FDM scalar
        is in [fdm_min, fdm_max]. With uncorrelated rules, FDM approaches
        1/sqrt(1/N). With perfectly correlated rules, FDM = 1.0.

    Formula:
        combined = sum(w_i * forecast_i)
        FDM = 1 / sqrt(w' R w)
        result = clip(combined * FDM, -20, 20)

    Example:
        {
            'ewmac_2_8': [ewmac(2, 8)],
            'ewmac_4_16': [ewmac(4, 16)],
            'ewmac_8_32': [ewmac(8, 32)],
        },
        AnalyticalFDMCombiner(
            weights={'ewmac_2_8': 0.33, 'ewmac_4_16': 0.34, 'ewmac_8_32': 0.33},
            correlation_window='90d',
        ),
components.usage_hint: Replaces the three-component pattern (ForecastCombiner + CorrelationEstimator + AnalyticalFDM) with a single Composer. Weights are specified once and used for both combination and FDM calculation — no duplication, no mismatch risk.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights keyed by branch name. Must sum to 1.0. Used for BOTH forecast combination and FDM calculation.

parameters.name: correlation_window
parameters.description: Lookback window for correlation estimation. Time string (e.g. "90d"). Shorter = more adaptive, longer = more stable.

parameters.name: shrinkage
parameters.description: Shrinkage method for correlation estimation. "none" = raw sample, "oas" = Oracle Approximating Shrinkage, "lw" = Ledoit-Wolf, "constant" = shrink toward constant target (requires shrinkage_target).

parameters.name: shrinkage_target
parameters.description: Target correlation for "constant" shrinkage. Required when shrinkage="constant". Typical range [0.3, 0.7].

parameters.name: pooling
parameters.description: How to pool correlation data across assets. "cross_asset" = stack all assets for pooled estimate (recommended). "per_asset" = average per-asset matrices.

parameters.name: fdm_min
parameters.description: Minimum FDM value. Default 1.0 (no amplification below this).

parameters.name: fdm_max
parameters.description: Maximum FDM value. Default 2.5. Caps the diversification benefit to prevent over-amplification.

changelog.1: Initial release — replaces ForecastCombiner + CorrelationEstimator + AnalyticalFDM

components.name: AndCombinator
components.description:
Combine multiple boolean filter masks with AND logic so all conditions must be True.

    Use after a Parallel step producing multiple filter signals (e.g., volume, liquidity,
    time filters) to require all conditions are met before trading. For OR logic where
    any condition is sufficient, use OrCombinator instead. NaN values are treated as False.

    Output:
        Boolean DataFrame (0.0/1.0). 1.0 only where ALL input masks are True.
        Same shape as input masks.

    Example:
        Parallel(vol=VolatilityFilter(...), liq=LiquidityFilter(...)) -> AndCombinator("vol", "liq")
components.usage_hint: Use after a Parallel step producing multiple filter signals (e.g., volume, liquidity, time filters) to require all conditions are met before trading. For OR logic where any condition is sufficient, use OrCombinator instead. NaN values are treated as False.

components.name: ApplyMask
components.description:
Apply a boolean filter mask to a score or entry/exit signal, zeroing out unavailable assets.

    Use after a Parallel step that produces both a trading signal and a filter mask
    (e.g., from TopNAssetSelector or AboveThresholdFilter). Sets signal values to 0.0
    wherever the filter is False, preserving values where the filter is True. Supports
    auto-detection of score and filter signals from the Parallel output dict.

    Output:
        Same range as the input score signal. Values where filter=False become 0.0.
        Values where filter=True are unchanged. NaN in filter treated as False.

    Example:
        Parallel(momentum=ROC(period=10), volume_mask=TopNAssetSelector(n=10)) -> ApplyMask("momentum", "volume_mask")
components.usage_hint: Use after a Parallel step that produces both a trading signal and a filter mask (e.g., from TopNAssetSelector or AboveThresholdFilter). Sets signal values to 0.0 wherever the filter is False, preserving values where the filter is True. Supports auto-detection of score and filter signals from the Parallel output dict.

components.name: ApplyUniverseMask
components.description:
Apply a stored universe mask to the current signal, setting excluded assets to NaN.

    Use before cross-sectional operations (z-score, forecast scaler) to ensure they
    operate only on the active universe. The mask is read from a stored slot (typically
    produced by RollingVolumeUniverseMask). Multiplication by 1.0 preserves values;
    multiplication by NaN produces NaN.

    Output:
        Same shape as input. Active assets unchanged, excluded assets become NaN.

    Example:
        ApplyUniverseMask(mask_slot='universe_mask') -> CrossSectionalZScore()

The mask is read from a stored slot (typically produced by RollingVolumeUniverseMask). Multiplication by 1.0 preserves values; multiplication by NaN produces NaN.

components.name: Aroon
components.description:
Compute Aroon Oscillator, a trend direction indicator measuring recency of highs versus lows over a lookback period.

    Use as a trend direction signal that responds to where the most recent high and low
    occurred within the lookback window. High positive values mean a recent high (uptrend),
    high negative values mean a recent low (downtrend). Requires high/low data only.
    Typically followed by CrossSectionalZScore or ForecastScaler. Prefer Aroon over MACD
    when you want a bounded trend signal that is purely based on high/low recency
    rather than moving average convergence.

    Output:
        Range [-100, +100]. Positive = uptrend (recent high closer than recent low),
        negative = downtrend. Values near zero = no clear trend.
        NaN during warmup (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        Aroon_Up = 100 * (period - bars_since_highest_high) / period
        Aroon_Down = 100 * (period - bars_since_lowest_low) / period
        Aroon_Oscillator = Aroon_Up - Aroon_Down

    Example:
        Aroon(period=14) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use as a trend direction signal that responds to where the most recent high and low occurred within the lookback window. High positive values mean a recent high (uptrend), high negative values mean a recent low (downtrend). Requires high/low data only. Typically followed by CrossSectionalZScore or ForecastScaler. Prefer Aroon over MACD when you want a bounded trend signal that is purely based on high/low recency rather than moving average convergence.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback window for tracking highest-high and lowest-low positions. Shorter (7-10) detects trend changes faster, longer (14-25) provides more stable readings. Default 14. Output jumps discretely as the highest/lowest bar rolls in/out of the window.

components.name: AssetAligner
components.description:
Align signal columns to match a reference OHLCV slot's asset universe without re-ranking.

    Use when the reference slot has already been reduced (e.g., by VolumeUniverseReducer)
    and you need a secondary signal (funding rate, open interest) to match the same
    asset set. Unlike VolumeUniverseReducerAny, this does no volume ranking -- it simply
    intersects columns with the reference slot's keys. Optionally aligns the date index
    as well.

    Output:
        Same DataFrame structure with columns filtered to match the reference slot.
        Row count may also be reduced if align_rows=True.

    Example:
        FundingRateLoader() -> AssetAligner(reference_slot="ohlcv_15min") -> ForecastScaler()

Unlike VolumeUniverseReducerAny, this does no volume ranking -- it simply intersects columns with the reference slot's keys. Optionally aligns the date index as well.

components.name: AssetSelect
components.description:
Keep only the specified assets, dropping all other columns.

    Permanently reduces the DataFrame to the listed symbols. Works on both
    OHLCVDict (pre-indicator) and DataFrame (post-indicator) inputs.

    This is a **reducer** — it changes the dimensions of the data. Use in
    Parallel branches to isolate asset groups, then recombine with
    WeightConcatenator. For time-varying filtering that preserves dimensions,
    use ApplyUniverseMask instead.

    Output:
        Same type as input (dict or DataFrame) with only the specified assets.
        Missing assets are silently excluded (not an error).

    Example:
        # Simple pair: split into long and short legs
        Parallel(
            long_leg=Pipeline(
                Load("ohlcv") -> AssetSelect(["HYPE"])
                -> ROC(20) -> ForecastScaler() -> ...
            ),
            short_leg=Pipeline(
                Load("ohlcv") -> AssetSelect(["BNB"])
                -> ROC(20) -> ForecastScaler() -> ...
            ),
        ) -> WeightConcatenator()

        # Multi-asset basket
        AssetSelect(["AAVE", "UNI", "COMP", "MKR", "SNX"])
components.usage_hint: Permanently reduces the DataFrame to the listed symbols. Works on both OHLCVDict (pre-indicator) and DataFrame (post-indicator) inputs.

parameters.name: symbols
parameters.description: Asset symbols to keep. Must not be empty. All other columns/keys are dropped. Order is preserved. Symbols not present in the data are silently skipped.

components.name: AveragePairwiseCorrelationRegime
components.description:
Compute average pairwise correlation of log returns across the universe as a correlation regime indicator.

    Use to detect crowded or highly correlated market regimes where
    cross-sectional signals lose their edge. Feed into RegimeGate to disable
    mean reversion or relative value strategies during high-correlation periods,
    or into RegimeScale to reduce forecast intensity. Pairs well with
    CrossSectionalDispersionRegime for complementary regime views.

    Output:
        Scalar GlobalSeries in range [-1, 1]. Values near 1 indicate all assets
        moving in lockstep (risk-off, correlated selloff), values near 0 indicate
        low correlation (idiosyncratic returns, favorable for cross-sectional
        strategies). NaN during the first lookback bars.

    Formula:
        R = rolling pairwise correlation matrix of log returns
        signal = mean of upper-triangular elements of R

    Example:
        Store('ohlcv') -> AveragePairwiseCorrelationRegime(ohlcv_slot='ohlcv', lookback=20) -> RegimeGate()
components.usage_hint: Use to detect crowded or highly correlated market regimes where cross-sectional signals lose their edge. Feed into RegimeGate to disable mean reversion or relative value strategies during high-correlation periods, or into RegimeScale to reduce forecast intensity. Pairs well with CrossSectionalDispersionRegime for complementary regime views.

parameters.description: Slot name for OHLCV price data. Must match a Store() upstream.

parameters.name: lookback
parameters.description: Rolling window in bars for pairwise correlation estimation. Shorter (10-15) captures fast correlation regime shifts, longer (30-60) measures structural correlation changes. Default 20. Must have >= 3 instruments for meaningful output.

components.name: BelowThresholdFilter
components.description:
Filter for values below a threshold, converting continuous scores to a binary mask.

    Use to detect weakness or negative conditions, e.g., identify assets with
    negative momentum or below-average funding. Works on raw (unnormalized) values.
    For above-threshold filtering use AboveThresholdFilter; for range-based filtering
    use RangeSelector.

    Output:
        Boolean DataFrame (True/False). True where value is below threshold,
        False otherwise. NaN values produce False.

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> BelowThresholdFilter(threshold=-1.0) -> ApplyMask()

components.name: BenchmarkDemean
components.description:
Subtract the per-bar mean signal of named benchmark symbols from every asset.

    Signal-level approximation of beta-neutralization. For a small set of
    "benchmark" symbols (e.g., BTC/ETH/SOL representing the systematic
    crypto factor), subtract the per-bar equal-weighted mean of their
    signals from every asset's signal. Biases the resulting positions
    away from the common-factor direction the benchmarks represent.

    Use after a signal transform when you want positions that are net-short
    the systematic factor without doing per-asset β regression at signal
    time. Cheaper than full position-level β hedging and produces
    equivalent first-order behavior when benchmarks span the factor.

    Output:
        Same shape as input. Per-bar mean across all columns is shifted
        toward zero (exactly zero only if all benchmarks are present and
        not NaN that bar). NaN values are preserved.

    Formula:
        bench_mean[t] = mean(signal[t, b] for b in benchmarks if not NaN)
        result[t, a]  = signal[t, a] - bench_mean[t]

    Example:
        ROC(period=14) -> CrossSectionalZScore() -> BenchmarkDemean(benchmarks=["BTC", "ETH"]) -> ForecastScaler()

    See also:
        CrossSectionalZScore — subtracts mean across ALL assets, not just
        named benchmarks (and divides by std).
components.usage_hint: Signal-level approximation of beta-neutralization. For a small set of "benchmark" symbols (e.g., BTC/ETH/SOL representing the systematic crypto factor), subtract the per-bar equal-weighted mean of their signals from every asset's signal. Biases the resulting positions away from the common-factor direction the benchmarks represent.

parameters.name: benchmarks
parameters.description: Symbol names whose mean signal is subtracted each bar. Default ("BTC", "ETH", "SOL") represents the systematic crypto factor for perp universes. Bars where none of the named benchmarks are present in the input pass through unchanged.

components.name: BetaEstimator
components.description:
Compute rolling beta of each asset relative to a benchmark for risk analysis and hedging.

    Use to measure how each asset co-moves with a benchmark (BTC or universe average).
    Reads OHLCV data from the slot named by ohlcv_slot. Pipe the output to Store(MARKET_BETA)
    to make betas available for BetaHedgeAllocator or other downstream components.
    Useful for building beta-neutral or beta-hedged portfolios.

    Output:
        Unbounded, typically [0.5, 2.0] for crypto assets. Beta > 1 means the asset
        amplifies benchmark moves, < 1 means it's more defensive. NaN during warmup
        (window // 2 bars).

    Formula:
        beta_i = cov(r_i, r_benchmark, window) / var(r_benchmark, window)
        where r = percentage returns from close prices

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> Store("ohlcv_1d") -> BetaEstimator(ohlcv_slot="ohlcv_1d", benchmark="BTC", window=60)
components.usage_hint: Use to measure how each asset co-moves with a benchmark (BTC or universe average). Reads OHLCV data from the slot named by ohlcv_slot. Pipe the output to Store(MARKET_BETA) to make betas available for BetaHedgeAllocator or other downstream components. Useful for building beta-neutral or beta-hedged portfolios.

parameters.name: benchmark
parameters.description: Reference asset or "universe" for equal-weighted mean. "BTC" (default) computes beta vs Bitcoin. "universe" uses the equal-weighted average of all assets as the benchmark.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Rolling window in bars for covariance/variance estimation. Shorter (20-30) = responsive but noisy, longer (60-120) = stable but slow to adapt. Default 60 is a good balance for daily data.

components.name: BetaHedgeAllocator
components.description:
Adjust portfolio weights to neutralize market beta exposure for beta-neutral hedging.

    Use to reduce directional market risk from a portfolio. Self-contained: reads OHLCV
    from a caller-specified OHLCV slot, computes rolling betas internally, then adjusts incoming
    weights. Place after weight allocation (e.g., EqualWeightAllocator or VolTargetWeightConverter).
    For a specific-asset benchmark (e.g., "BTC"), overlays a hedge position on that asset.
    For "universe", applies proportional beta reduction across all positions.

    Output:
        Adjusted portfolio weights (DataFrame). Same shape as input weights. The
        benchmark asset may have a negative weight if hedging a long-biased portfolio.
        Total exposure is preserved for universe mode via renormalization.

    Formula:
        Specific asset: hedge = -hedge_ratio * sum(weights * betas)
                        weights[benchmark] += hedge
        Universe:       weights = weights * (1 - hedge_ratio * betas)
                        then renormalize to preserve total exposure

    Example:
        EqualWeightAllocator() -> BetaHedgeAllocator(ohlcv_slot="ohlcv_1d", benchmark="BTC", hedge_ratio=0.8)
components.usage_hint: Use to reduce directional market risk from a portfolio. Place after weight allocation (e.g., EqualWeightAllocator or VolTargetWeightConverter). For a specific-asset benchmark (e.g., "BTC"), overlays a hedge position on that asset. For "universe", applies proportional beta reduction across all positions.

components.name: BollingerBands
components.description:
Compute normalized price position within Bollinger Bands for volatility breakout and mean reversion detection.

    Use to measure where price sits relative to its recent volatility envelope. Values near
    0 or 1 indicate price at the lower or upper band (potential mean reversion entries), while
    values outside [0, 1] signal breakouts. Feed into CrossSectionalZScore for cross-asset
    comparison or WingsTransform to trade extreme band positions.

    Output:
        Range approximately [0, 1] but can exceed on breakouts. 0 = price at lower band,
        0.5 = price at SMA midline, 1 = price at upper band. NaN during warmup (period bars).

    Formula:
        middle = SMA(close, period)
        upper = middle + num_std * StdDev(close, period)
        lower = middle - num_std * StdDev(close, period)
        output = (close - lower) / (upper - lower)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> BollingerBands(period=20, num_std=2.0) -> CrossSectionalZScore()
components.usage_hint: Use to measure where price sits relative to its recent volatility envelope. Values near 0 or 1 indicate price at the lower or upper band (potential mean reversion entries), while values outside [0, 1] signal breakouts. Feed into CrossSectionalZScore for cross-asset comparison or WingsTransform to trade extreme band positions.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback window in bars for SMA and standard deviation. Shorter (10-15) captures fast volatility shifts, longer (20-50) gives smoother bands. Default 20 is the standard Bollinger Band period.

parameters.name: num_std
parameters.description: Number of standard deviations for band width. 2.0 = standard (captures ~95% of price action), 1.5 = tighter bands (more signals, more noise), 2.5-3.0 = wider bands (fewer but more extreme signals).

components.name: BoxCox
components.description:
Apply Box-Cox power transform to stabilize variance and reduce skewness in positive-valued signals.

    Use on strictly positive signals (e.g., ATR, volume, realized volatility) to make
    their distribution more Gaussian before normalization. Helps downstream z-score
    transforms perform better on heavy-tailed data. Requires all input values > 0;
    apply an upstream shift if needed. With lambda_param=None, fits lambda per column
    via MLE (non-reproducible across data windows).

    Output:
        Unbounded, transformed scale. When lambda_param != 0: (x^lambda - 1) / lambda.
        When lambda_param = 0: ln(x). NaN preserved from input; inf replaced with NaN.

    Formula:
        y = (x^lambda - 1) / lambda   if lambda != 0
        y = ln(x)                      if lambda == 0

    Example:
        ATR(period=14) -> BoxCox(lambda_param=0.5) -> RollingZScoreTransform(window=60)
components.usage_hint: Use on strictly positive signals (e.g., ATR, volume, realized volatility) to make their distribution more Gaussian before normalization. Helps downstream z-score transforms perform better on heavy-tailed data. Requires all input values > 0; apply an upstream shift if needed. With lambda_param=None, fits lambda per column via MLE (non-reproducible across data windows).

parameters.name: lambda_param
parameters.description: Power parameter. None = auto-fit per column via scipy MLE (non-reproducible). 0.0 = log transform. 0.5 = square root. 1.0 = linear (no-op minus shift). Typical values: 0.0-0.5 for heavy-tailed distributions.

parameters.name: lambda_param
parameters.description: Power parameter. None = auto-fit per column via scipy MLE (non-reproducible). 0.0 = log transform. 0.5 = square root. 1.0 = linear (no-op minus shift). Typical values: 0.0-0.5 for heavy-tailed distributions.

components.name: BreakoutDistance
components.description:
Compute continuous breakout distance from rolling channel midpoint as a trend-following signal.

    Use after VolatilityAdjustedPriceSeries to measure how far price has broken
    beyond its recent range, producing a continuous (not binary) breakout strength
    signal already in vol-normalized units. Feed into ForecastScaler for
    standardized forecasts. Prefer over binary breakout flags when signal
    magnitude matters for position sizing.

    Output:
        Unbounded, already vol-normalized. Positive = price above channel midpoint
        (uptrend/long breakout), negative = below (downtrend/short breakout).
        Zero = price at exact channel midpoint. NaN during warmup (lookback bars).

    Formula:
        net = 2 * adj_price - (rolling_high + rolling_low)
        where rolling_high/low are computed over lookback bars
        (shifted by 1 bar when exclude_current=True to avoid lookahead)

    Example:
        VolatilityAdjustedPriceSeries(vol_span=32) -> BreakoutDistance(lookback=20) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use after VolatilityAdjustedPriceSeries to measure how far price has broken beyond its recent range, producing a continuous (not binary) breakout strength signal already in vol-normalized units. Feed into ForecastScaler for standardized forecasts. Prefer over binary breakout flags when signal magnitude matters for position sizing.

parameters.name: lookback
parameters.description: Rolling window in bars for high/low channel. Shorter (10-20) captures fast breakouts, longer (40-80) captures major trend breaks. Common values: 10 (fast), 20 (medium), 40 (slow), 80 (very slow). Multiple lookbacks can be combined via Parallel + ForecastCombiner.

parameters.name: exclude_current
parameters.description: Exclude current bar from rolling high/low to prevent lookahead bias. Default True for live trading; set False only for research/diagnostics.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum valid observations for rolling calculation. Defaults to lookback. Lower values produce earlier output but with less reliable channel estimates.

components.name: CCI
components.description:
Compute Commodity Channel Index (CCI), an unbounded momentum oscillator measuring deviation from the statistical mean price.

    Use for trend-following or mean reversion signals. CCI measures how far the typical
    price deviates from its moving average in terms of mean deviation units. Values beyond
    +/-100 indicate strong trend; extreme values (+/-200) suggest overbought/oversold.
    Requires high/low/close data. Prefer CCI over RSI when you want an unbounded indicator
    that can distinguish between moderate and extreme momentum.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 0. Typically [-200, +200] but can exceed during
        strong trends. Positive = bullish momentum, negative = bearish.
        NaN during warmup (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        TP = (high + low + close) / 3
        CCI = (TP - SMA(TP, period)) / (0.015 * mean_deviation(TP, period))

    Example:
        CCI(period=20) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> WingsTransform(threshold=1.5)
components.usage_hint: Use for trend-following or mean reversion signals. CCI measures how far the typical price deviates from its moving average in terms of mean deviation units. Values beyond +/-100 indicate strong trend; extreme values (+/-200) suggest overbought/oversold. Requires high/low/close data. Prefer CCI over RSI when you want an unbounded indicator that can distinguish between moderate and extreme momentum.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback window for mean and deviation calculation. Shorter (10-14) captures fast momentum shifts, longer (20-40) identifies sustained trends. Default 14. The 0.015 constant ensures ~70-80% of values fall within [-100, +100] at this period.

components.name: CDFTransform
components.description:
Compute empirical CDF percentile rank over a rolling window for distribution-free normalization.

    Use when you want to know where the current value sits relative to its own recent
    history without assuming normality. Similar to RollingPercentile but semantically
    framed as a CDF transform. Good for regime detection (e.g., "is volatility in the
    top 10% of recent history?"). Place before threshold-based filters or regime gates.

    Output:
        Range [0, 1]. 0 = at rolling minimum, 1 = at rolling maximum. 0.5 = median
        of rolling window. NaN during warmup period.

    Formula:
        output = rolling_rank(x, window, pct=True)

    Example:
        ATR(period=14) -> CDFTransform(window=120) -> ThresholdCross(threshold=0.9)
components.usage_hint: Use when you want to know where the current value sits relative to its own recent history without assuming normality. Similar to RollingPercentile but semantically framed as a CDF transform. Good for regime detection (e.g., "is volatility in the top 10% of recent history?"). Place before threshold-based filters or regime gates.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Lookback period in bars for percentile calculation. Longer (120-252) captures broader historical context, shorter (30-60) adapts faster. Default 252 (roughly one trading year on daily bars).

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations before producing output. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) output.

components.name: CMO
components.description:
Compute Chande Momentum Oscillator (CMO), a bounded momentum indicator using the ratio of up and down price movement.

    Use as a momentum or mean reversion signal similar to RSI but on a symmetric [-100, +100]
    scale centered at zero. Only requires close prices. CMO differs from RSI by being
    symmetric around zero (no 50-midline bias), making it easier to use with threshold-based
    transforms. Prefer CMO over RSI when you want a zero-centered oscillator for cleaner
    signal processing with WingsTransform or similar dead-zone filters.

    Output:
        Range [-100, +100]. Positive = bullish momentum (up moves dominate),
        negative = bearish (down moves dominate). Zero = equal up/down movement.
        NaN during warmup (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        CMO = ((sum_up - sum_down) / (sum_up + sum_down)) * 100
        where sum_up = sum of positive price changes over period
              sum_down = sum of absolute negative price changes over period

    Example:
        CMO(period=14) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use as a momentum or mean reversion signal similar to RSI but on a symmetric [-100, +100] scale centered at zero. Only requires close prices. CMO differs from RSI by being symmetric around zero (no 50-midline bias), making it easier to use with threshold-based transforms. Prefer CMO over RSI when you want a zero-centered oscillator for cleaner signal processing with WingsTransform or similar dead-zone filters.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback window for summing up/down price changes. Shorter (5-10) = more responsive and noisy, longer (14-28) = smoother. Default 14. Comparable to RSI period but output is zero-centered.

components.name: CalendarRebalance
components.description:
Emit periodic rebalance flags on a calendar schedule for time-based portfolio rebalancing.

    Use to trigger portfolio weight recalculation on a fixed schedule (daily, weekly,
    or monthly). Output is a sparse flag DataFrame that downstream components like
    EqualWeightRebalancer consume to know when to reassign weights. Useful for
    passive, index-style, or low-turnover strategies.

        1.0 on rebalance dates, NaN otherwise (sparse convention). All columns
        receive the same flag since rebalancing is portfolio-wide.

    Example:
        CalendarRebalance(period="monthly") -> EqualWeightRebalancer()

components.name: Clip
components.description:
Clip signal values to a bounded range, capping outliers at specified limits.

    Use to constrain unbounded signals before combining or sizing. Commonly placed
    after normalization (CrossSectionalZScore, RollingZScoreTransform) to prevent
    extreme values from dominating portfolio weights. Similar to ForecastCapper
    but operates on raw signals rather than forecasts.

    Output:
        Range [lower, upper]. Values outside bounds are clamped. NaN preserved.

    Example:
        CrossSectionalZScore() -> Clip(lower=-3.0, upper=3.0) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use to constrain unbounded signals before combining or sizing. Commonly placed after normalization (CrossSectionalZScore, RollingZScoreTransform) to prevent extreme values from dominating portfolio weights. Similar to ForecastCapper but operates on raw signals rather than forecasts.

components.name: ConstantForecast
components.description:
Emit a fixed forecast value for every loaded asset and timestamp.

    Accepts either OHLCVDict (raw loader output) or an existing
    ForecastSeries / DataFrame. Both produce a ForecastSeries with every
    non-NaN cell set to ``value`` and NaN cells preserved (no position for
    missing data). The two input forms cover the two natural usage patterns:

    1. Index / basket entry: PriceDataLoader → TargetTimeframeResampler →
       ConstantForecast(value=10) → ForecastWeightNormalizer.
       Emits a constant forecast for every asset the loader returned. The
       canonical entry point for index strategies that do not need a signal
       in front.

    2. Directional pair / basket leg: after AssetSelect or GroupAssetFilter
       inside a Parallel branch, ConstantForecast fixes the leg's direction
       and magnitude. NaN cells from the upstream selector mark assets the
       leg should ignore.

    Do NOT use after signal, ranking, or filtering components
    (QuantileRangeSelector, CrossSectionalZScore, QuantileRankTransform,
    etc.) — those carry direction and magnitude in their output values,
    and ConstantForecast would overwrite them (including 0.0, which
    represents "no position" in BinarySignal/mask outputs).

    Output:
        Same shape as input. All non-NaN cells set to ``value``.
        NaN cells preserved (no position for missing data).

    Examples:
        # Index basket entry — no signal needed
        PriceDataLoader(symbols=["VVV", "RENDER", "IO", "TAO"])
        -> TargetTimeframeResampler()
        -> ConstantForecast(value=10)
        -> ForecastWeightNormalizer(target_leverage=1.0)

        # Dollar-balanced pair — direction set per leg
        Parallel(
            long=[AssetSelect(["BTC"]), ConstantForecast(value=10)],
            short=[AssetSelect(["ZK"]), ConstantForecast(value=-10)],
        ) -> WeightConcatenator() -> ForecastWeightNormalizer(target_leverage=0.5)

        # WRONG — QuantileRangeSelector outputs 1.0/0.0, not NaN.
        # ConstantForecast will overwrite both, destroying the mask:
        # QuantileRangeSelector() -> ConstantForecast(value=10)  # BUG: all assets get 10

parameters.description: The constant forecast value. Positive = long, negative = short. Magnitude affects relative sizing when combined with other branches via ForecastWeightNormalizer. Default 10 (standard forecast magnitude).

components.name: CorrelationEstimator
components.description:
Estimate the correlation matrix between forecast rules.

    Computes inter-rule correlations from a dict of forecast DataFrames.
    Uses delta-forecasts (first differences) for correlation estimation.
    Cross-asset pooling and shrinkage improve stability when sample sizes
    are small relative to rule count.

    Note: For analytical FDM, prefer AnalyticalFDMCombiner which handles
    correlation estimation internally. Use this standalone component for
    diagnostics or when you need the correlation matrix for other purposes.

    Output:
        Static NxN correlation DataFrame (rule names as index and columns).
        Values in [-1, 1] with diagonal = 1. Rolling mode collapses to the
        last valid values for downstream compatibility.

    Example:
        Parallel(a=..., b=...) -> CorrelationEstimator(window="90d")
components.usage_hint: Computes inter-rule correlations from a dict of forecast DataFrames. Uses delta-forecasts (first differences) for correlation estimation. Cross-asset pooling and shrinkage improve stability when sample sizes are small relative to rule count.

components.name: CrossSectionalAggregator
components.description:
Aggregate multi-asset signal columns into a single market-level time series using mean, median, or trimmed mean.

    Use to create market-wide indices from per-asset signals, e.g., average momentum
    across all assets for regime detection, or median funding rate as a market sentiment
    indicator. Output is a single-column DataFrame. Trimmed mean is robust to outliers.
    Often used with CumulativeReturnTransform to build a market benchmark, or as input
    to CrossoverCombinator for regime signals.

    Output:
        Single-column DataFrame (column name = output_column). Range depends on input.
        NaN in individual assets ignored by default.

    Example:
        CumulativeReturnTransform() -> CrossSectionalAggregator(method="trimmed_mean", trim_pct=0.1)
components.usage_hint: Use to create market-wide indices from per-asset signals, e.g., average momentum across all assets for regime detection, or median funding rate as a market sentiment indicator. Output is a single-column DataFrame. Trimmed mean is robust to outliers. Often used with CumulativeReturnTransform to build a market benchmark, or as input to CrossoverCombinator for regime signals.

parameters.description: Aggregation method. "mean" = simple average across assets, "median" = robust to outliers, "trimmed_mean" = trims extreme values before averaging (requires trim_pct).

parameters.name: trim_pct
parameters.description: Fraction to trim from each tail for trimmed_mean. Range (0, 0.5). 0.1 = trim bottom and top 10%. Required when method="trimmed_mean".

components.name: CrossSectionalDispersionRegime
components.description:
Measure cross-sectional return dispersion to detect high-differentiation regimes.

    Use to identify periods where asset returns diverge, indicating opportunities for
    cross-sectional strategies like relative value and momentum. Feed into RegimeGate
    to enable cross-sectional signals only during high-dispersion periods, or into
    RegimeScale to modulate forecast intensity. Complements
    AveragePairwiseCorrelationRegime -- high dispersion with low correlation is the
    ideal regime for cross-sectional alpha.

    Output:
        Scalar GlobalSeries, non-negative (std of returns). Higher values indicate
        wider return spread across assets. Smoothed by rolling mean over lookback.
        NaN only if fewer than 2 instruments are available.

    Formula:
        raw = std(return_i) across instruments per timestamp
        signal = rolling_mean(raw, lookback)

    Example:
        Store('ohlcv') -> CrossSectionalDispersionRegime(ohlcv_slot='ohlcv', lookback=10) -> RegimeScale()
components.usage_hint: Use to identify periods where asset returns diverge, indicating opportunities for cross-sectional strategies like relative value and momentum. Feed into RegimeGate to enable cross-sectional signals only during high-dispersion periods, or into RegimeScale to modulate forecast intensity. Complements AveragePairwiseCorrelationRegime -- high dispersion with low correlation is the ideal regime for cross-sectional alpha.

parameters.name: ohlcv_slot
parameters.description: Slot name for OHLCV price data. Must match a Store() upstream.

parameters.name: lookback
parameters.description: Rolling window in bars for smoothing the cross-sectional return std. Shorter (5-10) captures rapid dispersion shifts, longer (15-30) produces a smoother regime signal. Default 10.

components.name: CrossSectionalZScore
components.description:
Normalize signals by cross-sectional z-score relative to other assets at each timestamp for delta-neutral ranking.

    Use for strategies where asset-relative ranking matters more than absolute levels
    (e.g., long/short, delta-neutral). Placed after any indicator and before ForecastScaler
    and ForecastCapper. Creates approximately zero-sum signals across assets. Prefer
    RollingZScoreTransform when absolute deviation from historical norm matters instead.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 0 with mean ~ 0 across assets at each bar.
        Positive = strong vs peers, negative = weak vs peers. NaN preserved from input.

    Formula:
        rank_i(t) = rank(x_i(t)) across N assets
        u_i(t) = rank_i(t) / (N + 1)
        z_i(t) = inverse_normal_cdf(u_i(t))

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> CrossSectionalZScore() -> ForecastScaler(method="mean") -> ForecastCapper()
components.usage_hint: Use for strategies where asset-relative ranking matters more than absolute levels (e.g., long/short, delta-neutral). Placed after any indicator and before ForecastScaler and ForecastCapper. Creates approximately zero-sum signals across assets. Prefer RollingZScoreTransform when absolute deviation from historical norm matters instead.

components.name: Crossover
components.description:
Compute the difference between two parallel signals for moving average crossover and momentum strategies.

    Use after a Parallel block that produces "fast" and "slow" branches (e.g.,
    two EWMA smoothers with different spans). The canonical EWMAC pattern is
    Parallel(fast=[EWMA(span=2)], slow=[EWMA(span=8)]) -> Crossover(). Feed
    the output into ForecastScaler or CrossSectionalZScore for normalization.

    Output:
        Unbounded (fast - slow). Positive = fast signal above slow (bullish
        momentum), negative = fast below slow (bearish). Magnitude reflects
        divergence strength. NaN where either input is NaN.

    Example:
        Parallel(fast=[EWMA(span=4)], slow=[EWMA(span=16)]) -> Crossover() -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use after a Parallel block that produces "fast" and "slow" branches (e.g., two EWMA smoothers with different spans). The canonical EWMAC pattern is Parallel(fast=[EWMA(span=2)], slow=[EWMA(span=8)]) -> Crossover(). Feed the output into ForecastScaler or CrossSectionalZScore for normalization.

components.name: CrossoverCombinator
components.description:
Generate binary regime signals from a fast/slow crossover for trend detection and regime switching.

    Use after computing two moving averages (fast and slow) to create a regime signal
    that switches between bull and bear states. Input must be a DataFrame with columns
    matching fast_key and slow_key. Commonly used with EWMA at different periods fed
    through a Parallel step, then combined here for regime-based signal gating.

    Output:
        Single-column DataFrame ("regime"). Values alternate between bull_value
        (default 1) and bear_value (default -1). No NaN in output unless inputs
        have NaN at the same timestamp.

    Example:
        Parallel(fast=EWMA(period=10), slow=EWMA(period=50)) -> CrossoverCombinator()

Input must be a DataFrame with columns matching fast_key and slow_key. Commonly used with EWMA at different periods fed through a Parallel step, then combined here for regime-based signal gating.

components.name: CumulativeReturnTransform
components.description:
Convert price series to cumulative returns for equity curve analysis and relative performance.

    Use to transform raw prices into growth-factor curves (normalized, starting at 1.0)
    or total-return percentages. Commonly used in regime detection pipelines to compare
    asset performance over time, or as input to CrossSectionalAggregator for
    market-wide return indices.

    Output:
        When normalize=True: starts at 1.0, unbounded above 0. Value of 1.5 = +50% return.
        When normalize=False: starts at 0.0, unbounded. Value of 0.5 = +50% return.
        First row is always 1.0 (normalized) or 0.0 (raw).

    Formula:
        pct = (P_t - P_{t-1}) / P_{t-1}
        normalized: cumprod(1 + pct)
        raw: cumprod(1 + pct) - 1

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> CumulativeReturnTransform(normalize=True) -> CrossSectionalAggregator()
components.usage_hint: Use to transform raw prices into growth-factor curves (normalized, starting at 1.0) or total-return percentages. Commonly used in regime detection pipelines to compare asset performance over time, or as input to CrossSectionalAggregator for market-wide return indices.

parameters.name: normalize
parameters.description: If True (default), output starts at 1.0 (growth factor). If False, output starts at 0.0 (total percentage return). Normalized form is easier for ratio comparisons.

components.name: CumulativeTransform
components.description:
Compute cumulative sum or product of signal values over the entire history.

    Use for running totals (cumsum of returns for equity curve) or compounding
    (cumprod for growth factor). This is an expanding-window operation, not rolling,
    so output grows monotonically in magnitude. For cumulative returns specifically,
    prefer CumulativeReturnTransform which handles the percentage-change step.

    Output:
        Unbounded, monotonically growing for positive inputs. NaN handling
        controlled by skipna parameter.

    Example:
        SimpleReturn(periods=1) -> CumulativeTransform(method="sum")

This is an expanding-window operation, not rolling, so output grows monotonically in magnitude. For cumulative returns specifically, prefer CumulativeReturnTransform which handles the percentage-change step.

components.name: DEMA
components.description:
Compute Double Exponential Moving Average (DEMA), a reduced-lag trend-following moving average.

    Use as a faster-responding alternative to EWMA for trend following. DEMA reduces the
    inherent lag of a standard EMA by applying a double-smoothing correction. Output is in
    price units, so it must be combined with another moving average for crossover signals
    or normalized before cross-asset comparison. Prefer DEMA over EWMA when lag reduction
    matters; use TEMA for even less lag at the cost of potential overshoot.

    Output:
        In price units, tracks the smoothed close price. Responds faster to price
        changes than a standard EMA of the same period. NaN during warmup
        (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        DEMA = 2 * EMA(close, period) - EMA(EMA(close, period), period)

    Example:
        DEMA(period=20) -> SlotStore("fast_ma")
        EWMA(window=60) -> SlotStore("slow_ma")
components.usage_hint: Use as a faster-responding alternative to EWMA for trend following. DEMA reduces the inherent lag of a standard EMA by applying a double-smoothing correction. Output is in price units, so it must be combined with another moving average for crossover signals or normalized before cross-asset comparison. Prefer DEMA over EWMA when lag reduction matters; use TEMA for even less lag at the cost of potential overshoot.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: EMA span for both smoothing passes. Shorter (10-20) closely tracks price, longer (30-60) captures broader trend. Default 30. Equivalent DEMA has roughly half the lag of a standard EMA at the same period.

components.name: Difference
components.description:
Compute first difference (absolute change) between current and lagged values.

    Use when you need raw level changes rather than percentage returns. Useful for
    signals already in normalized units (e.g., z-scores, funding rates) where
    percentage change is meaningless. Also serves as a simple derivative or
    rate-of-change in absolute terms.

    Output:
        Unbounded, same units as input. First N rows are NaN (warmup).

    Formula:
        diff = x_t - x_{t-n}

    Example:
        FundingRateLoader() -> Difference(period=4) -> CrossSectionalZScore()
components.usage_hint: Use when you need raw level changes rather than percentage returns. Useful for signals already in normalized units (e.g., z-scores, funding rates) where percentage change is meaningless. Also serves as a simple derivative or rate-of-change in absolute terms.

components.name: DispersionWeightMapper
components.description:
Map cross-sectional return dispersion to dynamic time-series vs cross-sectional weight allocations.

    Use to produce a weight DataFrame (w_ts, w_xs) that can be consumed by downstream
    blending logic. High dispersion favors directional (TS) bets; low dispersion favors
    relative-value (XS) ranking. For a single-step combine-and-blend, use
    DispersionWeightedCombinator instead.

    Output:
        DataFrame with columns [w_ts, w_xs]. Both in [min_weight, max_weight],
        summing to 1.0 at each timestamp. NaN during burn-in (~20 bars).

    Formula:
        z = expanding_zscore(dispersion)
        w_ts = sigmoid(disp_coeff * z) * (max_w - min_w) + min_w
        w_xs = 1 - w_ts
        Smooth (EWMA half_life) then clip.

    Example:
        Parallel(dispersion=...) -> DispersionWeightMapper(disp_coeff=1.0) -> ...
components.usage_hint: Use to produce a weight DataFrame (w_ts, w_xs) that can be consumed by downstream blending logic. High dispersion favors directional (TS) bets; low dispersion favors relative-value (XS) ranking. For a single-step combine-and-blend, use DispersionWeightedCombinator instead.

parameters.name: dispersion_key
parameters.description: Key in the Parallel output dict containing the dispersion signal (DataFrame or Series).

parameters.name: disp_coeff
parameters.description: Sigmoid sensitivity. Higher = sharper transitions between TS/XS allocation. Default 1.0. Range [0.5, 3.0].

parameters.name: min_weight
parameters.description: Minimum weight for TS allocation. Default 0.2. Sets the floor for TS exposure even in low-dispersion regimes.

parameters.name: max_weight
parameters.description: Maximum weight for TS allocation. Default 0.8. Sets the ceiling for TS exposure in high-dispersion regimes.

parameters.name: half_life_days
parameters.description: EWMA smoothing half-life in days. Longer = smoother weight transitions, less whipsaw. Default 10.

components.name: DispersionWeightedCombinator
components.description:
Blend time-series and cross-sectional forecasts with dynamic weights driven by return dispersion.

    Use to combine a directional (TS) and a relative-value (XS) signal in a single
    step, automatically allocating more to TS during high-dispersion regimes and more
    to XS during low-dispersion regimes. For weight output only (without blending),
    use DispersionWeightMapper.

    Output:
        Same scale as input forecasts (weighted blend). NaN during dispersion
        burn-in (~20 bars). Values reflect the TS/XS mix at each timestamp.

    Formula:
        z = expanding_zscore(dispersion)
        w_ts = sigmoid(disp_coeff * z) * (max_w - min_w) + min_w
        w_xs = 1 - w_ts
        blended = w_ts * ts_signal + w_xs * xs_signal

    Example:
        Parallel(ts=..., xs=..., dispersion=...) -> DispersionWeightedCombinator(ts_key="ts", xs_key="xs")

components.name: DonchianChannel
components.description:
Compute normalized price position within Donchian Channels for trend breakout detection.

    Use to identify breakout and trend-following setups. The channel is defined by the highest
    high and lowest low over the lookback period, making it purely price-range based (no
    averaging). Values near 1 indicate price at period highs (bullish breakout), near 0 at
    period lows (bearish breakout). Unlike BollingerBands and KeltnerChannel, Donchian uses
    raw extremes rather than volatility-adjusted bands.

    Output:
        Range [0, 1]. 0 = price at period low, 1 = price at period high, 0.5 = midrange.
        NaN during warmup (min_periods bars) or when channel width is zero.

    Formula:
        upper = max(high, period)
        lower = min(low, period)
        output = (close - lower) / (upper - lower)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> DonchianChannel(period=20) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60)
components.usage_hint: Use to identify breakout and trend-following setups. The channel is defined by the highest high and lowest low over the lookback period, making it purely price-range based (no averaging). Values near 1 indicate price at period highs (bullish breakout), near 0 at period lows (bearish breakout). Unlike BollingerBands and KeltnerChannel, Donchian uses raw extremes rather than volatility-adjusted bands.

parameters.description: Lookback window in bars for channel extremes. Shorter (10-15) captures recent breakouts, longer (20-55) identifies more significant trend breakouts. Default 20 is the classic Donchian period. period=55 is the original turtle trading breakout window.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations required before producing output. Defaults to period // 2. Lower values produce earlier but less reliable output.

components.name: DynamicEqualWeightAllocator
components.description:
Allocate equal weight (1/n_active) that dynamically adapts to the number of active positions.

    Use when the number of concurrent positions varies over time and you want all active
    positions to share capital equally. Automatically rebalances all active positions when
    the portfolio composition changes (entry or exit). Placed after SelectionToSignalConverter
    or InertiaManager. Prefer over FixedWeightAllocator when you don't know max positions in advance.

    Output:
        Range [0, 1]. Entries get 1/n_active weight, exits get 0.0, holds get NaN
        (sparse). All active positions are rebalanced on any composition change
        (e.g., 9 to 8 assets: weights shift from 0.111 to 0.125).

    Formula:
        weight = 1 / n_active_positions  (for all active assets on rebalance)

    Example:
        SelectionToSignalConverter(hold_periods=5) -> DynamicEqualWeightAllocator()
components.usage_hint: Use when the number of concurrent positions varies over time and you want all active positions to share capital equally. Automatically rebalances all active positions when the portfolio composition changes (entry or exit). Placed after SelectionToSignalConverter or InertiaManager. Prefer over FixedWeightAllocator when you don't know max positions in advance.

components.name: EWMA
components.description:
Compute exponential weighted moving average (EWMA) of close prices for trend following and smoothing.

    Use as a building block for trend-following strategies, typically paired with another
    EWMA of different span to form a crossover signal (EWMAC). Output is in price units,
    so not directly comparable across assets. For a crossover signal, use two EWMA
    components and subtract via a signal composer. Prefer EWMA over DEMA/TEMA when you
    want standard exponential smoothing without reduced-lag corrections.

    Output:
        In price units, tracks the smoothed close price. NaN for the first
        `min_periods` bars (defaults to `window`). Always positive for positive
        price assets.

    Formula:
        EWMA_t = alpha * close_t + (1 - alpha) * EWMA_{t-1}
        where alpha = 2 / (window + 1)

    Example:
        EWMA(window=8) -> SlotStore("fast_ma")
        EWMA(window=32) -> SlotStore("slow_ma")
components.usage_hint: Use as a building block for trend-following strategies, typically paired with another EWMA of different span to form a crossover signal (EWMAC). Output is in price units, so not directly comparable across assets. For a crossover signal, use two EWMA components and subtract via a signal composer. Prefer EWMA over DEMA/TEMA when you want standard exponential smoothing without reduced-lag corrections.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: EWMA span controlling smoothing weight. Shorter (4-16) = responsive (tracks price closely), longer (32-128) = smooth (captures macro trend). Common pairs for EWMAC crossover: (8, 32), (16, 64), (32, 128).

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations before producing output. Defaults to `window`. Lower values start output earlier but with less stable estimates. Set to 1 for earliest possible output.

components.name: EWMATransform
components.description:
Apply exponential moving average smoothing to reduce noise while preserving signal trends.

    Use as a generic noise filter between any indicator and downstream transforms.
    Works on scores, normalized signals, or forecasts. Place after noisy indicators
    (e.g., ROC, RSI) and before threshold or forecast scaling components. Prefer over
    SMA when recent observations should carry more weight. Introduces minimal lag
    compared to simple rolling mean of equivalent smoothness.

    Output:
        Same scale and range as input, smoothed. No NaN introduced beyond input NaN
        (EWMA handles partial windows gracefully). Preserves polarity and magnitude.

    Formula:
        s_t = alpha * x_t + (1 - alpha) * s_{t-1}
        where alpha = 2 / (window + 1)

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> EWMATransform(window=5) -> CrossSectionalZScore()
components.usage_hint: Use as a generic noise filter between any indicator and downstream transforms. Works on scores, normalized signals, or forecasts. Place after noisy indicators (e.g., ROC, RSI) and before threshold or forecast scaling components. Prefer over SMA when recent observations should carry more weight. Introduces minimal lag compared to simple rolling mean of equivalent smoothness.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: EWMA span controlling decay rate. Shorter (3-5) = light smoothing, responsive to recent changes. Longer (10-30) = heavier smoothing, more lag. The half-life is approximately window * ln(2) / 2 bars. Default 10.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations before producing output. None (default) uses pandas default of 0, producing output from bar 1. Set equal to window for stricter warmup.

parameters.name: adjust
parameters.description: Adjustment for imbalanced initial weights. False (default) uses standard recursive formula. True divides by decaying adjustment factor for more accurate early values at the cost of speed.

components.name: EmpiricalFDM
components.description:
Rescale combined forecast to restore target volatility.

    Standalone step in sequential flow: ForecastSeries -> ForecastSeries.
    Uses target/rolling_avg rescaling (empirical approach).

    Production parameters preserved from the original combined FDM implementation:
    - Cross-asset sum/count rolling approach (pools across all instruments)
    - shift(1) prevents look-ahead bias
    - EWMA smoothing with adjust=False
    - First window_bars values set to NaN

components.name: EqualForecastCombiner
components.description:
Combine forecast branches with equal (1/N) weighting.

    Zero-config combiner for after a Parallel step. Each branch gets equal
    weight automatically — no need to specify weights. Use ForecastCombiner
    for explicit per-branch weights.

    Output:
        Same scale as input forecasts (weighted sum). NaN propagates from any
        input branch (intersection semantics). Typically followed by EmpiricalFDM.

    Example:
        Parallel(mr=..., momentum=...) -> EqualForecastCombiner()
components.usage_hint: Zero-config combiner for after a Parallel step. Each branch gets equal weight automatically — no need to specify weights. Use ForecastCombiner for explicit per-branch weights.

components.name: EqualWeightAllocator
components.description:
Allocate equal weight (1/N) to each instrument for uniform risk distribution.

    Use as the simplest instrument weighting scheme where each asset gets the same
    allocation regardless of volatility or conviction. Placed after VolTargetWeightConverter
    and before IDMPortfolioAggregator or LeverageCap. Prefer RiskParityAllocator
    when assets have meaningfully different volatility profiles.

    Output:
        Weighted blocks (DataFrame, same shape as input). Each instrument's blocks
        are multiplied by 1/N where N is the number of assets. Weights sum to 1.0.

    Formula:
        weighted_blocks[asset] = subsystem_blocks[asset] * (1 / N)

    Example:
        VolTargetWeightConverter() -> EqualWeightAllocator() -> IDMPortfolioAggregator(...)
components.usage_hint: Use as the simplest instrument weighting scheme where each asset gets the same allocation regardless of volatility or conviction. Placed after VolTargetWeightConverter and before IDMPortfolioAggregator or LeverageCap. Prefer RiskParityAllocator when assets have meaningfully different volatility profiles.

components.name: EqualWeightRebalancer
components.description:
Assign equal weight (1/n_active) to all active assets on rebalance dates for passive portfolio construction.

    Use after CalendarRebalance or any component producing rebalance flags (1.0 on
    rebalance dates, NaN elsewhere). Also accepts binary universe masks (1.0 = included,
    0.0 = excluded). On rebalance rows, active assets get 1/n_active weight. Non-rebalance
    rows produce NaN (sparse convention). Ideal for index-tracking or passive strategies.

    Output:
        Range [0, 1]. Each active asset gets 1/n_active on rebalance rows. NaN on
        non-rebalance rows. Weights always sum to 1.0 on rebalance dates.

    Example:
        CalendarRebalance(period="weekly") -> EqualWeightRebalancer()
components.usage_hint: Use after CalendarRebalance or any component producing rebalance flags (1.0 on rebalance dates, NaN elsewhere). Also accepts binary universe masks (1.0 = included, 0.0 = excluded). On rebalance rows, active assets get 1/n_active weight. Non-rebalance rows produce NaN (sparse convention). Ideal for index-tracking or passive strategies.

components.name: EqualWeightSizer
components.description:
Split portfolio evenly across active positions with a target leverage.

    The default position sizer for entry/exit strategies. Each active position
    (non-zero in BinarySignal) gets target_leverage / n_active weight.
    Total gross leverage always equals target_leverage when positions are active.

    Use max_weight to cap individual position concentration. When positions
    drop out and fewer remain, the equal-weight formula can allocate a large
    share to each survivor (e.g., 1 position → 100%). max_weight prevents
    this — excess goes to cash (reduced gross leverage), not redistributed.

    Output:
        WeightSeries. 5 active positions at target_leverage=1.0 → 0.2 each.
        Direction preserved from input (+1 → positive weight, -1 → negative).

    Example:
        PositionStateMachine(...) → EqualWeightSizer()
        PositionStateMachine(...) → EqualWeightSizer(target_leverage=1.0, max_weight=0.3)
components.usage_hint: The default position sizer for entry/exit strategies. Each active position (non-zero in BinarySignal) gets target_leverage / n_active weight. Total gross leverage always equals target_leverage when positions are active.

parameters.name: target_leverage
parameters.description: Total gross leverage to distribute across active positions. 1.0 (default) = fully invested, 0.5 = half, 2.0 = 2x leveraged. Each position gets target_leverage / n_active.

parameters.name: max_weight
parameters.description: Maximum absolute weight per position. None (default) = no cap. 0.3 = no position exceeds 30% regardless of how few positions are active. Excess goes to cash (gross leverage decreases), not redistributed to other positions.

components.name: ExtractIndicatorOutput
components.description:
Extract a named output from a multi-output indicator like MACD or BollingerBands.

    Use after multi-output indicators that return a dict of DataFrames (e.g., MACD

Extract a single price field (close, open, high, low, volume) from an OHLCV dict into a flat DataFrame.

components.name: FallingSignalFilter
components.description:
Filter for assets where the signal is declining over a lookback period to detect downward momentum.

    Use to identify assets where an indicator is falling (e.g., WilliamsR declining from
    overbought, RSI rolling over). Produces a boolean mask. For detecting rising signals,
    use RisingSignalFilter instead.

    Output:

        `periods` bars ago. NaN during warmup (first `periods` bars).

    Example:
        WilliamsR(period=14) -> FallingSignalFilter(periods=1) -> AndCombinator(...)
