components.name: FixedWeightAllocator
components.description:
Assign fixed equal weight (1/max_positions) to each entry signal for static position sizing.

    Use for simple equal-weight strategies where the maximum number of concurrent
    positions is known in advance. Placed after SelectionToSignalConverter or any
    component producing entry/exit signals (+1/-1). Produces sparse weight output
    suitable for downstream execution via VectorBT. Prefer DynamicEqualWeightAllocator
    when position count varies and you need automatic rebalancing.

    Output:
        Range [0, 1/max_positions]. Entries get fixed weight, exits get 0.0,
        holds get NaN (sparse convention meaning "no change"). Weights are
        constant regardless of how many positions are actually active.

    Formula:
        weight = 1 / max_positions  (for entries)

    Example:
        SelectionToSignalConverter(hold_periods=5) -> FixedWeightAllocator(max_positions=10)

components.name: FixedWeightSizer
components.description:
Assign a fixed weight to each active position.

    Each active position gets exactly weight_per_position regardless of how
    many positions are active. Total leverage scales with position count:
    5 positions at 0.1 = 0.5x, 10 positions = 1.0x.

    Use LeverageCap downstream if you need a hard leverage limit.

    Output:

        PositionStateMachine(...) → FixedWeightSizer(weight_per_position=0.1)
        PositionStateMachine(...) → FixedWeightSizer(weight_per_position=0.05) → LeverageCap(max_leverage=1.0)

components.name: ForecastCapper
components.description:
Clip forecast values to a symmetric hard cap to prevent extreme position sizes.

    Place after ForecastScaler to limit outlier forecasts that would otherwise
    dominate portfolio positions. Standard cap is +/-20 (2x the target magnitude
    of 10). For a version with dead-zone filtering, use LinearForecastMapper instead.

    Output:
        Range [-limit, +limit]. Values within the cap pass through unchanged.
        NaN values are preserved.

    Formula:
        capped = clip(forecast, -limit, +limit)

    Example:
        ForecastScaler() -> ForecastCapper(limit=20) -> ForecastCombiner()
components.usage_hint: Place after ForecastScaler to limit outlier forecasts that would otherwise dominate portfolio positions. Standard cap is +/-20 (2x the target magnitude of 10). For a version with dead-zone filtering, use LinearForecastMapper instead.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Maximum absolute forecast value. Standard is 20 (2x the target avg|f|=10). Lower values (e.g., 15) constrain outliers more aggressively, reducing tail risk but dampening high-conviction signals.

parameters.description: Maximum absolute forecast value. Standard is 20 (2x the target avg|f|=10). Lower values (e.g., 15) constrain outliers more aggressively, reducing tail risk but dampening high-conviction signals.

components.name: ForecastClipper
components.description:
Clip forecast values with asymmetric bounds for directional constraints.

    Use to enforce long-only or short-only strategies on continuous forecasts.
    Values outside [lower, upper] are clamped to the bound. Values inside
    pass through unchanged. NaN values are preserved.

    Unlike ForecastCapper (symmetric ±limit), this supports asymmetric bounds.
    Unlike ForecastRemap (linear rescale), this is a hard clamp — no rescaling,
    no shifting. A forecast of +5 stays +5; a forecast of -5 becomes 0
    (with lower=0).

    Output:
        Range [lower, upper]. Values within bounds are unchanged.
        NaN preserved. Same shape as input.

    Examples:
        # Long-only: keep positive forecasts, zero out negative (flat when bearish)
        ForecastClipper(lower=0)
        # -15 → 0, -1 → 0, 0 → 0, +5 → +5, +20 → +20

        # Short-only: keep negative forecasts, zero out positive (flat when bullish)
        ForecastClipper(upper=0)
        # -20 → -20, -5 → -5, 0 → 0, +5 → 0, +15 → 0

        # Asymmetric bounds: tighter on shorts
        ForecastClipper(lower=-10, upper=20)
components.usage_hint: Use to enforce long-only or short-only strategies on continuous forecasts. Values outside [lower, upper] are clamped to the bound. Values inside pass through unchanged. NaN values are preserved.

parameters.description: Minimum forecast value. Forecasts below this are clamped up. Use 0 for long-only (negative forecasts → flat). None = no lower bound.

parameters.name: upper
parameters.description: Maximum forecast value. Forecasts above this are clamped down. Use 0 for short-only (positive forecasts → flat). None = no upper bound.

components.name: ForecastCombiner
components.description:
Combine forecast branches with explicit per-branch weights.

    Use after a Parallel step to combine multiple forecast rules with custom
    weights. Weights are keyed by branch name and must sum to 1.0. For equal
    weighting, use EqualForecastCombiner instead.

    Output:
        Same scale as input forecasts (weighted sum). NaN propagates from any
        input branch (intersection semantics). Typically followed by EmpiricalFDM.
        For analytical FDM, use AnalyticalFDMCombiner instead (combine+FDM in one step).

    Formula:
        combined = sum(w_i * forecast_i)  for each branch i

    Example:
        Parallel(mr=..., momentum=...) -> ForecastCombiner(weights={"mr": 0.6, "momentum": 0.4})
components.usage_hint: Use after a Parallel step to combine multiple forecast rules with custom weights. Weights are keyed by branch name and must sum to 1.0. For equal weighting, use EqualForecastCombiner instead.

parameters.description: Explicit weights keyed by branch name. Must sum to 1.0.

components.name: ForecastMapperConvex
components.description:
Apply power-law mapping to disproportionately amplify or compress extreme forecast values.

    Use when you want non-linear treatment of forecast magnitude. Power > 1 amplifies
    extremes (convex/aggressive), power < 1 compresses extremes (concave/conservative).
    At power=1.0 this is equivalent to ForecastCapper. Prefer over piecewise mappers
    when you want a smooth, continuous non-linearity.

    Output:
        Range [-cap, +cap]. Preserves sign. Smooth non-linear curve.
        NaN values are preserved.

    Formula:
        output = sign(x) * cap * (min(|x|, cap) / cap) ^ power

    Example:
        ForecastScaler() -> ForecastMapperConvex(power=1.5) -> ForecastCombiner()
components.usage_hint: Use when you want non-linear treatment of forecast magnitude. Power > 1 amplifies extremes (convex/aggressive), power < 1 compresses extremes (concave/conservative). At power=1.0 this is equivalent to ForecastCapper. Prefer over piecewise mappers when you want a smooth, continuous non-linearity.

parameters.name: power
parameters.description: Exponent controlling the non-linearity. Must be > 0. 1.0 = linear (same as ForecastCapper). 1.5 = moderate convexity (amplify extremes). 2.0 = strong convexity (quadratic amplification). 0.5 = concave (sqrt, compress extremes).

parameters.name: cap
parameters.description: Maximum absolute output value. Default 20.

parameters.name: power
parameters.description: Exponent controlling the non-linearity. Must be > 0. 1.0 = linear (same as ForecastCapper). 1.5 = moderate convexity (amplify extremes). 2.0 = strong convexity (quadratic amplification). 0.5 = concave (sqrt, compress extremes).

components.name: ForecastMapperGrouped
components.description:
Apply two-slope piecewise-linear mapping to concentrate forecast distribution around a target value.

    Use when you want forecasts clustered near a specific magnitude (+/- peak) rather
    than uniformly spread. The steep first slope rapidly lifts signals out of the dead
    zone to the target, while the gentle second slope compresses extremes. Produces a
    "double hump" distribution. Prefer over LinearForecastMapper when you want explicit
    control over the target forecast level.

    Output:
        Range [-cap_output, +cap_output]. Zero inside dead zone. Peaks at +/- peak
        value near the breakpoint. NaN values are preserved.

    Formula:
        slope1 = peak / (breakpoint - dead_zone)    (steep ramp)
        slope2 = (cap_output - peak) / (cap - breakpoint)  (gentle ramp)
        |x| < dead_zone           -> 0
        dead_zone <= |x| <= breakpoint -> slope1 * (|x| - dead_zone)
        breakpoint < |x| <= cap   -> peak + slope2 * (|x| - breakpoint)
        |x| > cap                 -> cap_output

    Example:
        ForecastScaler() -> ForecastMapperGrouped(dead_zone=2, breakpoint=8, peak=12) -> ForecastCombiner()
components.usage_hint: Use when you want forecasts clustered near a specific magnitude (+/- peak) rather than uniformly spread. The steep first slope rapidly lifts signals out of the dead zone to the target, while the gentle second slope compresses extremes. Produces a "double hump" distribution. Prefer over LinearForecastMapper when you want explicit control over the target forecast level.

components.name: ForecastMapperWinged
components.description:
Apply tent-shaped mapping that amplifies moderate forecasts and dampens extreme ones for adjusted trend following.

    Use for trend strategies where moderate signals are more predictive than
    extreme ones (which signal reversal risk). Output rises to a peak at the
    breakpoint, then declines back toward zero at the cap. Prefer over
    ForecastMapperGrouped when you want to explicitly penalize extreme forecasts.

    Output:
        Range [0, peak] (magnitude). Output rises then falls in a triangle shape.
        Beyond cap, output drops to floor_output (default 0). NaN preserved.

    Formula:
        dead_zone <= |x| <= breakpoint -> slope_up * (|x| - dead_zone)  (amplify)
        breakpoint < |x| <= cap -> peak - slope_down * (|x| - breakpoint)  (dampen)
        |x| > cap -> floor_output
        slope_up = peak / (breakpoint - dead_zone)
        slope_down = (peak - floor_output) / (cap - breakpoint)

    Example:
        ForecastScaler() -> ForecastMapperWinged(breakpoint=10, peak=20) -> ForecastCombiner()

Output rises to a peak at the breakpoint, then declines back toward zero at the cap. Prefer over ForecastMapperGrouped when you want to explicitly penalize extreme forecasts.

components.name: ForecastRemap
components.description:
Linearly rescale forecast values from one bounded range to another.

    Maps input values from [from_min, from_max] to [to_min, to_max] using a
    linear transform. Values outside the source range are clamped to the target
    range boundaries. NaN values are preserved.

    Use to enforce directional constraints in pair/basket strategies:
    a forecast in [-20, +20] remapped to to_min=1, to_max=20 is always positive
    (long-only), while to_min=-20, to_max=-1 is always negative (short-only).
    The signal shape and relative ordering are preserved — only the range shifts.

    Output:
        Range [to_min, to_max]. Input values outside [from_min, from_max] are
        clamped. NaN preserved. Same shape as input.

    Formula:
        y = to_min + (x - from_min) * (to_max - to_min) / (from_max - from_min)
        y = clip(y, to_min, to_max)

    Examples:
        # Always-long: weakest momentum -> small long, strongest -> full long
        ForecastRemap(to_min=1, to_max=20)
        # -20 -> 1, 0 -> 10.5, +20 -> 20

        # Always-short: weakest -> small short, strongest -> full short
        ForecastRemap(to_min=-20, to_max=-1)

        # Constant forecast (passive hedge leg):
        ForecastRemap(to_min=10, to_max=10)
        # All values -> 10

        # Rescale RSI (0-100) to forecast scale:
        ForecastRemap(from_min=0, from_max=100, to_min=-20, to_max=20)
components.usage_hint: Maps input values from [from_min, from_max] to [to_min, to_max] using a linear transform. Values outside the source range are clamped to the target range boundaries. NaN values are preserved.

components.name: ForecastScaler
components.description:
Scale raw signals to a target average absolute forecast magnitude for comparable combination.

    Use after any signal-generating component (indicators, transforms, mean reversion) to
    normalize forecast magnitudes before combining with ForecastCombiner,
    AnalyticalFDMCombiner, or EmpiricalFDM. Standard target is 10 (Pardo convention). Prefer rolling mode
    for realistic backtesting; static mode introduces in-sample bias.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 0. After scaling, median|f| or mean|f| equals
        avg_abs_target (default 10). NaN values pass through unchanged.

    Formula:
        scalar = target / statistic(|signal|)
        where statistic is rolling median (MAD) or mean over the window
        scaled_forecast = signal * scalar

    Example:
        TimeSeriesMeanReversionForecast(k=4) -> ForecastScaler(method="MAD", window="90d") -> ForecastCapper()
components.usage_hint: Use after any signal-generating component (indicators, transforms, mean reversion) to normalize forecast magnitudes before combining with ForecastCombiner, AnalyticalFDMCombiner, or EmpiricalFDM. Standard target is 10 (Pardo convention). Prefer rolling mode for realistic backtesting; static mode introduces in-sample bias.

parameters.description: Pooling strategy for scalar calculation. "global" = single scalar across all assets (most stable), "by_asset" = per-asset scalar (captures asset-specific signal scale differences).

parameters.name: method
parameters.description: Statistic for measuring signal magnitude. "MAD" = median absolute deviation (robust to outliers, recommended), "mean" = simple mean of absolute values (sensitive to fat tails).

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Lookback for scalar calculation. Time string ("90d", "30d") or integer bar count. Shorter = more adaptive but noisier, longer = more stable. Default "90d" is standard.

parameters.name: rolling
parameters.description: Whether to recalculate scalar at each bar. True (default) = realistic backtesting with no future information. False = static scalar from calibration window (in-sample bias).

components.name: ForecastWeightNormalizer
components.description:
Normalize forecasts to portfolio weights with target gross leverage.

    Use as the simplest position sizing step for forecast-based strategies. Divides
    each row of forecasts by the sum of absolute values, then scales by target_leverage.
    Preserves relative conviction: stronger forecasts get proportionally larger weights.
    No slots required — works purely from the pipeline value.

    For production strategies needing volatility-targeted sizing, upgrade to the
    ReturnVolatility → VolTargetWeightConverter chain instead.

    Output:
        WeightSeries (DataFrame, timestamps x assets). Absolute values sum to
        target_leverage per row. Sign preserved from forecasts (positive = long,
        negative = short). NaN for all-zero or all-NaN rows.

    Formula:
        weight_i(t) = forecast_i(t) / sum(|forecast_j(t)|) * target_leverage

    Example:
        ForecastCapper(limit=20.0) -> ForecastWeightNormalizer(target_leverage=1.0)
components.usage_hint: Use as the simplest position sizing step for forecast-based strategies. Divides each row of forecasts by the sum of absolute values, then scales by target_leverage. Preserves relative conviction: stronger forecasts get proportionally larger weights. No slots required — works purely from the pipeline value.

parameters.name: target_leverage
parameters.description: Target gross leverage (sum of absolute weights per row). 1.0 = fully invested (no leverage). 2.0 = 2x gross exposure. Default 1.0.

parameters.name: max_weight
parameters.description: Maximum absolute weight per asset. None = no cap. 0.20 = no single asset exceeds 20% of portfolio. Excess goes to cash (gross leverage reduced), not redistributed.

changelog.1: Initial release — with optional max_weight cap

components.name: FundingDataLoader
components.description:
Load hourly funding rate data for all universe symbols from cache or live database.

    Use to provide funding rate data for funding-based signals (carry, funding premium
    mean reversion) or regime detection. Sign convention (Hyperliquid): positive funding
    rate means longs pay shorts. Also stores raw funding data in the FUNDING_RATES context
    slot for the executor to use in P&L decomposition after pipeline.run().

    Output:
        StreamSeries (DataFrame, timestamps x symbols). Values are hourly funding
        rates as decimals (e.g., 0.0001 = 0.01% per hour). Typically [-0.01, 0.01].
        NaN where data is unavailable.

    Example:
        FundingDataLoader(interval="1h") -> Store(FUNDING_RATES)
components.usage_hint: Use to provide funding rate data for funding-based signals (carry, funding premium mean reversion) or regime detection. Sign convention (Hyperliquid): positive funding rate means longs pay shorts. Also stores raw funding data in the FUNDING_RATES context slot for the executor to use in P&L decomposition after pipeline.run().

components.name: FundingDispersionRegime
components.description:
Measure cross-sectional dispersion of funding rates to detect carry differentiation regimes.

    Use to gate or scale carry-based signals when funding rates diverge across
    instruments. High dispersion indicates that carry signals can better differentiate
    between long and short candidates. Feed into RegimeGate to enable carry strategies
    only during high-dispersion periods, or into RegimeScale to modulate carry forecast
    intensity.

    Output:
        Scalar GlobalSeries, non-negative (std of funding rates). Higher values
        indicate more divergent funding across instruments. NaN during warmup
        (lookback // 2 bars).

    Formula:
        raw = std(funding_rate_i) across instruments per timestamp
        signal = rolling_mean(raw, lookback)

    Example:
        FundingDataLoader() -> FundingDispersionRegime(lookback=20) -> RegimeGate()
components.usage_hint: Use to gate or scale carry-based signals when funding rates diverge across instruments. High dispersion indicates that carry signals can better differentiate between long and short candidates. Feed into RegimeGate to enable carry strategies only during high-dispersion periods, or into RegimeScale to modulate carry forecast intensity.

parameters.description: Rolling window in bars for smoothing the cross-sectional funding std. Shorter (5-10) reacts faster to shifts in funding dispersion, longer (20-40) filters noise. Default 20.

components.name: FundingLevelRegime
components.description:
Detect extreme funding regimes by averaging absolute funding rates across the universe.

    Use to gate or scale carry and mean reversion strategies based on overall funding
    intensity. High funding levels indicate large carry opportunities where
    funding-based signals are most profitable. Feed into RegimeGate to enable/disable
    strategy legs, or into RegimeScale to modulate forecast intensity.

    Output:
        Scalar GlobalSeries, non-negative (absolute funding rates). Higher values
        indicate more extreme funding across the universe. NaN during warmup
        (lookback // 2 bars).

    Formula:
        raw = mean(|funding_rate_i|) across instruments per timestamp
        signal = rolling_mean(raw, lookback)

    Example:
        FundingDataLoader() -> FundingLevelRegime(lookback=20) -> RegimeScale()

components.name: GarmanKlassVolatility
components.description:
Compute annualized Garman-Klass volatility from OHLC data for efficient variance estimation.

    Use when you need a more statistically efficient volatility estimate than close-to-close
    HistoricalVolatility. Garman-Klass uses all four OHLC prices per bar, extracting more
    information and producing lower-variance estimates. Ideal for volatility regime detection
    and risk management. Requires OHLC data (not just close prices).

    Output:
        Range [0, inf), annualized volatility as a decimal (e.g., 0.80 = 80% annualized vol).
        NaN during warmup (min_periods bars) or where any OHLC price is zero.

    Formula:
        per_bar_var = 0.5 * ln(H/L)^2 - (2*ln(2) - 1) * ln(C/O)^2
        GK_vol = sqrt(rolling_mean(per_bar_var, window) * annualization_factor)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> GarmanKlassVolatility(window=20) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60)
components.usage_hint: Use when you need a more statistically efficient volatility estimate than close-to-close HistoricalVolatility. Garman-Klass uses all four OHLC prices per bar, extracting more information and producing lower-variance estimates. Ideal for volatility regime detection and risk management. Requires OHLC data (not just close prices).

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Rolling lookback in bars for averaging per-bar variance. Shorter (10-20) captures recent vol regime changes, longer (30-60) gives smoother estimates. Default 20 is ~5 hours on 15-min bars.

parameters.name: annualization_factor
parameters.description: Number of periods per year for annualization. 365.25 for daily data (crypto trades every day), 35040 for 15-min bars (365.25 * 96). Must be positive.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations required. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier but noisier output.

components.name: HeikinAshi
components.description:
Convert OHLCV candles to Heikin-Ashi smoothed bars that reduce price noise and highlight trends.

    Use as an early pipeline step before indicators when you want smoother price action
    for trend-following strategies. Heikin-Ashi candles suppress intra-bar whipsaws,
    making momentum and trend indicators (EWMA, ROC) less noisy. Not recommended for
    mean reversion strategies that rely on exact price levels.

    Output:
        Same OHLCV dict structure with modified open/high/low/close columns.
        Volume is unchanged. First bar uses simple average for ha_open.

    Formula:
        ha_close = (O + H + L + C) / 4
        ha_open[i] = (ha_open[i-1] + ha_close[i-1]) / 2
        ha_high = max(H, ha_open, ha_close)
        ha_low = min(L, ha_open, ha_close)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> HeikinAshi() -> EWMA(period=20)
components.usage_hint: Use as an early pipeline step before indicators when you want smoother price action for trend-following strategies. Heikin-Ashi candles suppress intra-bar whipsaws, making momentum and trend indicators (EWMA, ROC) less noisy. Not recommended for mean reversion strategies that rely on exact price levels.

components.name: HistoricalVolatility
components.description:
Compute annualized close-to-close realized volatility for risk measurement and regime detection.

    Use to estimate historical volatility from log returns. Suitable for volatility targeting,
    regime filtering (high-vol vs low-vol environments), and risk-adjusted signal scaling.
    For a more efficient estimator that uses OHLC data, consider GarmanKlassVolatility instead.
    Often fed into slot stores for regime-based strategy switching.

    Output:
        Range [0, inf), annualized volatility as a decimal (e.g., 0.80 = 80% annualized vol).
        NaN during warmup (min_periods bars). Higher = more volatile.

    Formula:
        r = ln(close_t / close_{t-1})
        HV = std(r, window) * sqrt(annualization_factor)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> HistoricalVolatility(window=20) -> RollingZScoreTransform(window=120)
components.usage_hint: Use to estimate historical volatility from log returns. Suitable for volatility targeting, regime filtering (high-vol vs low-vol environments), and risk-adjusted signal scaling. For a more efficient estimator that uses OHLC data, consider GarmanKlassVolatility instead. Often fed into slot stores for regime-based strategy switching.

components.name: Hurst
components.description:
Compute rolling Hurst exponent via Rescaled Range analysis to classify trending vs mean-reverting regimes.

    Use as a regime indicator to dynamically select strategy type. When H > 0.5 the asset
    is trending (favor momentum signals), when H < 0.5 it is mean-reverting (favor mean
    reversion signals), and H ~ 0.5 indicates a random walk. Typically stored in a slot
    and used to gate or weight other signals. Computationally expensive due to R/S
    regression over logarithmically spaced sub-windows.

    Output:
        Range approximately [0, 1]. H > 0.5 = persistent/trending, H < 0.5 = anti-persistent/
        mean-reverting, H ~ 0.5 = random walk. NaN during warmup (max_window bars) and
        where standard deviation is near-zero.

    Formula:
        For each sub-window size n:
          R/S = (max(cumdev) - min(cumdev)) / std(returns)
        H = slope of log(R/S) vs log(n) regression

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> Hurst(max_window=100) -> SlotStore(slot="regime")
components.usage_hint: Use as a regime indicator to dynamically select strategy type. When H > 0.5 the asset is trending (favor momentum signals), when H < 0.5 it is mean-reverting (favor mean reversion signals), and H ~ 0.5 indicates a random walk. Typically stored in a slot and used to gate or weight other signals. Computationally expensive due to R/S regression over logarithmically spaced sub-windows.

parameters.description: Smallest sub-window size for R/S regression. Must be >= 4 for meaningful statistics. Smaller values (4-8) add more data points to the regression but increase noise. Default 10 is a good balance.

parameters.name: max_window
parameters.description: Rolling lookback in bars and largest sub-window size. Controls how much history is used. Shorter (50-80) adapts faster to regime changes, longer (100-200) gives more stable estimates. Default 100 is ~25 hours on 15-min bars.

components.name: IDMPortfolioAggregator
components.description:
Calculate the Instrument Diversification Multiplier (IDM) and aggregate subsystem blocks into portfolio positions.

    Use as the portfolio aggregation step that combines per-instrument subsystem blocks
    into final portfolio positions. Reads forecast, return volatility, and OHLCV data
    from slots to build a synthetic PnL, estimate instrument correlations, and compute
    the diversification benefit. Place after VolTargetWeightConverter and before
    LeverageCap. IDM rewards diversification: low-correlated instruments get a higher
    multiplier.

    Output:
        Portfolio blocks (DataFrame, same shape as input subsystem blocks). Values are
        subsystem_blocks * (1/N) * IDM. IDM typically ranges [1.0, 2.5]. Higher IDM
        means greater diversification benefit.

    Formula:
        IDM = 1 / sqrt(u' R u)
        where u = [1/N, ..., 1/N] (equal weights), R = correlation matrix (negatives floored to 0)
        portfolio_blocks = subsystem_blocks * (1/N) * IDM

    Example:
        VolTargetWeightConverter(return_vol_slot="return_vol") -> IDMPortfolioAggregator(
            forecast_slot="forecast", return_vol_slot="return_vol", ohlcv_slot="ohlcv_1d"
        ) -> LeverageCap()

Place after VolTargetWeightConverter and before LeverageCap. IDM rewards diversification: low-correlated instruments get a higher multiplier.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Correlation estimation window. "180d" (default) = 180 days. Shorter windows adapt faster but are noisier. Accepts time strings or integer bar counts.

parameters.name: resample_freq
parameters.description: Frequency for resampling synthetic PnL before correlation estimation. "1D" (default) for daily. Lower frequency = fewer samples but less noise.

parameters.name: shrinkage
parameters.description: Covariance shrinkage method. "oas" (default) = Oracle Approximating Shrinkage, "lw" = Ledoit-Wolf, "constant" = fixed shrinkage intensity, "none" = sample correlation.

parameters.name: shrinkage_target
parameters.description: Target correlation for shrinkage="constant" mode. None = auto-computed from sample average. Ignored for other methods.

parameters.name: cap

parameters.description: Minimum number of periods before first IDM estimate. None (default) = window // 3 (e.g., 60 for 180d window). Shrinkage handles the noisier early estimates, and EWMA smoothing (smoothing_half_life) dampens volatility in the IDM series.

components.name: IchimokuCloud
components.description:
Compute Ichimoku Cloud direction signal, a multi-component trend system that outputs discrete trend state based on cloud position.

    Use as a trend direction and strength filter. The cloud (kumo) provides support/resistance
    zones; price above cloud = bullish, below = bearish, inside = neutral/consolidating.
    Requires high/low/close data. The discrete output (+1/0/-1) works well as a regime
    gate or as input to a forecast mapper. Prefer IchimokuCloud over SuperTrend when
    you want a neutral zone (inside cloud) rather than a binary trend signal.

    Output:
        Discrete values: +1.0 (close above cloud top, bullish), -1.0 (close below
        cloud bottom, bearish), 0.0 (inside cloud, neutral). NaN during warmup
        (`senkou_b_period` + `kijun_period` bars due to forward displacement).

    Formula:
        tenkan_sen = (highest_high(tenkan) + lowest_low(tenkan)) / 2
        kijun_sen = (highest_high(kijun) + lowest_low(kijun)) / 2
        senkou_a = ((tenkan + kijun) / 2), shifted forward by kijun_period
        senkou_b = ((HH(senkou_b_period) + LL(senkou_b_period)) / 2), shifted forward
        cloud = [min(senkou_a, senkou_b), max(senkou_a, senkou_b)]

    Example:
        IchimokuCloud(tenkan_period=9, kijun_period=26) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use as a trend direction and strength filter. The cloud (kumo) provides support/resistance zones; price above cloud = bullish, below = bearish, inside = neutral/consolidating. Requires high/low/close data. The discrete output (+1/0/-1) works well as a regime gate or as input to a forecast mapper. Prefer IchimokuCloud over SuperTrend when you want a neutral zone (inside cloud) rather than a binary trend signal.

parameters.description: Lookback for the conversion line (fast midpoint). Shorter (7-9) captures recent price structure. Default 9 is standard Ichimoku.

parameters.name: kijun_period
parameters.description: Lookback for the base line (slow midpoint) and cloud forward displacement. Default 26. Also determines how far the cloud projects forward. Larger values (33-52) widen the cloud and increase lag.

parameters.name: senkou_b_period
parameters.description: Lookback for the second cloud boundary (slowest component). Default 52. Controls the width and stability of the cloud. Must be > kijun_period for meaningful cloud structure.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations for rolling calculations. Defaults to half of each respective period. Lower values produce earlier (less stable) cloud boundaries.

components.name: IndexShiftTransform
components.description:
Shift signal timestamps by a fixed number of bars for execution timing adjustment.

    Use to test execution at different offsets within a bar period, or to align
    signals that should be evaluated with a delay (e.g., trade on the bar after
    the signal fires). Positive bars shift forward (delay execution), negative
    bars shift backward (earlier execution). Place at the end of a signal chain
    before position sizing.

    Output:
        Same scale and values as input, with shifted timestamps. No bars are
        dropped; the index is simply offset. No NaN introduced.

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> CrossSectionalZScore() -> IndexShiftTransform(bars=1)
components.usage_hint: Use to test execution at different offsets within a bar period, or to align signals that should be evaluated with a delay (e.g., trade on the bar after the signal fires). Positive bars shift forward (delay execution), negative bars shift backward (earlier execution). Place at the end of a signal chain before position sizing.

parameters.name: bars
parameters.description: Number of bars to shift the index. Positive = forward in time (delay signal), negative = backward. 0 = no shift (pass-through). Typical use: bars=1 to execute on the next bar after signal generation.

components.name: InertiaManager
components.description:
Clean raw entry/exit signals into proper position state transitions using sequential signal cleaning.

    Use after SelectionToSignalConverter to deduplicate consecutive entry signals and
    ensure clean position state management for downstream weight allocation. Converts
    raw signals (which may have repeated entries) into clean entry/exit pairs: only the
    first entry in a sequence produces a +1, subsequent entries become holds (0), and
    exits produce -1. Can accept entry and exit from separate slots or a combined signal.

    Output:
        Clean entry/exit signals (DataFrame). Values: +1 (position open), -1 (position
        close), 0 (hold/no action). Each position lifecycle has exactly one entry
        and one exit.

    Example:
        SelectionToSignalConverter(hold_periods=5) -> InertiaManager() -> DynamicEqualWeightAllocator()
components.usage_hint: Use after SelectionToSignalConverter to deduplicate consecutive entry signals and ensure clean position state management for downstream weight allocation. Converts raw signals (which may have repeated entries) into clean entry/exit pairs: only the first entry in a sequence produces a +1, subsequent entries become holds (0), and exits produce -1. Can accept entry and exit from separate slots or a combined signal.

components.name: KeltnerChannel
components.description:
Compute normalized price position within Keltner Channels using EMA center and ATR-based bands.

    Use as a volatility-adaptive trend and breakout indicator. Unlike BollingerBands which uses
    standard deviation bands, KeltnerChannel uses ATR for band width, making it less sensitive
    to outlier closes. Values near +1/-1 indicate price at the channel extremes. Feed into
    CrossSectionalZScore or a threshold component to generate trading signals.

    Output:
        Approximately [-1, +1] but can exceed on breakouts. 0 = price at EMA center,
        +1 = price at upper channel, -1 = price at lower channel. NaN during warmup
        (period bars). Values beyond +/-1 indicate a channel breakout.

    Formula:
        center = EMA(close, period)
        upper = center + multiplier * ATR(period)
        lower = center - multiplier * ATR(period)
        output = (close - center) / (multiplier * ATR)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> KeltnerChannel(period=20, multiplier=2.0) -> WingsTransform(threshold=0.8)
components.usage_hint: Use as a volatility-adaptive trend and breakout indicator. Unlike BollingerBands which uses standard deviation bands, KeltnerChannel uses ATR for band width, making it less sensitive to outlier closes. Values near +1/-1 indicate price at the channel extremes. Feed into CrossSectionalZScore or a threshold component to generate trading signals.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback window in bars for both EMA and ATR calculation. Shorter (10-15) = tighter, more responsive channels; longer (20-40) = wider, smoother channels. Default 20 is the standard Keltner period.

parameters.name: multiplier
parameters.description: ATR multiplier for channel width. 2.0 = standard (captures most price action), 1.5 = narrower channels (more breakout signals), 2.5-3.0 = wider channels (fewer, stronger breakouts).

components.name: Lag
components.description:
Shift signal forward by N periods to introduce execution delay or create lagged features.

    Use to simulate realistic execution lag (signal computed now, traded next bar) or
    to create lagged versions of signals for crossover strategies. Positive periods
    shift data forward (older data appears at later timestamps), introducing NaN at
    the beginning.

    Output:
        Same range as input, shifted forward. First N rows become NaN.

    Example:
        EWMA(period=20) -> Lag(periods=1) -> CrossoverCombinator(fast_key="fast", slow_key="slow")
components.usage_hint: Use to simulate realistic execution lag (signal computed now, traded next bar) or to create lagged versions of signals for crossover strategies. Positive periods shift data forward (older data appears at later timestamps), introducing NaN at the beginning.

parameters.name: periods
parameters.description: Number of bars to shift forward. 1 = one-bar execution delay, higher values for multi-bar lag. Typical range 1-5.

components.name: LeverageCap
components.description:
Cap gross portfolio leverage by scaling down weights proportionally when exceeded.

    Use as the final risk control before execution to enforce a hard leverage limit.
    When sum(|weights|) exceeds max_leverage on any bar, all weights on that bar are
    scaled down proportionally to hit the cap. Relative allocations are preserved.

    Output:
        WeightSeries (DataFrame, same shape as input). Gross leverage per row
        is guaranteed <= max_leverage. Rows already below the cap pass through
        unchanged.

    Formula:
        if sum(|w_i|) > max_leverage:
            scale = max_leverage / sum(|w_i|)
            w_i = w_i * scale

    Example:
        ForecastWeightNormalizer(target_leverage=2.0) -> LeverageCap(max_leverage=5.0)
components.usage_hint: Use as the final risk control before execution to enforce a hard leverage limit. When sum(|weights|) exceeds max_leverage on any bar, all weights on that bar are scaled down proportionally to hit the cap. Relative allocations are preserved.

parameters.name: max_leverage
parameters.description: Maximum allowed gross leverage (sum of absolute weights per row). Rows exceeding this are scaled down proportionally. Default 5.0. Common values: 1.0 (no leverage), 2.0, 5.0.

components.name: LinearForecastMapper
components.description:
Apply piecewise-linear mapping with dead zone and boost to eliminate weak forecasts and amplify conviction.

    Drop-in replacement for ForecastCapper that adds a dead zone (zeroing weak signals)
    and a boost (amplifying strong ones). Controlled by a single parameter `a_param`
    that preserves forecast variance. Use after ForecastScaler when you want to filter
    out noise near zero without losing the continuous signal shape.

    Output:
        Range [-cap*a_param, +cap*a_param]. Zero inside the dead zone (|x| < threshold).
        NaN values are preserved. Polarity matches input.

    Formula:
        threshold = -19.811 + 22.263 * a_param
        slope = (cap * a_param) / (cap - threshold)
        |x| < threshold        -> 0             (dead zone)
        threshold <= |x| <= cap -> slope * (|x| - threshold)  (linear ramp)
        |x| > cap              -> cap * a_param  (boosted cap)

    Example:
        ForecastScaler() -> LinearForecastMapper(a_param=1.4) -> ForecastCombiner()
components.usage_hint: Drop-in replacement for ForecastCapper that adds a dead zone (zeroing weak signals) and a boost (amplifying strong ones). Controlled by a single parameter `a_param` that preserves forecast variance. Use after ForecastScaler when you want to filter out noise near zero without losing the continuous signal shape.

parameters.name: a_param
parameters.description: Controls dead zone width and boost magnitude. Must be in (1.0, 2.0). Lower (1.1-1.2) = narrow dead zone, mild boost. Higher (1.5-1.8) = wide dead zone, strong boost. Default 1.4 is a good balance for typical scaled forecasts.

parameters.name: cap
parameters.description: Input cap level (should match ForecastScaler target * 2, default 20). Rarely changed.

components.name: LinearRegressionR2
components.description:
Compute rolling R² of log-price linear regression to measure trend cleanliness.

    Use to distinguish clean, linear trends from noisy, choppy price action. High R²
    (near 1.0) indicates price follows a smooth trend line — ideal for trend-following
    strategies. Low R² (near 0.0) indicates random or choppy movement — better for
    mean reversion or range strategies. Complements Hurst for regime detection:
    Hurst measures persistence (trending vs mean-reverting), R² measures linearity
    (smooth vs noisy). Feed into QuantileRankTransform for screening or use as a
    regime filter to gate other signals.

    Output:
        Range [0, 1]. 1.0 = perfectly linear price trend, 0.0 = no linear relationship.
        NaN during warmup (window bars) and where price is constant. Values above 0.7
        indicate a strong, clean trend; below 0.3 indicates choppy/range-bound.

    Formula:
        y = log(close) over rolling window
        x = [0, 1, ..., window-1]
        R² = (Σ(x-x̄)(y-ȳ))² / (Σ(x-x̄)² · Σ(y-ȳ)²)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> LinearRegressionR2(window=20) -> QuantileRankTransform()
components.usage_hint: Use to distinguish clean, linear trends from noisy, choppy price action. High R² (near 1.0) indicates price follows a smooth trend line — ideal for trend-following strategies. Low R² (near 0.0) indicates random or choppy movement — better for mean reversion or range strategies. Complements Hurst for regime detection: Hurst measures persistence (trending vs mean-reverting), R² measures linearity (smooth vs noisy). Feed into QuantileRankTransform for screening or use as a regime filter to gate other signals.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Rolling lookback in bars for the linear regression fit. Shorter (10-15) measures short-term trend quality, longer (20-40) measures medium-term. Default 20 balances sensitivity and stability. The x-axis is simply [0..window-1], so this is a fixed-length sliding window regression.

components.name: LogReturn
components.description:
Compute logarithmic return over N periods for additive compounding and volatility analysis.

    Use when returns need to be additive across time (e.g., for volatility estimation
    or cumulative return decomposition). Log returns are symmetric around zero and
    better suited for statistical analysis than SimpleReturn. Prefer SimpleReturn
    when interpretability as percentage change matters more.

    Output:
        Unbounded log-ratio. Positive = price increase, negative = decrease.
        First N rows are NaN (warmup). Zero prices produce NaN (not inf).

    Formula:
        return = log(x_t / x_{t-n})

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> LogReturn(periods=1) -> RollingZScoreTransform(window=60)
components.usage_hint: Use when returns need to be additive across time (e.g., for volatility estimation or cumulative return decomposition). Log returns are symmetric around zero and better suited for statistical analysis than SimpleReturn. Prefer SimpleReturn when interpretability as percentage change matters more.

components.name: LongShortWeightConverter
components.description:
Flip portfolio weight signs to enable short selling in long-short strategies.

    Use at the end of a pipeline to convert a long-only weight allocation into
    short positions. In "short" mode, all positive weights are negated. In "long"
    mode (default), weights pass through unchanged. Useful for building long-short
    pairs: run the same signal pipeline twice with mode="long" and mode="short",
    then combine.

    Output:
        Same range as input weights but with signs potentially flipped. In "short"
        mode, all non-zero weights become negative. Zero weights stay zero.

    Example:
        DynamicEqualWeightAllocator() -> LongShortWeightConverter(mode="short")
components.usage_hint: Use at the end of a pipeline to convert a long-only weight allocation into short positions. In "short" mode, all positive weights are negated. In "long" mode (default), weights pass through unchanged. Useful for building long-short pairs: run the same signal pipeline twice with mode="long" and mode="short", then combine.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: Weight conversion mode. "long" = pass through unchanged (default). "short" = negate all positive weights for short selling. The position sizing and allocation logic remain the same regardless of mode.

components.name: MACD
components.description:
Compute MACD histogram, a trend-following momentum indicator measuring the difference between two exponential moving averages.

    Use as a trend direction and momentum signal. The histogram output captures acceleration
    of the trend: positive and rising = strengthening uptrend, negative and falling =
    strengthening downtrend. Typically followed by CrossSectionalZScore or ForecastScaler.
    Prefer MACD over simple moving average crossovers (EWMA pairs) when you want a
    single-output indicator with built-in signal-line smoothing.

    Output:
        Unbounded, in price units. The histogram = MACD line - signal line.
        Positive = bullish momentum, negative = bearish. Zero-crossings indicate
        trend transitions. NaN during warmup (`slow_period` + `signal_period` bars).

    Formula:
        MACD_line = EMA(close, fast_period) - EMA(close, slow_period)
        signal_line = EMA(MACD_line, signal_period)
        histogram = MACD_line - signal_line

    Example:
        MACD(fast_period=12, slow_period=26) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use as a trend direction and momentum signal. The histogram output captures acceleration of the trend: positive and rising = strengthening uptrend, negative and falling = strengthening downtrend. Typically followed by CrossSectionalZScore or ForecastScaler. Prefer MACD over simple moving average crossovers (EWMA pairs) when you want a single-output indicator with built-in signal-line smoothing.

parameters.name: slow_period

components.name: MFI
components.description:
Compute Money Flow Index as a volume-weighted momentum oscillator for overbought and oversold detection.

    Use as a volume-confirmed version of RSI. MFI incorporates both price and volume,
    making it less susceptible to false signals during low-volume moves. Values above 80
    indicate overbought conditions, below 20 oversold. Feed into CrossSectionalZScore for
    relative ranking or WingsTransform to trade extreme readings. Requires volume data.

    Output:
        Range [0, 100]. Above 80 = overbought (selling pressure expected), below 20 =
        oversold (buying pressure expected). NaN during warmup (period bars).

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> MFI(period=14) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()

Values above 80 indicate overbought conditions, below 20 oversold. Feed into CrossSectionalZScore for relative ranking or WingsTransform to trade extreme readings. Requires volume data.

components.name: MOM
components.description:
Compute raw price momentum as the absolute price difference over a lookback period.

    Use as a simple momentum signal when you need absolute price change rather than
    percentage (ROC). Because output is in price units, it is not directly comparable
    across assets with different price levels -- always follow with CrossSectionalZScore
    or RollingZScoreTransform to normalize. Prefer ROC over MOM for most strategies
    unless you specifically need unnormalized price deltas.

    Output:
        Unbounded, in price units. Positive = price rose over the period,
        negative = price fell. Magnitude depends on asset price level.
        NaN during warmup (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        MOM = close - close[t - period]

    Example:
        MOM(period=10) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use as a simple momentum signal when you need absolute price change rather than percentage (ROC). Because output is in price units, it is not directly comparable across assets with different price levels -- always follow with CrossSectionalZScore or RollingZScoreTransform to normalize. components.name: MarketLeverageScaler
components.description:
Scale portfolio leverage uniformly based on a market-wide regime signal for conditional exposure management.

    Use to increase leverage during favorable market regimes (bullish, high volume) and
    reduce it otherwise. Reads a market-wide regime signal (must be Series, not per-asset
    DataFrame) from a named slot. Place after weight allocation and before execution.
    For long-only strategies, bullish regime = regime_leverage, everything else = base_leverage.
    For long-short, bullish scales longs up and bearish scales shorts up.

    Output:
        Scaled portfolio weights (DataFrame). Same shape as input. Absolute weight
        magnitudes change but relative allocation is preserved within each side
        (long/short).

    Example:
        EqualWeightAllocator() -> MarketLeverageScaler(base_leverage=1.0, regime_leverage=3.0, regime_slot="volume_regime")

components.name: MarketRiskScaler
components.description:
Scale portfolio positions based on a market-wide risk metric for volatility-adaptive exposure management.

    Use to dynamically adjust position sizes based on market volatility or stress
    conditions. Reads a market-wide risk metric (must be Series) from a named slot.
    Increases positions when risk is low and reduces them when risk is high. An
    alternative to regime-based leverage scaling that uses continuous risk metrics
    rather than discrete regime labels.

    Output:
        Risk-adjusted portfolio weights (DataFrame). Same shape as input.
        Magnitudes scaled by low_risk_scale or high_risk_scale depending on
        whether the risk metric is below or above the threshold.

    Example:
        EqualWeightAllocator() -> MarketRiskScaler(low_risk_scale=1.5, high_risk_scale=0.5, risk_slot="market_vol")

components.name: MarketTrendRegimeFilter
components.description:
Detect market-wide bull/bear trend regime using EMA crossover on a market index for portfolio-level decisions.

    Use for portfolio-level exposure management. Constructs a market index from
    cross-asset price data (trimmed mean or equal weight), then applies EMA crossover
    to classify bull/bear/neutral regimes. Output is a Series suitable for leverage
    scaling or regime gating. For volatility-based regimes, use VolatilityFilter.

    Output:
        Series with +1.0 (bull, fast EMA > slow EMA), -1.0 (bear), or 0.0 (neutral).
        Shifted by 1 bar to prevent look-ahead bias. NaN during EMA warmup.

    Formula:
        market_index = trimmed_mean(prices) or equal_weight_mean(prices)
        fast_ema = EMA(market_index, fast_period)
        slow_ema = EMA(market_index, slow_period)
        regime = +1 if fast > slow, -1 if fast < slow, 0 otherwise

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> MarketTrendRegimeFilter(fast_period=5, slow_period=17)
components.usage_hint: Use for portfolio-level exposure management. Constructs a market index from cross-asset price data (trimmed mean or equal weight), then applies EMA crossover to classify bull/bear/neutral regimes. Output is a Series suitable for leverage scaling or regime gating. For volatility-based regimes, use VolatilityFilter.

components.name: MarketVolumeRegimeFilter
components.description:
Detect market-wide high-volume regime from cross-asset volume data for portfolio-level leverage scaling.

    Use for portfolio-level decisions when you want to scale leverage based on overall
    market volume activity. Computes the mean volume across all assets and compares to
    a rolling average. Returns a binary Series (1.0 = high volume regime, 0.0 = normal).
    For per-asset volume filtering, use AssetVolumeRegimeFilter instead.

    Output:
        Series with 1.0 (high volume regime) or 0.0 (normal). Shifted by 1 bar
        to prevent look-ahead bias. NaN during warmup (window/2 bars).

    Formula:
        market_vol = mean(volume across assets)
        threshold_line = threshold * rolling_mean(market_vol, window)
        regime = 1.0 if market_vol >= threshold_line else 0.0

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> MarketVolumeRegimeFilter(threshold=2.0, window=20)

Computes the mean volume across all assets and compares to a rolling average. Returns a binary Series (1.0 = high volume regime, 0.0 = normal). For per-asset volume filtering, use AssetVolumeRegimeFilter instead.

parameters.description: Volume multiplier for regime detection. 2.0 = trigger when market volume is 2x the rolling average. Higher values (3.0-5.0) detect only extreme volume events. Default 2.0.

components.name: MaskAnd
components.description:
Combine boolean masks with AND — True where ALL branches are True.

    Use after a Parallel block that produces multiple boolean masks. Requires
    all conditions to pass. Common use: combining a trend filter with a
    volume filter to require both.

    Output:
        Boolean DataFrame. True only where every branch is True.
        NaN in any branch treated as False.

    Example:
        Parallel(
            low_trend=[ADX(14), BelowThresholdFilter(threshold=25)],
            high_volume=[NotionalVolumeExtractor(), AboveThresholdFilter(threshold=1e6)],
        ) -> MaskAnd()
components.usage_hint: Use after a Parallel block that produces multiple boolean masks. Requires all conditions to pass. Common use: combining a trend filter with a volume filter to require both.

components.name: MaskOr
components.description:
Combine boolean masks with OR — True where ANY branch is True.

    Use after a Parallel block that produces multiple boolean masks. Passes
    assets matching any condition. Common use: combining multiple threshold
    filters into a single "any extreme" mask.

    Output:
        Boolean DataFrame. True where at least one branch is True.
        NaN in any branch treated as False.

    Example:
        Parallel(
            overbought=[RSI(3), AboveThresholdFilter(threshold=90)],
            oversold=[RSI(3), BelowThresholdFilter(threshold=10)],
        ) -> MaskOr()
components.usage_hint: Use after a Parallel block that produces multiple boolean masks. Passes assets matching any condition. Common use: combining multiple threshold filters into a single "any extreme" mask.

components.name: MaxDrawdownStopLoss
components.description:
Fixed-percentage stop loss that resets per trade, with long and short support.

    Reads entry signals and OHLCV data from pipeline slots. For each trade
    (per-asset), tracks the entry price and direction, then exits when the
    unrealized loss exceeds the threshold:

    Long (+1 entry): loss = (close - entry_price) / entry_price.
        Exit when loss <= -drawdown_threshold.
    Short (-1 entry): loss = (entry_price - close) / entry_price.
        Exit when loss <= -drawdown_threshold (price moved against short).

    Entry price resets on each new entry signal. Different assets can be
    long and short simultaneously — state is tracked independently.

    Output:
        1.0 = exit triggered, 0.0 = hold. Direction-agnostic scalar
        compatible with PSM(exit_mode='scalar').

    Example:
        Store('entry_signals'),
        MaxDrawdownStopLoss(entry_slot='entry_signals', ohlcv_slot='ohlcv',
                            drawdown_threshold=0.05),
        Store('exit_signals'),
components.usage_hint: Reads entry signals and OHLCV data from pipeline slots. For each trade (per-asset), tracks the entry price and direction, then exits when the unrealized loss exceeds the threshold:

components.name: MeanReversionDistance
components.description:
Compute price displacement from its moving average in ATR units for mean reversion detection.

    Use to measure how far price has stretched from its mean in volatility-normalized terms.
    Positive values indicate price above the SMA (extended upward), negative indicates below
    (extended downward). Extreme values suggest reversion potential. Already vol-normalized
    by ATR, so output is comparable across assets without additional normalization. Feed into
    QuantileRankTransform for screening or CrossSectionalZScore for signal generation.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 0. Positive = price above SMA (overextended long),
        negative = below SMA (overextended short). Typical range [-3, +3] for liquid
        crypto on 4h bars. NaN during warmup (sma_period bars).

    Formula:
        MR = (close - SMA(close, sma_period)) / ATR(atr_period)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> MeanReversionDistance(sma_period=20, atr_period=14) -> QuantileRankTransform()
components.usage_hint: Use to measure how far price has stretched from its mean in volatility-normalized terms. Positive values indicate price above the SMA (extended upward), negative indicates below (extended downward). Extreme values suggest reversion potential. Already vol-normalized by ATR, so output is comparable across assets without additional normalization. Feed into QuantileRankTransform for screening or CrossSectionalZScore for signal generation.

parameters.description: Lookback for the simple moving average baseline. Shorter (10-15) measures deviation from fast mean, longer (20-50) from slow mean. Default 20 captures medium-term equilibrium.

parameters.name: atr_period
parameters.description: Lookback for ATR volatility normalization. Default 14 is Wilder's standard. Normalizing by ATR makes the output comparable across assets with different volatility levels.

components.name: MinMaxNormalize
components.description:
Scale signals to [0, 1] range using rolling min-max normalization.

    Use when downstream components require bounded input (e.g., regime classifiers,
    threshold-based filters) and you want to preserve the signal's shape. Each asset
    is normalized independently against its own rolling history. Prefer
    RollingZScoreTransform when you need centering around zero, or SigmoidNormalize
    for a stateless bounded transform without lookback.

    Output:
        Range [0, 1]. 0 = at rolling minimum, 1 = at rolling maximum.
        NaN during warmup and when rolling min equals rolling max (flat signal).

    Formula:
        output = (x - rolling_min(x, window)) / (rolling_max(x, window) - rolling_min(x, window))

    Example:
        RSI(period=14) -> MinMaxNormalize(window=60) -> ThresholdCross(threshold=0.8)

Each asset is normalized independently against its own rolling history. Prefer RollingZScoreTransform when you need centering around zero, or SigmoidNormalize for a stateless bounded transform without lookback.

parameters.description: Minimum observations before producing output. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) output.

components.name: NATR
components.description:
Compute Normalized Average True Range as a percentage of price for cross-asset volatility comparison.

    Use when you need volatility that is comparable across assets with different price levels.
    Unlike ATR which returns absolute price units, NATR normalizes by close price so a 2%
    NATR means the same relative volatility whether the asset is $100 or $50,000. Ideal as
    input to universe filters (exclude low-vol assets) or volatility regime detection.

    Output:
        Range [0, inf), in percentage units. Typically 1-5% for liquid crypto on 15-min bars.
        Higher = more volatile. NaN during warmup (period bars).

    Formula:
        NATR = ATR(period) / close * 100

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> NATR(period=14) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60)

Unlike ATR which returns absolute price units, NATR normalizes by close price so a 2% NATR means the same relative volatility whether the asset is $100 or $50,000. Ideal as input to universe filters (exclude low-vol assets) or volatility regime detection.

components.name: NegateTransform
components.description:
Negate (multiply by -1) signal values to flip polarity for carry, mean reversion, or directional inversion.

    Use when a signal has the opposite polarity from what downstream components
    expect. For example, high funding rate naturally means "pay to be long" so
    negating converts it to a short signal. Also useful when combining signals
    that need opposite-sign alignment before ForecastCombiner.

    Output:
        Same range as input, sign-flipped. NaN preserved.

    Example:
        FundingRateLoader() -> NegateTransform() -> ForecastScaler()

For example, high funding rate naturally means "pay to be long" so negating converts it to a short signal. components.name: OBV
components.description:
Compute On-Balance Volume to detect buying and selling pressure from cumulative volume flow.

    Use to confirm price trends with volume or spot divergences where price moves without
    volume support. OBV is cumulative and unbounded, so it is not directly comparable across
    assets. Feed into RollingZScoreTransform or CrossSectionalZScore to normalize before
    combining with other signals. Requires volume data in PriceFrame.

    Output:
        Unbounded cumulative value. Rising OBV = net buying pressure, falling = net selling.
        Absolute scale depends on asset volume. No NaN warmup (starts from first bar).

    Formula:
        OBV_t = OBV_{t-1} + volume  (if close > prev_close)
        OBV_t = OBV_{t-1} - volume  (if close < prev_close)
        OBV_t = OBV_{t-1}           (if close == prev_close)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> OBV() -> RollingZScoreTransform(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use to confirm price trends with volume or spot divergences where price moves without volume support. OBV is cumulative and unbounded, so it is not directly comparable across assets. Feed into RollingZScoreTransform or CrossSectionalZScore to normalize before combining with other signals. Requires volume data in PriceFrame.

components.name: OrCombinator
components.description:
Combine multiple boolean filter masks with OR logic so any condition being True is sufficient.

    Use after a Parallel step producing multiple filter signals when you want to trade
    if ANY condition is met (e.g., trade if volume OR momentum is favorable). For AND
    logic requiring all conditions, use AndCombinator instead. NaN values are treated
    as False.

    Output:
        Boolean DataFrame (0.0/1.0). 1.0 where ANY input mask is True.
        Same shape as input masks.

    Example:
        Parallel(vol=VolatilityFilter(...), momentum=MomentumFilter(...)) -> OrCombinator("vol", "momentum")

components.name: ParabolicSAR
components.description:
Compute Parabolic SAR (Stop and Reverse) trailing stop levels for trend-following and exit signals.

    Use as a trailing stop or trend reversal detector. Output is a price level that follows
    the trend: below price in uptrends, above price in downtrends. Comparing SAR to close
    gives a direction signal. Requires high/low data. Since output is in absolute price units,
    it must be transformed (e.g., (close - SAR) / close) before cross-asset comparison.
    Prefer SuperTrend over ParabolicSAR when you want a pre-computed direction signal
    rather than raw stop levels.

    Output:
        In price units (absolute levels). Tracks below price during uptrends and
        above price during downtrends. NaN for the first bar only (minimal warmup).
        Not directly comparable across assets without normalization.

    Formula:
        SAR_t = SAR_{t-1} + AF * (EP - SAR_{t-1})
        where AF starts at `acceleration`, increments by `acceleration` on new extremes,
        capped at `maximum`. EP = extreme point (highest high or lowest low in trend).

    Example:
        ParabolicSAR(acceleration=0.02, maximum=0.2) -> PriceNormalize() -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use as a trailing stop or trend reversal detector. Output is a price level that follows the trend: below price in uptrends, above price in downtrends. Comparing SAR to close gives a direction signal. Requires high/low data. Since output is in absolute price units, it must be transformed (e.g., (close - SAR) / close) before cross-asset comparison. Prefer SuperTrend over ParabolicSAR when you want a pre-computed direction signal rather than raw stop levels.

parameters.name: acceleration
parameters.description: Starting and increment value for the acceleration factor (AF). Smaller (0.01) = slower trailing, larger (0.03-0.04) = faster trailing that stays closer to price. Default 0.02 is standard.

parameters.name: maximum
parameters.description: Cap for the acceleration factor. Limits how aggressively the SAR trails price. Default 0.2. Reducing to 0.1 makes the indicator less responsive to new extremes; increasing to 0.3 makes it tighter but more prone to whipsaw.

components.name: PercentageNormalizationTransform
components.description:
Normalize indicator values by price to create percentage-based measures for cross-asset comparison.

    Use to convert absolute indicators (ATR, Bollinger bandwidth, spread) into
    price-relative percentages so assets at different price levels are comparable.
    Requires an OHLCV slot for price data. Commonly placed after ATR or similar
    absolute indicators and before CrossSectionalZScore or a ranking transform.

    Output:
        Non-negative, unbounded. Represents indicator as a percentage of price
        (e.g., ATR of $500 on a $50,000 asset = 1.0%). NaN where price is zero.

    Formula:
        output = (indicator / price) * multiplier
        where multiplier defaults to 100 for percentage

    Example:
        ATR(period=14) -> PercentageNormalizationTransform(ohlcv_slot="ohlcv") -> CrossSectionalZScore()
components.usage_hint: Use to convert absolute indicators (ATR, Bollinger bandwidth, spread) into price-relative percentages so assets at different price levels are comparable. Requires an OHLCV slot for price data. Commonly placed after ATR or similar absolute indicators and before CrossSectionalZScore or a ranking transform.

components.name: PositionStateMachine
components.description:
Generate stateful position signals from entry and exit events for event-driven strategies.

    Use when entry/exit logic is computed by separate upstream components stored
    in pipeline slots (e.g., ThresholdCross writing to an "entry" slot and a
    stop-loss component writing to an "exit" slot). Unlike ThresholdCross which
    is stateless and re-evaluates every bar, this component has memory — once
    entered, a position holds until an explicit exit signal fires. Supports
    direct long-to-short flips without intermediate flat bar.

    Output:
        Values in {-1, 0, +1}. +1 = long, -1 = short, 0 = flat. State persists
        across bars until changed by a new entry or exit event. NaN in entry
        signal poisons state forward indefinitely (upstream must fillna(0)).
        When entry and exit fire on the same bar, entry wins.

    Example (scalar exit — direction-blind):
        ThresholdCross(upper=0.7) -> Store("entry")
        StopLoss(pct=0.02) -> Store("exit")
        PositionStateMachine(entry_slot="entry", exit_slot="exit")

    Example (directional exit — exit when signal opposes position):
        ThresholdCross(upper=2, lower=-2) -> Store("entries")
        ThresholdCross(upper=0, lower=0) -> Store("exits")
        PositionStateMachine(entry_slot="entries", exit_slot="exits", exit_mode="directional")
components.usage_hint: Use when entry/exit logic is computed by separate upstream components stored in pipeline slots (e.g., ThresholdCross writing to an "entry" slot and a stop-loss component writing to an "exit" slot). Unlike ThresholdCross which is stateless and re-evaluates every bar, this component has memory — once entered, a position holds until an explicit exit signal fires. Supports direct long-to-short flips without intermediate flat bar.

parameters.name: entry_slot
parameters.description: Name of the pipeline slot containing entry signals (BinarySignal with +1 for long, -1 for short, 0 for no entry). Must match the slot name used by the upstream Store.

parameters.name: exit_slot
parameters.description: Name of the pipeline slot containing exit signals. Must match the slot name used by the upstream Store. Signal interpretation depends on exit_mode.

parameters.name: exit_mode
parameters.description: How exit signals are interpreted. "scalar" (default): exit fires when exit_signal == 1.0, regardless of position direction. Use with boolean/scalar exit signals (TimeBasedExitFilter, ValueStopExitFilter). "directional": exit fires when exit_signal opposes current position (exit * state < 0). Use with ThresholdCross as exit signal — e.g., ThresholdCross(upper=0, lower=0) produces +1 when signal > 0 (exits shorts) and -1 when signal < 0 (exits longs). Enables "enter at extreme, exit at mean" strategies.

parameters.description: How exit signals are interpreted. "scalar" (default): exit fires when exit_signal == 1.0, regardless of position direction. Use with boolean/scalar exit signals (TimeBasedExitFilter, ValueStopExitFilter). "directional": exit fires when exit_signal opposes current position (exit * state < 0). Use with ThresholdCross as exit signal — e.g., ThresholdCross(upper=0, lower=0) produces +1 when signal > 0 (exits shorts) and -1 when signal < 0 (exits longs).     Use as the first component in any pipeline that needs price data. Supports smart

        and DatetimeIndex. NaN for gaps larger than max_gap_fill.

    Example:

components.name: QuantileRangeSelector
components.description:
Select assets whose quantile rank falls within a specified percentile range (0-100 scale).

    Use after QuantileRankTransform to select assets in a specific percentile band.
    For example, select the top quartile (75-100) for momentum or the bottom decile
    (0-10) for mean reversion entries. Outputs a binary mask (1.0 = in range,
    0.0 = out of range) suitable for MaskAnd or ApplyMask.

    Output:
        BinarySignal DataFrame (1.0/0.0). 1.0 where quantile rank is within the
        specified range, 0.0 otherwise.

    Example:
        QuantileRankTransform() -> QuantileRangeSelector(low_quantile=75, high_quantile=100) -> MaskAnd()

components.name: QuantileRankTransform
components.description:
Rank assets cross-sectionally into 0-100 percentiles for comparable quantile-based filtering.

    Use to convert any raw indicator into cross-sectional percentile ranks so that
    different indicators share a common [0, 100] scale. Parameter-free and suitable
    for universe filtering (e.g., "top 20% by momentum"). Prefer CrossSectionalZScore
    when you need unbounded z-scores for forecast scaling, or RankTransform when you
    need ordinal ranks (1 to N) rather than percentiles.

    Output:
        Range [0, 100]. 100 = highest-ranked asset, 0 = lowest. NaN preserved
        from input. Cross-sectional: ranks are relative to other assets at each bar.

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> QuantileRankTransform() -> ThresholdCross(threshold=80)
components.usage_hint: Use to convert any raw indicator into cross-sectional percentile ranks so that different indicators share a common [0, 100] scale. Parameter-free and suitable for universe filtering (e.g., "top 20% by momentum"). Prefer CrossSectionalZScore when you need unbounded z-scores for forecast scaling, or RankTransform when you need ordinal ranks (1 to N) rather than percentiles.

components.name: ROC
components.description:
Compute rate of change (percentage return) over a lookback period as a momentum signal.

    Use as a momentum or mean reversion input signal. For momentum strategies, feed into
    ForecastScaler directly. For mean reversion, pipe through CrossSectionalZScore then
    WingsTransform to fade extreme moves. Differs from MOM by outputting percentage change
    rather than absolute price difference, making it comparable across assets.

    Output:
        Percentage change, unbounded. Typically [-30, +30] for 15-min crypto bars
        with period=14. Positive = price rising, negative = price falling.
        NaN for the first `period` bars (warmup).

    Formula:
        ROC = ((close - close[t-period]) / close[t-period]) * 100

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use as a momentum or mean reversion input signal. For momentum strategies, feed into ForecastScaler directly. For mean reversion, pipe through CrossSectionalZScore then WingsTransform to fade extreme moves. Differs from MOM by outputting percentage change rather than absolute price difference, making it comparable across assets.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback in bars for return calculation. Shorter (2-8) captures fast momentum, longer (14-32) captures medium-term trends. Common values: 4 (fast MR), 10 (standard), 20 (slow trend).

components.name: RSI
components.description:
Compute Relative Strength Index (RSI), a bounded momentum oscillator measuring overbought and oversold conditions.

    Use as a mean reversion signal when assets reach extremes (>70 overbought, <30 oversold),
    or as a trend-confirmation filter (midline 50 crossover). Typically followed by
    CrossSectionalZScore or a threshold-based transform like WingsTransform. Prefer RSI over
    CMO when you want a bounded [0, 100] oscillator; CMO provides a similar signal on [-100, 100].

    Output:
        Range [0, 100]. Values > 70 = overbought (potential short), < 30 = oversold
        (potential long), ~50 = neutral. NaN during warmup (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        RS = avg_gain(period) / avg_loss(period)
        RSI = 100 - 100 / (1 + RS)

    Example:
        RSI(period=14) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> WingsTransform(threshold=1.0)
components.usage_hint: Use as a mean reversion signal when assets reach extremes (>70 overbought, <30 oversold), or as a trend-confirmation filter (midline 50 crossover). Typically followed by CrossSectionalZScore or a threshold-based transform like WingsTransform. Prefer RSI over CMO when you want a bounded [0, 100] oscillator; CMO provides a similar signal on [-100, 100].

components.name: RankTransform
components.description:
Rank assets cross-sectionally from 1 to N for ordinal signal comparison and portfolio construction.

    Use when portfolio construction requires ordinal ranks (e.g., "go long top 5, short
    bottom 5"). More configurable than QuantileRankTransform with control over sort
    direction, tie handling, and percentage mode. Prefer QuantileRankTransform for
    simple percentile-based filtering, or CrossSectionalZScore for continuous z-scores.

        from input. Cross-sectional: ranks computed across assets at each timestamp.

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> RankTransform(ascending=False) -> TopNFilter(n=5)

components.name: RealizedVolCap
components.description:
Cap portfolio weights by recent realized portfolio vol.

    Computes per-bar portfolio P&L from lagged weights × per-asset returns,
    measures rolling stdev over `recent_window`, and scales weights down
    proportionally if the annualized recent vol exceeds `target_vol × ratio`.

    Use as a final risk overlay when you want positions to respect a realized-
    vol budget. Common pairing: VolTargetWeightConverter sets intended vol →
    IDM aggregation may push above → RealizedVolCap pulls back to target if
    the strategy's recent realized vol drifts above the ceiling.

    Output:
        WeightSeries (same shape as input). Per-bar weights scaled by M ≤ 1.
        Logs fire count + average M at INFO when log_fires=True.

    Formula:
        pnl[t]      = Σ w_lag[t-1, i] × r[t, i]
        recent_vol  = rolling_std(pnl, recent_window) × √bars_per_year
        M[t]        = clip(target_vol × ratio / recent_vol[t], 0, 1)
        capped      = weights × M

    Causality:
        weights.shift(1) before computing portfolio P&L — no lookahead. Rolling
        stdev uses past-only window. M[t] is bar-t value applied to bar-t
        weights (correct under right-labeled bars / decision-at-close[t]).

    Tuning:
        ratio=1.0 binds at exactly the target_vol — strict cap, fires often.
        ratio=2.0 fires only when recent vol exceeds 2× target — looser.
        For the v1 strategy, ratio=2.0 with target_vol=0.50 (50% annualized)
        gives Carver-orthodox tail-only firing (~2% of bars).

    Example:
        VolTargetWeightConverter() → IDMPortfolioAggregator()
          → RealizedVolCap(target_vol=0.50, ratio=2.0, recent_window=30)
          → AdverseVolCap(target_vol=0.50, ratio=6.5)
          → LeverageCap(5)

components.name: RealizedVolatilityRegime
components.description:
Compute annualized realized volatility averaged across the universe as a volatility regime indicator.

    Use to detect high-volatility regimes where strategies may need position scaling
    or risk reduction. Feed into RegimeGate to disable momentum strategies during
    vol spikes, or into RegimeScale to reduce forecast intensity. Pairs well with
    StressCompositeRegime for multi-factor regime detection.

    Output:
        Scalar GlobalSeries, non-negative (annualized vol). Higher values indicate
        elevated market-wide volatility. Annualized using sqrt(365) crypto factor.
        NaN during warmup (lookback // 2 bars) or when fewer than 2 instruments
        are available.

    Formula:
        vol_i = rolling_std(returns_i, lookback) * sqrt(365)
        signal = mean(vol_i) across instruments

    Example:
        Store('ohlcv') -> RealizedVolatilityRegime(ohlcv_slot='ohlcv', lookback=20) -> RegimeGate()

Feed into RegimeGate to disable momentum strategies during vol spikes, or into RegimeScale to reduce forecast intensity. Pairs well with StressCompositeRegime for multi-factor regime detection.

components.name: RegimeGate
components.description:
Gate a forecast signal on/off based on a regime index condition for hard regime conditioning.

    Use to completely disable a signal branch during unfavorable regime states (e.g.,
    turn off momentum in choppy markets). Receives signal and index from a preceding
    Parallel step. For proportional scaling instead of binary on/off, use RegimeScale.
    Condition is parsed safely without eval().

    Output:
        Same range as input signal. Zero where condition is False (gated off).
        NaN where index is NaN (not gated to 0). Signal unchanged where True.

    Example:
        Parallel(momentum=..., regime=...) -> RegimeGate(signal_key="momentum", index_key="regime", condition="index < 0")

Receives signal and index from a preceding Parallel step. For proportional scaling instead of binary on/off, use RegimeScale. Condition is parsed safely without eval().

components.name: RegimeLeverageScaler
components.description:
Scale portfolio leverage based on a market regime signal with staleness protection for conditional exposure.

    Use to increase leverage during favorable market regimes and apply base leverage
    otherwise. Similar to MarketLeverageScaler but adds staleness protection: if regime
    data stops updating for max_stale_bars, it reverts to neutral (base_leverage). Place
    after weight allocation and before execution. Reads regime signal from a named slot.

    Output:
        Scaled portfolio weights (DataFrame). Same shape as input. Bullish regime
        scales long positions to regime_leverage, bearish scales short positions
        (if not long_only). Neutral = base_leverage for all.

    Example:
        EqualWeightAllocator() -> RegimeLeverageScaler(base_leverage=1.0, regime_leverage=5.0, regime_slot="regime_filter")

Similar to MarketLeverageScaler but adds staleness protection: if regime data stops updating for max_stale_bars, it reverts to neutral (base_leverage). Place after weight allocation and before execution. Reads regime signal from a named slot.
