components.name: RegimeScale
components.description:
Continuously scale a forecast signal proportional to a regime index for soft regime conditioning.

    Use when you want proportional exposure adjustment rather than binary on/off gating.
    Receives signal and index from a preceding Parallel step. For hard on/off gating,
    use RegimeGate instead. The "linear" method is the standard choice; "inverse" and
    "sigmoid" are experimental alternatives.

    Output:
        Same units as input signal, multiplied by a scaling factor in clip_range
        (default [0.0, 2.0]). NaN where index is NaN. Factor of 1.0 means
        no change, 0.0 means fully suppressed, 2.0 means doubled.

    Formula:
        linear:  factor = clip(1 - index/3, clip_range)
        inverse: factor = clip(1 / (1 + |index|), clip_range)
        sigmoid: factor = clip(sigmoid(-index), clip_range)

    Example:
        Parallel(momentum=..., regime=...) -> RegimeScale(signal_key="momentum", index_key="regime", scale_method="linear")
components.usage_hint: Use when you want proportional exposure adjustment rather than binary on/off gating. Receives signal and index from a preceding Parallel step. For hard on/off gating, use RegimeGate instead. The "linear" method is the standard choice; "inverse" and "sigmoid" are experimental alternatives.

parameters.description: Method for computing the scaling factor. "linear" (default) = 1 - index/3, assumes index in [-3, 3]. "inverse" = 1/(1+|index|), smooth decay (experimental). "sigmoid" = S-curve transition (experimental).

Blend two forecast signals with dynamic weights driven by a regime signal for adaptive allocation.

    Use to smoothly shift allocation between two strategies based on a regime indicator

    regime signal. For binary gating instead of blending, use RegimeGate.

    Output:
        Same scale as input forecasts (weighted blend). NaN during regime signal
        burn-in (~20 bars). Weight for signal_a is in [min_weight_a, max_weight_a].

    Formula:
        z = expanding_zscore(regime_signal)
        w_a = sigmoid(regime_coeff * z) * (max_w_a - min_w_a) + min_w_a
        w_b = 1 - w_a
        blended = w_a * signal_a + w_b * signal_b

    Example:
        Parallel(mr=..., momentum=..., regime=...) -> RegimeWeightedBlender(signal_a_key="mr", signal_b_key="momentum", regime_key="regime")

Unlike DispersionWeightedCombinator which uses return dispersion, this uses an arbitrary regime signal. For binary gating instead of blending, use RegimeGate.

components.name: RelativeVolume
components.description:
Compute relative volume as a ratio of current volume to its rolling average.

    Use to identify unusual volume activity — values above 1.0 indicate higher-than-normal
    participation, below 1.0 indicates quiet trading. Feed into CrossSectionalZScore for
    cross-sectional comparison or use as a filter dimension (Z axis in screening).

    Output:
        Range [0, inf). 1.0 = average volume, 2.0 = double average, 0.5 = half.
        NaN during warmup (lookback bars).

    Formula:
        rel_vol = volume_t / SMA(volume, lookback)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> RelativeVolume(lookback=20) -> CrossSectionalZScore()

components.name: ResidualMomentum
components.description:
Cross-sectional residual momentum after stripping out N named benchmark factors.

    For each bar t, fits OLS of each asset's returns on benchmark returns over a
    strictly past window [t-bw, t-1] (EXCLUDES bar t). Applies that β to compute
    a strict out-of-sample residual ε_t at bar t, then cumulates ε over
    `momentum_window` bars to produce a momentum-style signal that is orthogonal
    to the named benchmarks.

    Use as the signal source for cross-sectional residual-momentum strategies on
    a universe with one or more obvious common factors (e.g., crypto with
    BTC/ETH/SOL representing the systematic component). The output ranks alts by
    their idiosyncratic outperformance, NOT by their direction relative to the
    market — by construction, MOM weights on the benchmarks themselves are
    forced to zero (their residual against themselves is degenerate).

    Output:
        SignalSeries (T × A) of cumulative residuals over the past
        `momentum_window` bars. Positive = outperformed its β-implied move.
        Benchmark columns are exactly zero. NaN during warmup (β fit needs at
        least max(N+5, beta_window/2) past observations; cumulation needs at
        least momentum_window/2 valid residuals).

    Causality:
        β-fit window [t-bw, t-1] is STRICTLY past — does not include bar t.
        Residual at bar t uses bar-t benchmark and asset returns (both
        observable at close[t] under right-labeled bars). Cumulation uses
        rolling window [t-W+1, t] — past-only ending at t.

        Verified bit-exact against hand-OLS in research (Phase III.2 audit
        diff = 0.00e+00). Causality also independently confirmed by end-date
        stability test (Phase III.1) — 0 differences across 80K+ cells when
        re-run with three different end dates.

    Performance:
        Implementation batches `np.linalg.lstsq` across "fully-valid" assets
        per bar (one OLS solve per bar instead of one per (bar, asset) pair),
        with a per-asset fallback for partial-NaN columns. ~11× faster than
        the naive per-asset loop on a 600-bar / 50-asset universe (~0.04s per
        call vs ~0.45s). Bit-equivalent to the per-asset path within machine
        epsilon (max diff observed 4.44e-16).

    Slot pattern:
        Source-like component: ignores upstream `current` and reads OHLCV
        from a named slot. `input_type` is overridden to `None` so the type
        validator skips the upstream-shape check.

    Example:
        ResidualMomentum(momentum_window=21, beta_window=90, benchmarks=["BTC", "ETH", "SOL"])
        -> VolAttenuator(return_vol_slot="return_vol")
        -> CrossSectionalZScore()
components.usage_hint: For each bar t, fits OLS of each asset's returns on benchmark returns over a strictly past window [t-bw, t-1] (EXCLUDES bar t). Applies that β to compute a strict out-of-sample residual ε_t at bar t, then cumulates ε over `momentum_window` bars to produce a momentum-style signal that is orthogonal to the named benchmarks.

parameters.description: Bars to cumulate residuals over after the OLS fit. Typical choices for daily bars: {14, 21, 63, 126} as a multi-leaf bucket. Shorter = faster reaction, more turnover. Longer = smoother signal, slower.

parameters.description: Rolling window (bars) for the OLS β fit. Default 60. For crypto on daily bars, 90 gives more stable β at the cost of slower regime adaptation. Strictly past — fit window is [t-bw, t-1].

parameters.name: benchmarks

components.name: ReturnVolatility
components.description:
Calculate EWMA volatility of percentage returns — per-asset vol indicator.

    Indicator-class component (OHLCVDict → SignalSeries): produces per-asset return
    volatility estimates for use by downstream position sizers, vol attenuators, and
    risk overlays. Not itself a position sizer — it's the input that vol-targeted
    sizers consume.

    Unlike EWMAInstrumentValueVolatility (which multiplied by price to get dollar vol),
    this outputs raw return volatility (dimensionless). This is the correct input for
    VolTargetWeightConverter, which converts forecasts directly to weights without the
    intermediate dollar-space detour.

    Output:
        SignalSeries (DataFrame, timestamps x symbols). Values are per-bar return
        volatility (dimensionless, e.g. 0.02 = 2% per bar). NaN during burn-in
        period (first burn_in * window bars, default 1x) and where volatility
        is near-zero.

    Formula:
        returns = pct_change(close) or diff(ln(close))
        sigma = EWMA_std(returns, span=window)

    Example (in a Parallel branch after TargetTimeframeResampler):
        ReturnVolatility(window="36d") -> Store("return_vol")
components.usage_hint: Indicator-class component (OHLCVDict → SignalSeries): produces per-asset return volatility estimates for use by downstream position sizers, vol attenuators, and risk overlays. Not itself a position sizer — it's the input that vol-targeted sizers consume.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: EWMA span for volatility estimation. "36d" (default) = ~25-day equivalent moving average. Accepts time strings ("36d", "60d") or integer bar counts. Shorter = more responsive, longer = smoother.

parameters.name: returns
parameters.description: Return calculation method. "pct" (default) = percentage returns, "log" = log returns. Both give similar results for small returns; "log" is slightly more accurate for large moves.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations for valid volatility. None (default) = use pandas EWMA default. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) output.

parameters.name: burn_in
parameters.description: Multiplier on window for burn-in period. During burn-in, EWMA output is set to NaN because the estimate hasn't accumulated enough weight to be stable. After 1x span, ~63% of effective weight is accumulated (1 - 1/e). Default 1.0. Set to 0 to disable burn-in entirely (EWMA min_periods still applies).

changelog.1: Initial release — unitless return vol Indicator (replaces EWMAInstrumentValueVolatility); Indicator base class corrects prior PositionSizer mis-categorization

components.name: RisingSignalFilter
components.description:
Filter for assets where the signal is rising over a lookback period to detect upward momentum.

    Use to identify assets where an indicator is increasing (e.g., RSI rising from
    oversold, momentum accelerating). Produces a boolean mask. Complement to
    FallingSignalFilter.

    Output:
        DataFrame of 0.0/1.0 float mask. 1.0 where current value > value from
        `periods` bars ago. NaN during warmup (first `periods` bars).

    Example:
        RSI(period=14) -> RisingSignalFilter(periods=1) -> AndCombinator(...)

components.name: RiskBudgetSizer
components.description:
Size each position to target a fixed volatility contribution.

    Each active position is sized so its expected daily return volatility
    equals risk_per_position of portfolio capital. High-vol assets get
    smaller notional positions, low-vol assets get larger ones — but each
    contributes the same risk.

    Unlike VolWeightSizer (which distributes a total leverage target
    inversely by vol), RiskBudgetSizer sets an absolute risk budget per
    position. Total leverage floats with position count and volatility.
    Use LeverageCap downstream for a hard leverage limit.

    Formula:
        weight_i = risk_per_position / vol_i

    Output:
        WeightSeries. Direction preserved. A position with 3% daily vol
        and risk_per_position=0.02 gets weight = 0.02/0.03 = 0.67.

    Example:
        ReturnVolatility() → Store('vol'),
        ...
        PositionStateMachine(...) → RiskBudgetSizer(vol_slot='vol', risk_per_position=0.02)
        → LeverageCap(max_leverage=2.0)
components.usage_hint: Each active position is sized so its expected daily return volatility equals risk_per_position of portfolio capital. High-vol assets get smaller notional positions, low-vol assets get larger ones — but each contributes the same risk.

components.name: RiskParityAllocator
components.description:
Allocate inverse-volatility weights for risk parity across instruments.

    Use when assets have meaningfully different volatility profiles and you want
    each instrument to contribute roughly equal risk. Reads volatility estimates
    from a named slot (typically the output of ReturnVolatility stored
    via Store). Placed after VolTargetWeightConverter and before IDMPortfolioAggregator.

    Output:
        Weighted blocks (DataFrame, same shape as input). Each instrument's blocks
        are multiplied by its inverse-volatility weight. Weights sum to 1.0.
        Lower-volatility assets get higher weights.

    Formula:
        w_i = (1 / sigma_i) / sum(1 / sigma_j)
        weighted_blocks[asset] = subsystem_blocks[asset] * w_i

    Example:
        VolTargetWeightConverter() -> RiskParityAllocator(volatility_slot="return_vol") -> IDMPortfolioAggregator(...)
components.usage_hint: Use when assets have meaningfully different volatility profiles and you want each instrument to contribute roughly equal risk. Reads volatility estimates from a named slot (typically the output of ReturnVolatility stored via Store). Placed after VolTargetWeightConverter and before IDMPortfolioAggregator.

components.name: RobustZScore
components.description:
Normalize signals using rolling median and MAD for outlier-resistant z-score standardization.

    Use instead of RollingZScoreTransform when the signal has heavy tails or frequent
    outliers that would distort mean/std estimates. The median-based approach is more
    robust to extreme values. Note: uses raw MAD (no 1.4826 scaling), so output
    magnitudes are ~1.48x larger than standard z-scores -- recalibrate downstream
    thresholds when switching from RollingZScoreTransform.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 0. Positive = above rolling median, negative = below.
        NaN during warmup and where MAD is zero (constant signal).

    Formula:
        z = (x - rolling_median(x, window)) / rolling_MAD(x, window)
        where MAD = median(|x - median(x)|), unscaled

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> RobustZScore(window=60) -> WingsTransform(threshold=1.5)
components.usage_hint: Use instead of RollingZScoreTransform when the signal has heavy tails or frequent outliers that would distort mean/std estimates. The median-based approach is more robust to extreme values. Note: uses raw MAD (no 1.4826 scaling), so output magnitudes are ~1.48x larger than standard z-scores -- recalibrate downstream thresholds when switching from RollingZScoreTransform.

parameters.description: Lookback period in bars for rolling median and MAD. Shorter (10-20) adapts quickly, longer (40-90) gives more stable robust estimates. Default 20.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations before producing output. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) output.

components.name: RollingAutocorrelation
components.description:
Compute rolling autocorrelation at a specified lag to detect trending or mean-reverting regimes.

    Use as a regime signal to measure serial dependence in returns or signals. Positive
    autocorrelation suggests trending behavior (momentum), negative suggests mean reversion.
    Commonly used to dynamically blend momentum and mean-reversion strategies. Place before
    a SigmoidNormalize or threshold to classify regime state.

    Output:
        Range [-1, 1]. Positive = trending/persistent, negative = mean-reverting.
        Zero = no serial dependence (random walk). NaN during warmup and when
        either sub-window has zero variance.

    Formula:
        output = corr(x[t-window:t-lag], x[t-window+lag:t]) per asset

    Example:
        ROC(period=4) -> RollingAutocorrelation(window=60, lag=1) -> SigmoidNormalize()
components.usage_hint: Use as a regime signal to measure serial dependence in returns or signals. Positive autocorrelation suggests trending behavior (momentum), negative suggests mean reversion. Commonly used to dynamically blend momentum and mean-reversion strategies. Place before a SigmoidNormalize or threshold to classify regime state.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Lookback period in bars for the correlation window. Shorter (10-20) captures recent regime, longer (40-90) measures structural persistence. Default 20. Must be > lag.

parameters.name: lag
parameters.description: Number of bars offset for autocorrelation. lag=1 (default) measures one-bar serial dependence. Higher lags (2-5) detect longer-range persistence patterns. Effective lookback = window + lag.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations required. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) output.

components.name: RollingBeta
components.description:
Compute rolling OLS beta of each asset versus a benchmark for market sensitivity measurement.

    Use to measure how each asset co-moves with a benchmark or the equal-weighted
    universe average. Beta > 1 means amplified market moves, beta < 1 means dampened.
    Useful for beta-neutral portfolio construction, risk management, or as a regime
    signal. When no benchmark_col is specified, uses the equal-weighted average of
    all other assets.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 1 for market-tracking assets. Beta > 1 = amplified
        moves, beta < 1 = dampened, negative = inverse. NaN during warmup and when
        benchmark variance is zero.

    Formula:
        beta = cov(asset, benchmark, window) / var(benchmark, window)

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> RollingBeta(window=60, benchmark_col="BTC") -> MinMaxNormalize()
components.usage_hint: Use to measure how each asset co-moves with a benchmark or the equal-weighted universe average. Beta > 1 means amplified market moves, beta < 1 means dampened. Useful for beta-neutral portfolio construction, risk management, or as a regime signal. When no benchmark_col is specified, uses the equal-weighted average of all other assets.

parameters.description: Column name to use as benchmark. None (default) uses the equal-weighted average of all other assets as the benchmark. Set to a specific asset (e.g., "BTC") for single-benchmark beta.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations required. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) output.

components.name: RollingCorrelation
components.description:
Compute mean pairwise rolling correlation per asset to measure co-movement and diversification potential.

    Use to detect periods of high correlation (herding/risk-on) vs low correlation
    (dispersion/stock-picking opportunity). Each asset gets the average of its pairwise
    correlations with all other assets. Useful as a regime signal for portfolio
    construction -- high mean correlation suggests reducing position count.

    Output:
        Range [-1, 1]. High positive = assets moving together, near zero = independent.
        NaN during warmup, or when only one asset is present. One value per asset
        representing its average co-movement with the rest of the universe.

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> RollingCorrelation(window=60) -> SigmoidNormalize(center=0.5)

components.name: RollingKurtosis
components.description:
Compute rolling excess kurtosis to measure tail heaviness and detect fat-tailed regimes.

    Use to identify periods of unusually heavy or light tails relative to a normal
    distribution. High kurtosis signals elevated tail risk (large moves more likely).
    Often paired with RollingSkewness as a joint regime indicator. Place before a
    threshold filter or regime gate to reduce exposure during fat-tailed periods.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 0 (excess kurtosis, so normal distribution = 0).
        Positive = fat tails (leptokurtic), negative = thin tails (platykurtic).
        Typical range [-1, 10] for crypto on 15-min bars. NaN during warmup.

    Example:
        RollingKurtosis(window=60) -> MinMaxNormalize(window=120) -> ThresholdCross(threshold=0.8)
components.usage_hint: Use to identify periods of unusually heavy or light tails relative to a normal distribution. High kurtosis signals elevated tail risk (large moves more likely). Often paired with RollingSkewness as a joint regime indicator. Place before a threshold filter or regime gate to reduce exposure during fat-tailed periods.

Compute rolling percentile rank to measure where a value sits in its own recent history.

    Use for regime detection or conditional filtering based on historical context

components.name: RollingSkewness
components.description:
Compute rolling skewness to detect asymmetric return distributions.

    Use as a regime or filter signal to identify periods where returns are
    skewed (e.g., persistent negative skew may indicate crash risk). Often
    combined with a threshold to gate other signals during extreme skew periods.
    Can also serve as a standalone mean-reversion signal when skew reverts to zero.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 0. Negative = left-skewed (tail risk),
        positive = right-skewed (upside bias). Typical range [-2, 2] for
        liquid crypto assets on 15-min bars. NaN during warmup.

    Example:
        RollingSkewness(window=60) -> SigmoidNormalize(center=0, scale=1.0)
components.usage_hint: Use as a regime or filter signal to identify periods where returns are skewed (e.g., persistent negative skew may indicate crash risk). Often combined with a threshold to gate other signals during extreme skew periods. Can also serve as a standalone mean-reversion signal when skew reverts to zero.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Lookback period in bars. Shorter (10-20) captures recent distribution shape, longer (60-120) measures structural skew. Must be >= 8 for meaningful skewness estimation. Default 20.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations required. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) output.

components.name: RollingVolumeUniverseMask
components.description:
Generate a rolling binary mask (1.0/NaN) from notional volume ranking that changes at rebalance_freq.

    Use as an early pipeline step (after Store('ohlcv_1d')) to define which assets
    are tradable at each point in time. Unlike VolumeUniverseReducer which permanently
    drops columns, this preserves all columns and uses NaN to exclude assets. The mask
    changes only at rebalance boundaries (e.g., weekly) for stability.

    Supports hysteresis via entry_buffer/exit_buffer to reduce turnover: assets must
    rank in top_n - entry_buffer to enter, and only exit when they fall below
    top_n + exit_buffer.

    Output:
        SignalSeries DataFrame (timestamps × assets) with values 1.0 (active) or
        NaN (excluded). Forward-filled between rebalance dates.

    Example:
        Store('ohlcv_1d') -> RollingVolumeUniverseMask(top_n=50, rebalance_freq='7d')
        -> Store('universe_mask')
components.usage_hint: Use as an early pipeline step (after Store('ohlcv_1d')) to define which assets are tradable at each point in time. Unlike VolumeUniverseReducer which permanently drops columns, this preserves all columns and uses NaN to exclude assets. The mask changes only at rebalance boundaries (e.g., weekly) for stability.

parameters.name: top_n
parameters.description: Number of most-liquid assets to include. Default 50.

parameters.name: rebalance_freq
parameters.description: How often to re-rank assets. Parsed as bar count via the data index (e.g., "7d" = 7 calendar days of bars). Default "7d".

parameters.name: lookback_bars
parameters.description: Number of bars to average notional volume over for ranking. Longer = more stable, shorter = more responsive. Default 60.

parameters.name: entry_buffer
parameters.description: Assets must rank in top_n - entry_buffer to enter the universe (more selective entry). Default 0.

parameters.name: exit_buffer
parameters.description: Assets exit only when ranking below top_n + exit_buffer (more lenient exit, reduces turnover). Default 0.

components.name: RollingZScoreTransform
components.description:
Normalize signals by time-series z-score relative to each asset's own rolling history.

    Use when absolute deviation from an asset's historical norm matters more than
    cross-asset ranking. Placed after an indicator (RSI, ROC, ATR%) and before a
    threshold or forecast mapper. Prefer CrossSectionalZScore when relative ranking
    across assets matters, or RobustZScore when outliers distort mean/std estimates.

    Output:
        Unbounded, centered around 0. Positive = above rolling mean, negative = below.
        NaN during warmup (min_periods bars) and where rolling std is near-zero.

    Formula:
        z = (x - rolling_mean(x, window)) / rolling_std(x, window)
        where x is the input signal per asset

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> RollingZScoreTransform(window=60) -> WingsTransform(threshold=1.0)
components.usage_hint: Use when absolute deviation from an asset's historical norm matters more than cross-asset ranking. Placed after an indicator (RSI, ROC, ATR%) and before a threshold or forecast mapper. Prefer CrossSectionalZScore when relative ranking across assets matters, or RobustZScore when outliers distort mean/std estimates.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Lookback period in bars for rolling mean and std. Shorter (20-40) adapts faster to regime changes, longer (60-120) gives smoother normalization. Default 90 suits daily-scale patterns on 15-min bars.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations before producing output. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) output.

components.name: ScalingPositionManager
components.description:
Manage multi-level positions for pyramiding, DCA, and laddered entries.

    Unlike PositionStateMachine (binary {-1, 0, +1}), this component outputs
    integer position levels {-N, ..., 0, ..., +N} where N = max_entries.
    Sign = direction, magnitude = number of active entry levels.

    Two entry modes:
    - **Single slot**: Each 0→non-zero transition in the entry signal increments
      the level (up to max_entries). Use for DCA / simple pyramiding.
    - **Multiple slots**: Each slot independently controls one level. Level =
      count of active (non-zero) entry slots. Use for laddered entries with
      different trigger conditions per level.

    Two exit modes:
    - **'all'** (default): Any exit signal closes the entire position (level → 0).
    - **'one_level'**: Each exit fire decrements level by 1. Supports partial exits.

    Output:
        SignalSeries with integer values. FixedWeightSizer with weight_per_position=0.1
        and level=3 produces weight=0.3. EqualWeightSizer counts total levels across
        assets for proportional weighting.

    Example (laddered entries):
        ScalingPositionManager(
            entry_slots=['entry_1', 'entry_2', 'entry_3'],
            exit_slots='exits',
        )

    Example (simple DCA):
        ScalingPositionManager(entry_slots='entries', exit_slots='exits', max_entries=5)
components.usage_hint: Unlike PositionStateMachine (binary {-1, 0, +1}), this component outputs integer position levels {-N, ..., 0, ..., +N} where N = max_entries. Sign = direction, magnitude = number of active entry levels.

parameters.name: entry_slots
parameters.description: Single slot name (DCA/pyramiding mode) or list of slot names (laddered entry mode). In multi-slot mode, max_entries is implicitly len(entry_slots).

parameters.name: exit_slots
parameters.description: Single slot name or list of slot names for exit signals. In 'one_level' mode with multiple exit slots, each fires independently to decrement one level.

parameters.name: max_entries
parameters.description: Maximum position levels (single-slot mode only). Ignored in multi-slot mode where max = len(entry_slots).

parameters.name: exit_mode
parameters.description: 'all' closes entire position on any exit. 'one_level' decrements by 1 per exit fire.

changelog.1: Initial release — pyramiding, DCA, laddered entries

components.name: SelectionToSignalConverter
components.description:
Convert binary asset selection masks into entry/exit trading signals with configurable hold periods.

    Use after a universe filter or ranking component that produces a binary selection mask
    (1 = selected, 0 = not selected). Produces entry (+1) and exit (-1) signals suitable
    for downstream weight allocation (FixedWeightAllocator, DynamicEqualWeightAllocator).
    Each new selection resets the hold countdown, and exits fire only after hold_periods
    consecutive bars without selection.

    Output:
        Integer values: +1 (entry), -1 (exit), 0 (hold/no signal). Every selected bar
        produces an entry. Exit fires when countdown reaches 0 after hold_periods bars
        of no selections.

    Example:
        UniverseFilter() -> SelectionToSignalConverter(hold_periods=5) -> DynamicEqualWeightAllocator()
components.usage_hint: Use after a universe filter or ranking component that produces a binary selection mask (1 = selected, 0 = not selected). Produces entry (+1) and exit (-1) signals suitable for downstream weight allocation (FixedWeightAllocator, DynamicEqualWeightAllocator). Each new selection resets the hold countdown, and exits fire only after hold_periods consecutive bars without selection.

parameters.name: hold_periods
parameters.description: Number of bars to hold a position after the last selection before generating an exit signal. Higher values = longer holds, fewer trades, lower turnover. Typical range 3-10. Must be >= 1.

components.name: SelectiveCombinator
components.description:
Extract a single named signal from a Parallel output dict for downstream processing.

    Use when a Parallel step produces multiple signals but you only need one for the
    next pipeline stage. Acts as a simple selector/passthrough. For combining multiple
    signals, use AndCombinator, OrCombinator, or ForecastCombiner instead.

    Output:
        The selected DataFrame, unchanged. Same type and shape as the original
        signal in the dict.

    Example:
        Parallel(roc_z=..., momentum=...) -> SelectiveCombinator("roc_z") -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use when a Parallel step produces multiple signals but you only need one for the next pipeline stage. Acts as a simple selector/passthrough. For combining multiple signals, use AndCombinator, OrCombinator, or ForecastCombiner instead.

components.name: SigmoidNormalize
components.description:
Compress signals into (0, 1) range using a sigmoid function for bounded normalization.

    Use when you need a stateless, non-rolling bounded transform. Unlike MinMaxNormalize,
    sigmoid has no lookback window and never produces NaN from warmup. Good for mapping
    unbounded z-scores or raw indicators into a probability-like range. Adjust center and
    scale to control where the transition zone sits.

    Output:
        Range (0, 1), never exactly 0 or 1. 0.5 when input equals center.
        Values above center map toward 1, below toward 0. No NaN from the transform itself.

    Formula:
        output = 1 / (1 + exp(-(x - center) / scale))

    Example:
        RollingZScoreTransform(window=60) -> SigmoidNormalize(center=0.0, scale=1.5)
components.usage_hint: Use when you need a stateless, non-rolling bounded transform. Unlike MinMaxNormalize, sigmoid has no lookback window and never produces NaN from warmup. Good for mapping unbounded z-scores or raw indicators into a probability-like range. Adjust center and scale to control where the transition zone sits.

parameters.name: center
parameters.description: Input value that maps to 0.5 output. Shift this to match the natural midpoint of your signal. Default 0.0 suits z-scored inputs.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Controls steepness of the sigmoid transition. Smaller values (0.3-0.5) create a sharper step-like response, larger values (2.0-5.0) create a gentler curve. Must be non-zero. Default 1.0.

components.name: SignalROC
components.description:
Compute rate of change on any signal series for measuring signal acceleration or slope.

    Use after any indicator or signal to measure its rate of change. Unlike ROC (which
    operates on OHLCV data and extracts close), SignalROC operates on any signal DataFrame.
    Common use: MACD -> SignalROC(period=3) gives MACD histogram slope.

    Output:
        Percentage change over the lookback period. Unbounded, centered around 0.
        Positive = signal increasing, negative = signal decreasing.
        NaN for the first `period` bars.

    Formula:
        signal_roc = (signal_t - signal_{t-period}) / |signal_{t-period}| * 100
        (uses absolute value in denominator to preserve sign information)

    Example:
        MACD(12, 26, 9) -> SignalROC(period=3) -> CrossSectionalZScore()
        RSI(14) -> SignalROC(period=5) -> CrossSectionalZScore()
components.usage_hint: Use after any indicator or signal to measure its rate of change. Unlike ROC (which operates on OHLCV data and extracts close), SignalROC operates on any signal DataFrame. Common use: MACD -> SignalROC(period=3) gives MACD histogram slope.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback period in bars for rate of change. Shorter (1-3) captures rapid signal changes, longer (5-10) smooths noise. Default 3 suits MACD slope measurement on hourly bars.

components.name: SignalResampler
components.description:
Downsample signals to a lower timeframe by aggregation for multi-timeframe strategy construction.

    Use to convert higher-frequency signals (e.g., 15-min, 1h) to lower-frequency
    (e.g., 4h, 1d) for multi-timeframe analysis. Place after any signal producer when
    the downstream pipeline operates on a different timeframe. Prefer
    SignalTimeframeConverter for upsampling.

    Output:
        Same scale as input, aggregated to target timeframe. Output index aligns
        to the target frequency. NaN where insufficient data exists for aggregation.

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> SignalResampler(target_timeframe="4h", method="mean")
components.usage_hint: Use to convert higher-frequency signals (e.g., 15-min, 1h) to lower-frequency (e.g., 4h, 1d) for multi-timeframe analysis. Place after any signal producer when the downstream pipeline operates on a different timeframe. Prefer SignalTimeframeConverter for upsampling.

parameters.name: target_timeframe
parameters.description: Target timeframe string (e.g., "1d", "4h", "1h"). Must be lower frequency than input. Defaults to "1d".

parameters.name: method
parameters.description: Aggregation function for downsampling. "mean" (default) = average, "sum" = total, "last" = end-of-period value, "first" = start-of-period, "median" = median. Use "last" for point-in-time signals, "mean" for averaged indicators.

components.name: SignalReversionExit
components.description:
Exit when a normalized signal reverts to a neutral zone.

    Use after the same normalization chain as entry signals to detect
    when the extreme condition that triggered entry has normalized.
    Outputs scalar exit (1.0) — direction-agnostic, works with both
    long and short positions via PSM(exit_mode='scalar').

    Unlike ThresholdCross (which fires when signal is OUTSIDE thresholds),
    this fires when signal is INSIDE the neutral zone — the inverse logic
    needed for exits.

    Output:
        1.0 when abs(signal) <= exit_threshold (signal reverted to neutral)
        0.0 when abs(signal) > exit_threshold (signal still extreme)

    Example:
        # Entry at ±2 sigma, exit when signal returns within ±0.5
        # Use in a Parallel block branching from the same normalized signal:
        {
            "entry": [ThresholdCross(upper=2, lower=-2), Store('entries')],
            "exit":  [SignalReversionExit(exit_threshold=0.5), Store('exits')],
        }
components.usage_hint: Use after the same normalization chain as entry signals to detect when the extreme condition that triggered entry has normalized. Outputs scalar exit (1.0) — direction-agnostic, works with both long and short positions via PSM(exit_mode='scalar').

parameters.name: exit_threshold
parameters.description: Absolute signal level at or below which the signal is considered "reverted." 0.5 = exit when |signal| <= 0.5. Lower values (0.1-0.3) wait for stronger reversion. Higher values (0.7-1.0) exit sooner. 0.0 = exit only when signal crosses exactly zero.

parameters.description: Absolute signal level at or below which the signal is considered "reverted." 0.5 = exit when |signal| <= 0.5. Lower values (0.1-0.3) wait for stronger reversion. Higher values (0.7-1.0) exit sooner. 0.0 = exit only when signal crosses exactly zero.

components.name: SignalTimeframeConverter
components.description:
Convert signals between timeframes with forward-fill or first-only placement for multi-timeframe alignment.

    Use when a lower-frequency signal (e.g., 4h regime) needs to drive decisions at
    a higher-frequency execution timeframe (e.g., 15-min). Unlike SignalResampler
    which downsamples, this component handles both up- and cross-sampling with no
    lookahead bias. Place after any signal producer before combining with signals at
    the target timeframe.

    Output:
        Same scale as input, reindexed to target timeframe. With fill_all, values
        are forward-filled across target bars. With first_only, only the first
        target bar gets the value; others are NaN (hold semantics).

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> SignalTimeframeConverter(target_timeframe="15min", method="fill_all")
components.usage_hint: Use when a lower-frequency signal (e.g., 4h regime) needs to drive decisions at a higher-frequency execution timeframe (e.g., 15-min). Unlike SignalResampler which downsamples, this component handles both up- and cross-sampling with no lookahead bias. Place after any signal producer before combining with signals at the target timeframe.

parameters.name: target_timeframe
parameters.description: Target timeframe string (e.g., "15min", "1h", "4h"). Determines the output index frequency.

parameters.name: method
parameters.description: How to place signal values on the target index. "fill_all" (default) = forward-fill, every target bar gets the latest signal (safe, no lookahead). "first_only" = signal only in the first target bar, NaN elsewhere (most conservative, for VBT sparse weight semantics where NaN = hold position).

components.name: SignalToExit
components.description:
Convert entry signals to exit semantics by flipping polarity for composable exit pipelines.

    Use at the end of an exit signal branch to convert entry-style signals (1 = active)
    into exit signals (-1 = exit). This lets you build exit conditions using the same
    indicator and filter components as entry signals, then convert at the end. Typically
    the last step in a Parallel branch before SignalCombiner merges entry and exit branches.

    Output:
        Range {-1, 0}. Entry values (1) become -1 (exit), all other values become 0.
        When preserve_strength=True, positive values are negated proportionally
        (e.g., 0.8 becomes -0.8).

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> QuantileRangeSelector(low_quantile=30, high_quantile=40) -> SignalToExit()
components.usage_hint: Use at the end of an exit signal branch to convert entry-style signals (1 = active) into exit signals (-1 = exit). This lets you build exit conditions using the same indicator and filter components as entry signals, then convert at the end. Typically the last step in a Parallel branch before SignalCombiner merges entry and exit branches.

parameters.name: preserve_strength
parameters.description: If True, negate positive values proportionally (0.8 -> -0.8) to preserve signal conviction in exit strength. If False (default), normalize all entries to standard -1 exit signal.

components.name: SimpleReturn
components.description:
Compute simple percentage return over N periods for momentum or rate-of-change analysis.

    Use to convert price levels into returns before feeding into normalization or
    scoring components. Prefer SimpleReturn over LogReturn when percentage changes
    are small (<10%) and interpretability matters. For larger moves or compounding
    analysis, use LogReturn instead.

    Output:
        Unbounded percentage change. Positive = price increase, negative = decrease.
        First N rows are NaN (warmup). Infinite values (from zero prices) become NaN.

    Formula:
        return = (x_t - x_{t-n}) / x_{t-n}

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> SimpleReturn(periods=4) -> CrossSectionalZScore()
components.usage_hint: Use to convert price levels into returns before feeding into normalization or scoring components. Prefer SimpleReturn over LogReturn when percentage changes are small (<10%) and interpretability matters. For larger moves or compounding analysis, use LogReturn instead.

parameters.name: periods
parameters.description: Lookback in bars for return calculation. 1 = single-bar return, 4 = 4-bar return (1h on 15min bars). Longer periods capture slower momentum. Typical range 1-20.

components.name: SpeedWeightMapper
components.description:
Map regime mode and stress signals to dynamic fast/slow trend-following weight allocations.

    Use to shift between fast and slow trend strategies based on market conditions.
    In risky regimes (high mode + stress), shifts weight toward slow trend-following
    for stability. In calm regimes, favors fast trend for responsiveness. Outputs
    a DataFrame with w_fast and w_slow columns for downstream blending.

    Output:
        DataFrame with columns [w_fast, w_slow]. Both in [min_weight, max_weight],
        summing to 1.0 at each timestamp. NaN during regime burn-in.

    Formula:
        z = mode_coeff * mode + stress_coeff * stress
        w_fast = sigmoid(-z) * (max_w - min_w) + min_w
        w_slow = 1 - w_fast
        Smooth (EWMA half_life) then clip.

    Example:
        Parallel(mode=ModeIndex(...), stress=StressIndex(...)) -> SpeedWeightMapper(mode_coeff=0.7, stress_coeff=0.3)
components.usage_hint: Use to shift between fast and slow trend strategies based on market conditions. In risky regimes (high mode + stress), shifts weight toward slow trend-following for stability. In calm regimes, favors fast trend for responsiveness. Outputs a DataFrame with w_fast and w_slow columns for downstream blending.

components.name: Stochastic
components.description:
Compute Stochastic Oscillator slow %K, a bounded momentum indicator comparing close to the high-low range.

    Use for overbought/oversold detection similar to RSI but with built-in smoothing via the
    slow %K and %D periods. Requires high/low/close data. The double-smoothing (fastk ->
    slowk via slowk_period, then slowd via d_period) reduces noise compared to raw stochastic.
    Output is the slow %K line; the %D line is computed internally but not returned. Prefer
    Stochastic over WilliamsR when you want built-in smoothing of the oscillator.

    Output:
        Range [0, 100]. Values > 80 = overbought (potential short),
        < 20 = oversold (potential long). NaN during warmup.

    Formula:
        fast_%K = (close - lowest_low(k_period)) / (highest_high(k_period) - lowest_low(k_period)) * 100
        slow_%K = SMA(fast_%K, slowk_period)
        slow_%D = SMA(slow_%K, d_period)  (computed but not output)

    Example:
        Stochastic(k_period=14, slowk_period=3) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use for overbought/oversold detection similar to RSI but with built-in smoothing via the slow %K and %D periods. Requires high/low/close data. The double-smoothing (fastk -> slowk via slowk_period, then slowd via d_period) reduces noise compared to raw stochastic. Output is the slow %K line; the %D line is computed internally but not returned. Prefer Stochastic over WilliamsR when you want built-in smoothing of the oscillator.

parameters.name: k_period
parameters.description: Lookback for the raw %K calculation (highest-high/lowest-low window). Shorter (5-9) = more responsive, longer (14-21) = smoother. Default 14 is the standard Stochastic period.

parameters.name: d_period
parameters.description: Smoothing period for the slow %D line (SMA of slow %K). Typically 3. Not output directly but affects internal computation.

parameters.name: slowk_period
parameters.description: Smoothing period applied to fast %K to produce slow %K. Typically 3. Higher values (5-7) further reduce whipsaw. Must be >= 1.

components.name: SuperTrend
components.description:
Compute SuperTrend direction signal using ATR-based adaptive bands with band locking to reduce whipsaw.

    Use as a trend direction filter or primary trend signal. The band-locking mechanism
    (upper band can only decrease, lower band can only increase during their respective
    trends) prevents premature direction changes. Requires high/low/close data. Typically
    used as a regime gate to enable/disable other signals, or fed through ForecastScaler
    for direct trend-following. Prefer SuperTrend over simple moving average crossovers
    when you want volatility-adaptive trend detection.

    Output:
        Discrete values: +1.0 (uptrend) or -1.0 (downtrend). Changes only when
        price decisively breaks through the opposite band (strict inequality).
        NaN during ATR warmup (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        ATR = talib.ATR(high, low, close, period)
        basic_upper = (high + low) / 2 + multiplier * ATR
        basic_lower = (high + low) / 2 - multiplier * ATR
        Bands lock (tighten only) when price stays within; direction flips on break.

    Example:
        SuperTrend(period=10, multiplier=3.0) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use as a trend direction filter or primary trend signal. The band-locking mechanism (upper band can only decrease, lower band can only increase during their respective trends) prevents premature direction changes. Requires high/low/close data. Typically used as a regime gate to enable/disable other signals, or fed through ForecastScaler for direct trend-following. Prefer SuperTrend over simple moving average crossovers when you want volatility-adaptive trend detection.

parameters.description: ATR lookback period for volatility estimation. Shorter (7-10) reacts faster to volatility changes, longer (14-20) provides more stable bands. Default 10.

parameters.name: multiplier
parameters.description: ATR multiplier controlling band width. Smaller (1.5-2.0) triggers more frequent direction changes, larger (3.0-4.0) only flips on major moves. Default 3.0. Higher values reduce whipsaw but may miss trend reversals.

components.name: SymbolLoader
components.description:
Discover and load available trading symbols from cache or live database for universe construction.

    Use at the start of a pipeline to determine which symbols are available for trading.
    Supports cache-only mode (fast, offline-safe), live-first with cache fallback, and
    live-only mode. Outputs a list of symbol strings for downstream components like
    PriceDataLoader to consume. Apply symbol_filter to restrict to specific assets or
    max_symbols to limit universe size.

    Output:
        List of symbol strings (e.g., ["BTC", "ETH", "SOL"]). Empty list never
        returned (raises ValueError if no symbols found).

    Example:
        SymbolLoader(exchange="hyperliquid", max_symbols=50) -> PriceDataLoader()

components.name: TEMA
components.description:
Compute Triple Exponential Moving Average (TEMA), a minimal-lag trend-following moving average.

    Use as the fastest-responding moving average variant for trend following. TEMA applies
    a triple-smoothing correction to further reduce lag beyond DEMA. Output is in price
    units. Can overshoot price during sharp reversals due to aggressive lag correction.
    Prefer TEMA over DEMA when minimal lag is critical; use EWMA or DEMA for more
    conservative smoothing with less overshoot risk.

    Output:
        In price units, tracks the smoothed close price. Responds fastest to price
        changes among EMA variants. May temporarily exceed the actual high/low
        during sharp moves. NaN during warmup (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        TEMA = 3*EMA - 3*EMA(EMA) + EMA(EMA(EMA))
        where each EMA uses the same `period`

    Example:
        TEMA(period=15) -> SlotStore("fast_ma")
        EWMA(window=60) -> SlotStore("slow_ma")
components.usage_hint: Use as the fastest-responding moving average variant for trend following. TEMA applies a triple-smoothing correction to further reduce lag beyond DEMA. Output is in price units. Can overshoot price during sharp reversals due to aggressive lag correction. Prefer TEMA over DEMA when minimal lag is critical; use EWMA or DEMA for more conservative smoothing with less overshoot risk.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: EMA span for all three smoothing passes. Shorter (10-20) gives very aggressive trend tracking, longer (30-60) captures macro trend with less overshoot. Default 30. TEMA at period N has roughly one-third the lag of a standard EMA at the same period.

components.name: TakeProfitExit
components.description:
Fixed-percentage take profit that resets per trade, with long and short support.

    Mirror of MaxDrawdownStopLoss — exits when unrealized gain from entry
    exceeds a threshold. Shares the same per-trade state machine.

    Long (+1 entry): gain = (close - entry_price) / entry_price.
        Exit when gain >= profit_threshold.
    Short (-1 entry): gain = (entry_price - close) / entry_price.
        Exit when gain >= profit_threshold.

    Output:
        1.0 = exit triggered, 0.0 = hold.

    Example:
        Store('entry_signals'),
        TakeProfitExit(entry_slot='entry_signals', ohlcv_slot='ohlcv',
                       profit_threshold=0.10),
        Store('exit_signals'),
components.usage_hint: Mirror of MaxDrawdownStopLoss — exits when unrealized gain from entry exceeds a threshold. Shares the same per-trade state machine.

components.name: TargetSignalResampler
components.description:
Resample any signal to the strategy's target timeframe from Globals.

    Use for the standard case where a signal (carry rates, funding, OI, or any
    non-OHLCV series) needs resampling to the strategy's declared target timeframe.
    Reads target_timeframe and bar_offset from Globals via declaration references —
    no explicit timeframe parameter needed. For multi-timeframe strategies that need
    an explicit timeframe independent of Globals, use SignalResampler instead.

    Output:
        Same scale as input, aggregated to target timeframe. Output index aligns
        to the target frequency. NaN where insufficient data exists for aggregation.

    Example:
        Globals(target_timeframe="1d") -> FundingDataLoader() -> TargetSignalResampler(method="mean")

Reads target_timeframe and bar_offset from Globals via declaration references — no explicit timeframe parameter needed. For multi-timeframe strategies that need an explicit timeframe independent of Globals, use SignalResampler instead.

parameters.description: Aggregation function for downsampling. "mean" (default) = average, "sum" = total, "last" = end-of-period value, "first" = start-of-period, "median" = median. Use "last" for point-in-time signals like funding rates, "mean" for averaged indicators.

components.name: ThresholdCross
components.description:
Convert a continuous normalized signal to discrete positions {-1, 0, +1} via threshold crossing.

    Use after any normalization step (CrossSectionalZScore, MinMaxNormalize) to
    produce binary entry/exit decisions for event-driven strategies. Supports
    long-only, short-only, and symmetric modes. For stateless re-evaluation
    every bar — if you need position memory (hold until exit), pipe through
    PositionStateMachine downstream instead.

    Output:
        Discrete values in {-1, 0, +1}. +1 = long, -1 = short, 0 = flat.
        Comparisons are strict (> / <), so signal exactly at threshold
        produces 0 (flat). NaN input produces 0 (flat, not hold).

    Example:
        CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ThresholdCross(upper=0.5, lower=-0.5) -> BinaryToWeight()
components.usage_hint: Use after any normalization step (CrossSectionalZScore, MinMaxNormalize) to produce binary entry/exit decisions for event-driven strategies. Supports long-only, short-only, and symmetric modes. For stateless re-evaluation every bar — if you need position memory (hold until exit), pipe through PositionStateMachine downstream instead.

parameters.description: Signal level above which to go long (+1). Higher values (0.7-1.0) require stronger conviction, reducing trade frequency. Lower values (0.2-0.4) trigger more often. Must be >= lower.

parameters.name: lower
parameters.description: Signal level below which to go short (-1). More negative values (-0.7 to -1.0) require stronger bearish signals. Symmetric with upper for balanced strategies (e.g., upper=0.5, lower=-0.5).

parameters.description: Which thresholds to evaluate. "symmetric" (default) — apply both upper and lower thresholds. signal > upper → +1, signal < lower → -1, between → 0. "long_only" — only apply upper threshold. signal > upper → +1, otherwise → 0. Short signals never generated. "short_only" — only apply lower threshold. signal < lower → -1, otherwise → 0. Long signals never generated.

components.name: TimeBasedExitFilter
components.description:
Generate exit signals after a fixed holding period for time-based position management.

    Use for systematic strategies with fixed holding periods (e.g., rebalance every N bars).
    Shifts entry signals forward by hold_periods bars to produce exit signals.
    Reads entry_signals from a pipeline slot. For momentum-based exits, use
    ValueStopExitFilter instead.

    Output:
        DataFrame with 0.0 (no exit) and 1.0 (exit signal). 1.0 appears exactly
        hold_periods bars after each entry signal.

    Example:
        TimeBasedExitFilter(hold_periods=5, entry_slot="entry_signals")

components.name: TimeSeriesMeanReversionForecast
components.description:
Compute a vol-normalized mean reversion signal that fades recent price moves.

    Use for short-term mean reversion strategies that bet against outsized moves.
    Placed after a PriceDataLoader; feed the output through ForecastScaler and
    ForecastCapper to get a standardized forecast suitable for combination.

    Output:
        Unbounded raw signal. Negative = short (price ripped up), positive = long
        (price puked down). Magnitude reflects how many vol-units the asset moved.
        NaN during warmup (vol_window bars) and where local vol is near-zero.

    Formula:
        R = ln(P_t / P_{t-k})                  # k-bar log return
        sigma = EWMA(r^2, vol_window)^{1/2}    # local vol from single-bar returns
        z = R / (sigma * sqrt(k))               # vol-normalized move
        signal = -z                              # negate to fade the move
        Optional: signal = EWMA(signal, smooth_window)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> TimeSeriesMeanReversionForecast(k=4) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use for short-term mean reversion strategies that bet against outsized moves. Placed after a PriceDataLoader; feed the output through ForecastScaler and ForecastCapper to get a standardized forecast suitable for combination.

parameters.description: Lookback in bars for return calculation (how many bars to fade). Shorter = faster/noisier, longer = smoother/laggier.

parameters.description: Optional EWMA span to smooth the final signal. Small values (2-5) reduce noise, None for no smoothing.

components.name: TopNAssetSelector
components.description:
Select the top N assets by signal strength at each timestamp, producing a dynamic boolean mask.

    Use for concentration-based portfolio construction where you want to trade only
    the strongest (or weakest) assets. Unlike VolumeUniverseReducer which permanently
    drops columns, this creates a per-timestamp mask that can change every bar. Feed
    the output into ApplyMask to gate a trading signal. Common upstream signals:
    momentum (ROC), volume, or composite scores.

    Output:
        Boolean DataFrame (True/False). True for the N highest (or lowest) ranked
        assets at each bar, False for all others. Zero-valued and NaN assets are
        excluded from ranking.

    Example:
        ROC(period=20) -> TopNAssetSelector(n=5, method="largest") -> ApplyMask()

components.name: TrailingStopExit
components.description:
ATR-based trailing stop that resets per trade, with long and short support.

    Reads entry signals and OHLCV data from pipeline slots. For each trade
    (per-asset), tracks a trailing extreme that resets on each new entry:

    Long (+1 entry): trailing_high = max(trailing_high, close) each bar.
        Exit when close < trailing_high - atr_multiplier * ATR.
    Short (-1 entry): trailing_low = min(trailing_low, close) each bar.
        Exit when close > trailing_low + atr_multiplier * ATR.

    The trailing extreme resets to the entry bar's close on each new entry
    signal, preventing stale highs/lows from previous trades affecting
    the current stop level. Different assets can be long and short
    simultaneously — state is tracked independently per asset.

    Output:
        1.0 = exit triggered, 0.0 = hold. Direction-agnostic scalar
        compatible with PSM(exit_mode='scalar'). During ATR warmup
        (first atr_period bars), output is 0.0 (no stop possible).

    Example:
        Store('entry_signals'),
        TrailingStopExit(entry_slot='entry_signals', ohlcv_slot='ohlcv',
                         atr_multiplier=2.5, atr_period=14),
        Store('exit_signals'),

components.name: UltimateOscillator
components.description:
Compute Ultimate Oscillator, a multi-timeframe momentum oscillator that blends three lookback periods to reduce false signals.

    Use when single-period oscillators (RSI, Stochastic) produce too many whipsaws. The
    three-period blending reduces false divergence signals common with single-timeframe
    indicators. Requires high/low/close data. Typically followed by CrossSectionalZScore
    or used as a confirmation filter alongside other momentum indicators. Prefer over RSI
    when you want built-in multi-timeframe smoothing.

    Output:
        Range [0, 100]. Values > 70 = overbought, < 30 = oversold. The multi-period
        construction makes extreme readings more meaningful than single-period oscillators.
        NaN during warmup (max of the three periods).

    Formula:
        BP = close - min(low, prev_close)                    # buying pressure
        TR = max(high, prev_close) - min(low, prev_close)    # true range
        UO = 100 * (4*avg(BP/TR,fast) + 2*avg(BP/TR,medium) + avg(BP/TR,slow)) / 7

    Example:
        UltimateOscillator(fast=7, medium=14, slow=28) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use when single-period oscillators (RSI, Stochastic) produce too many whipsaws. The three-period blending reduces false divergence signals common with single-timeframe indicators. Requires high/low/close data. Typically followed by CrossSectionalZScore or used as a confirmation filter alongside other momentum indicators. Prefer over RSI when you want built-in multi-timeframe smoothing.

parameters.name: fast
parameters.description: Shortest lookback period. Captures fast momentum shifts. Default 7. Weighted 4x in the final blend (most influence).

parameters.name: medium
parameters.description: Middle lookback period. Default 14. Weighted 2x. Should be ~2x the fast period for proper timeframe separation.

parameters.name: slow
parameters.description: Longest lookback period. Default 28. Weighted 1x. Should be ~2x the medium period. Larger values (42-56) further reduce noise but increase lag.

components.name: VWAP
components.description:
Compute Volume Weighted Average Price as a volume-anchored fair value reference level.

    Use as a dynamic support/resistance level that accounts for where most volume traded.
    Price above VWAP suggests bullish bias (buyers in control), below suggests bearish.
    The deviation from VWAP can feed into mean reversion signals. Use cumulative mode
    (window=None) for session-based VWAP or rolling mode for a moving reference. Output
    is in price units, so normalize before cross-asset comparison. Requires volume data.

    Output:
        Price-level values in same units as input prices. NaN where cumulative volume
        is zero. Not comparable across assets without normalization.

    Formula:
        typical_price = (high + low + close) / 3
        VWAP = sum(typical_price * volume, window) / sum(volume, window)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> VWAP(window=96) -> RollingZScoreTransform(window=60)
components.usage_hint: Use as a dynamic support/resistance level that accounts for where most volume traded. Price above VWAP suggests bullish bias (buyers in control), below suggests bearish. The deviation from VWAP can feed into mean reversion signals. Use cumulative mode (window=None) for session-based VWAP or rolling mode for a moving reference. components.name: VolAttenuator
components.description:
Attenuate signal by vol regime — boost in calm regimes, dampen in stormy ones.

    Carver's vol-regime attenuation (qoppac.blogspot.com/2021/12). Computes
    each asset's current return-vol as a rolling percentile rank within its
    own history, then maps the percentile to a multiplier in [lo, hi]:

        Q[t] = rank(vol[t] in vol[t-W..t])  ∈ [0, 1]
        L[t] = clip(2 - 1.5·Q[t], lo, hi)
        L_smooth = EWMA(L, span=smooth_span)
        attenuated = signal × L_smooth

    When current vol is at the BOTTOM of its history (Q≈0): L=2 (boost 2×).
    When current vol is at the TOP of its history (Q≈1): L=0.5 (halve).

    Use after a price-derived signal (trend / momentum) to lean against
    the typical pattern of vol regime → forward Sharpe (high-vol regimes
    tend to have lower Sharpe per unit of position, low-vol the opposite).
    Carver shows this lifts both Sharpe and tail behavior on systematic
    futures strategies; confirmed in-sample on crypto perps.

    Output:
        Same shape as input signal, multiplied by L_smooth ∈ [lo, hi].
        NaN preserved. Warmup of `min_periods` bars before output begins.

    Notes:
        - The vol input comes from a STORED slot (typically `return_vol`
          written by `ReturnVolatility(window="36d") → Store("return_vol")`).
          The pipeline must Store this slot before any VolAttenuator step.
        - Uses RAW asset return vol, not residual vol. For residual-momentum
          signals, this is consistent with the broader signal stack;
          residual-vol attenuation was tested and found WORSE in audit.
        - Default history_window=90 is much shorter than Carver's classic
          1000-bar futures default — crypto vol regimes shift in weeks,
          not years.

    Example:
        EWMAC(2, 8) -> VolAttenuator(return_vol_slot="return_vol", history_window=90) -> CrossSectionalZScore()

components.name: VolTargetWeightConverter
components.description:
Convert forecasts to vol-targeted weights in one step using return volatility.

    Replaces the old 5-component chain (VolatilityTargetConverter -> EWMAInstrumentValueVolatility
    -> VolatilityScaler -> SubsystemPosition -> WeightConverter) with a single component.
    The reference_capital and price terms that appeared in the old chain cancel out
    mathematically, leaving this clean formula.

    The forecast flows as the primary pipeline input (`current`). Return volatility
    is side-loaded from a named slot.

    Output:
        WeightSeries (DataFrame, timestamps x symbols). Dimensionless weights.
        Positive = long, negative = short. Sum of absolute values represents
        gross leverage before IDM/LeverageCap.

    Formula:
        weight = (forecast / forecast_target) * pct_target / (sqrt(bars_per_year) * sigma)

    Example:
        VolTargetWeightConverter(return_vol_slot="return_vol", pct_target=0.25)

The reference_capital and price terms that appeared in the old chain cancel out mathematically, leaving this clean formula.

components.name: VolWeightSizer
components.description:
Inverse-volatility sizing for equal risk contribution per position.

    Sizes each active position inversely proportional to its recent volatility,
    so all positions contribute roughly equal risk to the portfolio. High-vol
    assets (e.g. memecoins) get smaller positions, low-vol assets (e.g. BTC)
    get larger positions.

    Reads per-asset return volatility from a slot (computed by ReturnVolatility
    upstream). Rescales so gross leverage = target_leverage.

    Use max_weight to cap individual position concentration. A single low-vol
    survivor could otherwise get the full target_leverage. max_weight clips
    per-position weight — excess goes to cash, not redistributed.

    Output:
        WeightSeries. Lower-vol assets get higher weights. Total gross leverage
        equals target_leverage when positions are active (before max_weight cap).

    Example:
        Store('ohlcv'),
        ReturnVolatility() → Store('vol'),
        ...
        PositionStateMachine(...) → VolWeightSizer(vol_slot='vol')
        PositionStateMachine(...) → VolWeightSizer(vol_slot='vol', max_weight=0.3)
components.usage_hint: Sizes each active position inversely proportional to its recent volatility, so all positions contribute roughly equal risk to the portfolio. High-vol assets (e.g. memecoins) get smaller positions, low-vol assets (e.g. BTC) get larger positions.

parameters.description: Maximum absolute weight per position. None (default) = no cap. 0.3 = no position exceeds 30%. Excess goes to cash (gross leverage decreases), not redistributed.

components.name: VolatilityAdjustedPriceSeries
components.description:
Convert raw prices to volatility-adjusted price series for cross-asset breakout analysis.

    Use as a preprocessing step before BreakoutDistance to normalize price
    movements into "vol units" so breakout signals are comparable across assets
    with different volatility levels. Essential for crypto markets where betas
    vary dramatically. Pairs exclusively with BreakoutDistance downstream.

    Output:
        Unbounded cumulative vol-adjusted returns, starting near zero. Units are
        "vol-normalized return units" — one unit equals one annualized standard
        deviation of returns. NaN during warmup (vol_span bars).

    Formula:
        log_ret = ln(P_t / P_{t-1})
        sigma = EWstd(log_ret, span=vol_span) * sqrt(annual_factor)
        adj_ret = log_ret / sigma_{t-1}      # lagged vol avoids lookahead
        adj_price = cumsum(adj_ret)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> VolatilityAdjustedPriceSeries(vol_span=32) -> BreakoutDistance(lookback=20)
components.usage_hint: Use as a preprocessing step before BreakoutDistance to normalize price movements into "vol units" so breakout signals are comparable across assets with different volatility levels. Essential for crypto markets where betas vary dramatically. Pairs exclusively with BreakoutDistance downstream.

parameters.description: EWMA span for volatility estimation in bars. Shorter (16-24) adapts faster to vol regime changes, longer (32-64) gives smoother normalization. Default 32 balances responsiveness and stability.

parameters.name: annual_factor
parameters.description: Annualization factor for volatility scaling. Use 365 for daily crypto bars, 365*24 for hourly, 365*96 for 15-min. Must match the bar frequency of input data.

components.name: VolatilityFilter
components.description:
Filter trading based on volatility z-score to target high or low volatility regimes.

    Use to restrict trading to specific volatility environments (e.g., mean reversion
    in low-vol, trend-following in high-vol). Computes rolling volatility z-score from
    price returns, then masks periods based on the mode and threshold. Accepts either
    OHLCV dict or price DataFrame as input. Timeframe-agnostic z-score thresholds.

    Output:
        DataFrame of 0.0/1.0 float mask. 1.0 where volatility z-score meets the
        condition, 0.0 otherwise. NaN during warmup (~2x window bars). Shifted
        by 1 bar to prevent look-ahead bias.

    Formula:
        vol = rolling_std(returns, window)
        z = (vol - rolling_mean(vol, window)) / rolling_std(vol, window)
        mode="high": mask = (z >= threshold)
        mode="low":  mask = (z <= threshold)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> VolatilityFilter(mode="low", threshold=-1.0, window=20)

Computes rolling volatility z-score from price returns, then masks periods based on the mode and threshold. Accepts either OHLCV dict or price DataFrame as input. Timeframe-agnostic z-score thresholds.

components.name: VolatilitySqueeze
components.description:
Compute short-term to long-term ATR ratio to detect volatility compression (squeeze) setups.

    Use to identify periods when volatility is contracting relative to its recent norm — a
    classic precursor to directional breakouts. Values below 1.0 indicate compression (short-term
    vol < long-term vol), values above 1.0 indicate expansion. Particularly useful as a filter
    dimension (Z axis) in momentum screens to find breakout candidates. Feed into QuantileRankTransform
    for screening or RollingPercentile for historical context.

    Output:
        Range (0, inf), centered around 1.0. Values < 1.0 = squeeze (compression),
        > 1.0 = expansion. Typical range [0.5, 1.5] for liquid crypto on 4h bars.
        NaN during warmup (long_period bars).

    Formula:
        Squeeze = ATR(short_period) / ATR(long_period)

    Example:
        PriceDataLoader() -> VolatilitySqueeze(short_period=7, long_period=21) -> QuantileRankTransform()

Values below 1.0 indicate compression (short-term vol < long-term vol), values above 1.0 indicate expansion. Particularly useful as a filter dimension (Z axis) in momentum screens to find breakout candidates. Feed into QuantileRankTransform for screening or RollingPercentile for historical context.

parameters.description: ATR lookback for baseline volatility. Longer (14-28) represents the normal vol regime. Default 21 is ~3x the short period. The ratio short/long < 1.0 signals compression.

components.name: VolatilityStandardizer
components.description:
Normalize signals by dividing by EWMA price volatility to produce unitless, cross-asset-comparable values.

    Use before ForecastScaler when the raw signal has units that vary across assets
    (e.g., price-point crossover distances, percentage returns). Reads OHLCV data
    from a named slot to compute volatility from price returns (not signal values).
    Choose signal_type="percentage" for ROC/momentum signals, "price_points" for
    EWMA crossover or breakout distance signals.

    Output:
        Unbounded, unitless. Magnitude represents how many vol-units the signal
        moved. NaN during burn-in period (~2x window bars) and where volatility
        is near-zero. Infinities replaced with NaN.

    Formula:
        sigma_pct = EWMA_std(price_returns, window)
        "percentage":    standardized = signal / sigma_pct
        "price_points":  standardized = signal / (sigma_pct * price)

    Example:
        EWMACrossover() -> VolatilityStandardizer(signal_type="price_points") -> ForecastScaler()

components.name: VolumeWeightedMultiplier
components.description:
Scale per-asset signal by a per-bar volume-rank multiplier.

    Multiplies each asset's signal by a factor in [lo, hi] derived from its
    rolling average dollar-volume rank within the cross-section. Higher
    average volume = larger multiplier — boosts positions in liquid names
    where the signal is more reliable, dampens positions in thin names
    where execution cost and signal noise dominate.

    Use after a signal transform and before forecast scaling, when you
    want position sizing to reflect liquidity differences across assets.
    Different `method` choices trade smoothness vs concentration: linear
    methods give a continuous gradient; quantile methods bucket assets
    into tiers.

    Output:
        Same shape as input signal. Values multiplied by [lo, hi] factor
        (NaN preserved). The `quantile_3tier` method uses 1.0 for the
        middle 50% of assets, preserving signal in the bulk of the
        cross-section while emphasizing/dampening extremes.

    Methods:
        - "mean_normalized" — multiplier = clip(vol_i / mean_vol, lo, hi).
          Continuous, sensitive to outlier high-volume assets.
        - "log_rank" — multiplier = lo + (hi - lo) * percentile_rank.
          Continuous, robust to outliers.
        - "quantile_binary" — top half (rank > 0.5) → hi, bottom half → lo.
          Aggressive concentration, no neutral middle.
        - "quantile_3tier" — top quartile → hi, bottom quartile → lo,
          middle 50% → 1.0. Preserves diversification in the body.
        - "top_n_binary" — top N (where N is overloaded into `hi`) → 1.5,
          rest → 1.0. **Note:** the parameter overload is awkward; this
          mode predates the cleaner methods. Prefer quantile_3tier.

    Example:
        EWMAC(2, 8) -> VolumeWeightedMultiplier(method="quantile_3tier", lo=0.6, hi=1.4) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Multiplies each asset's signal by a factor in [lo, hi] derived from its rolling average dollar-volume rank within the cross-section. Higher average volume = larger multiplier — boosts positions in liquid names where the signal is more reliable, dampens positions in thin names where execution cost and signal noise dominate.

parameters.description: Slot name to read OHLCV data from. Must be a Stored slot of OHLCVDict. Default "ohlcv_1d" matches the standard target-timeframe slot in Keel pipelines.

parameters.name: lookback
parameters.description: Rolling window in bars for average dollar volume. Default 60 (~3 months on daily bars). Shorter is more adaptive but noisier.

parameters.name: lo
parameters.description: Lower bound of the multiplier. Default 0.5 (halve position in low-volume names).

parameters.name: hi
parameters.description: Upper bound of the multiplier. Default 2.0 (double position in high-volume names).

parameters.name: method
parameters.description: Multiplier formula — see class docstring for trade-offs.

components.name: WarmupMask
components.description:
Mask the initial warmup period of a signal by setting early values to NaN, with optional execution lag.

    Use at the end of a signal pipeline to remove unreliable values produced during
    indicator warmup (e.g., first N bars where rolling windows are incomplete). Also
    supports adding execution lag to prevent look-ahead bias. Commonly the last
    transform before a signal enters a composer or position sizer.

    Output:
        Same range as input. First warmup_period rows set to NaN. If lag > 0,
        signal is shifted forward by lag bars (additional NaN at start). If
        drop_na=True, NaN rows are removed from output.

    Example:
        EWMA(period=50) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> WarmupMask(warmup_period=60, lag=1)
components.usage_hint: Use at the end of a signal pipeline to remove unreliable values produced during indicator warmup (e.g., first N bars where rolling windows are incomplete). Also supports adding execution lag to prevent look-ahead bias. Commonly the last transform before a signal enters a composer or position sizer.

parameters.name: warmup_period
parameters.description: Number of initial bars to set to NaN. Should match the longest lookback window in the upstream pipeline. Typical range 10-200 depending on indicator complexity.

parameters.name: lag
parameters.description: Number of bars to shift the signal forward for execution delay. 0 = no lag (default), 1 = one-bar delay (standard for preventing look-ahead bias).

parameters.name: drop_na
parameters.description: Whether to remove NaN rows from the output. True (default) shortens the output. False preserves original index length.

components.name: WeightConcatenator
components.description:
Merge weight branches with different asset columns into one portfolio.

    Unlike ForecastCombiner (which averages matching columns), WeightConcatenator
    concatenates columns from branches that may have completely different assets.
    Overlapping assets have their weights summed. Missing assets filled with 0.

    Use after Parallel branches with GroupAssetFilter to merge multi-group strategies.

    Output:
        WeightSeries DataFrame with all assets from all branches combined.

    Example:
        Parallel(defi=[..., ForecastWeightNormalizer()], l1=[..., ForecastWeightNormalizer()])
        -> WeightConcatenator()
components.usage_hint: Unlike ForecastCombiner (which averages matching columns), WeightConcatenator concatenates columns from branches that may have completely different assets. Overlapping assets have their weights summed. Missing assets filled with 0.

components.name: WilliamsR
components.description:
Compute Williams %R, a momentum oscillator that measures closing price relative to the high-low range.

    Use as a mean reversion or overbought/oversold signal similar to RSI but on a [-100, 0]
    scale. Requires high/low/close data (unlike RSI which uses close only). Prefer Williams %R
    over RSI when you want to incorporate high-low range information. Typically followed by
    CrossSectionalZScore or a normalization transform before forecast mapping.

    Output:
        Range [-100, 0]. Values > -20 = overbought (potential short),
        < -80 = oversold (potential long). NaN during warmup (`period` bars).

    Formula:
        %R = (highest_high(period) - close) / (highest_high(period) - lowest_low(period)) * -100

    Example:
        WilliamsR(period=14) -> CrossSectionalZScore(window=60) -> ForecastScaler()

Prefer Williams %R over RSI when you want to incorporate high-low range information. Typically followed by CrossSectionalZScore or a normalization transform before forecast mapping.

parameters.name: period
parameters.description: Lookback window for highest-high and lowest-low calculation. Shorter (5-10) reacts faster to price swings, longer (14-28) provides smoother oscillator output. Default 14 is standard.

components.name: WingsTransform
components.description:
Apply a dead-zone threshold to isolate extreme signal moves for mean reversion.

    Use after CrossSectionalZScore or RollingZScoreTransform to filter out normal
    fluctuations and only trade the "wings" of the distribution. Typically followed
    by ForecastScaler and ForecastCapper. Prefer over ThresholdCross when you want
    a continuous (not binary) signal that grows with extremity.

    Output:
        Unbounded, zero inside the dead zone. When negate=True (default):
        positive = long the underperformer, negative = short the outperformer.
        Zero when |input| < threshold (normal moves ignored).

    Formula:
        u = sign(z) * max(|z| - threshold, 0)^power
        output = -u  (when negate=True)

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> CrossSectionalZScore() -> WingsTransform(threshold=1.0) -> ForecastScaler()
components.usage_hint: Use after CrossSectionalZScore or RollingZScoreTransform to filter out normal fluctuations and only trade the "wings" of the distribution. Typically followed by ForecastScaler and ForecastCapper. Prefer over ThresholdCross when you want a continuous (not binary) signal that grows with extremity.

parameters.name: threshold
parameters.description: Minimum |z| to trigger a signal. Values below this produce zero output (dead zone). Typical range 0.5-2.0. 0.5 = triggers on smaller moves, 1.0 = standard (1 sigma), 1.5-2.0 = only very extreme moves.

parameters.name: power
parameters.description: Convexity control for signal magnitude beyond threshold. 1.0 = linear, 1.5 = moderate convexity, 2.0 = quadratic (strongly amplifies extremes). Values < 1.0 give diminishing returns for larger moves.

parameters.name: negate
parameters.description: Negate output for mean reversion (default True). Set False if the input signal is already in mean-reversion polarity.

components.name: Winsorize
components.description:
Clip signal outliers to rolling percentile bounds for robust outlier suppression.

    Use before normalization or forecast scaling to prevent extreme values from
    distorting downstream transforms. Unlike hard-coded clipping, Winsorize adapts
    to the signal's own distribution over time. Place after raw indicators and before
    RollingZScoreTransform or CrossSectionalZScore for cleaner normalization.

    Output:
        Same scale as input, but clipped to rolling [lower_pct, upper_pct] percentiles.
        NaN during warmup period. Preserves signal shape within bounds.

    Example:
        ROC(period=10) -> Winsorize(lower_pct=5, upper_pct=95, window=120) -> RollingZScoreTransform()
components.usage_hint: Use before normalization or forecast scaling to prevent extreme values from distorting downstream transforms. Unlike hard-coded clipping, Winsorize adapts to the signal's own distribution over time. Place after raw indicators and before RollingZScoreTransform or CrossSectionalZScore for cleaner normalization.

parameters.name: lower_pct
parameters.description: Lower percentile bound (0-100). Values below this rolling percentile are clipped up. Default 5.0 (5th percentile). Lower values (1-2) preserve more of the left tail.

parameters.name: upper_pct
parameters.description: Upper percentile bound (0-100). Values above this rolling percentile are clipped down. Default 95.0 (95th percentile). Must be >= lower_pct.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Lookback in bars for percentile calculation. Longer (120-252) gives more stable bounds, shorter (30-60) adapts faster to regime changes. Default 252.

parameters.name: min_periods
parameters.description: Minimum observations before producing output. Defaults to window // 2. Lower values produce earlier (noisier) bounds.
